finding.title: Dead, divergent applyPadConfigToVoice() helper in processor.cpp duplicates the live plumbing path and omits ~15 params
finding.problem: applyPadConfigToVoice() (anonymous namespace) is never called from anywhere (confirmed by grep: only its definition and two comments reference it). Its banner comment (lines 51-52) falsely claims 'This is called after loading state to push values into the DSP voice.' The live path that actually configures voices at noteOn is VoicePool::applyPadConfigToSlot() in voice_pool.cpp. The two functions have drifted: the dead helper forwards only the Phase 1-2 fields (core, tone-shaper, unnatural, morph) and OMITS noiseLayer*, clickLayer*, noiseBurstDuration, fmRatio, feedbackAmount, frictionPressure, bodyDampingB1/B3, airLoading, modeScatter, couplingStrength, secondaryEnabled/Size/Material, and tensionModAmt — roughly 15 per-pad parameters.
finding.why_wrong: There should be a single source of truth for denormalizing PadConfig into a DrumVoice. A second, stale copy of that logic that omits 15 params is exactly the class of mistake that produced the historically shipped 'dead knob' bugs (audit findings 3-5: fmRatio/feedback/friction were stored but never forwarded). Per project rule 'Check full context around every fix / unify rather than creating parallel paths', this parallel path must be removed or made the canonical one. As a free function in an anonymous namespace with no [[maybe_unused]], it is also an unused-function warning candidate under Clang/GCC -Wunused-function, violating the zero-warning policy.
finding.sound_impact: No audible effect today because it is never invoked. The risk is regression: if anyone re-wires this 'intended' helper back into the state-load or noteOn path (its comment invites exactly that), 15 parameters silently stop reaching the DSP again, which would re-flatten timbre across presets (every voice plays with boot-time tone-shaper/secondary state) — the same symptom this audit is investigating.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. processor.cpp:53-133 defines `applyPadConfigToVoice(DrumVoice&, const PadConfig&)` in an anonymous namespace. Its banner comment (lines 51-52) literally states "This is called after loading state to push values into the DSP voice." A repo-wide grep for `applyPadConfigToVoice` returns ONLY: the definition (processor.cpp:53), a CHANGELOG note, and two explanatory comments in voice_pool.cpp (761, 833). There is no call site anywhere — confirming the helper is dead and the banner comment is false.

The omission claim is also accurate. The dead helper forwards only: core fields (setExciterType/BodyModel/Material/Size/Decay/StrikePosition/Level, lines 55-61), the tone-shaper block (65-112), unnatural-zone modeStretch/decaySkew/modeInject/nonlinearCoupling (115-122), and material-morph (125-132). It OMITS the per-pad params that the live path does forward.

The live canonical path is `VoicePool::applyPadConfigToSlot` (voice_pool.cpp:709-837). It forwards everything the dead helper has PLUS: noiseLayerMix/Cutoff/Resonance/Decay/Color (727-731), clickLayerMix/ContactMs/Brightness (733-735), noiseBurstDuration (738), fmRatio/feedbackAmount/frictionPressure (741-743), bodyDampingB1/B3 (746-747), airLoading/modeScatter (749-750), couplingStrength/secondaryEnabled/Size/Material (752-755), tensionModAmt (757) — i.e. the ~15 params the finding lists. The voice_pool comment at 761 explicitly documents that applyPadConfigToVoice "was never called from anywhere."

So the described behaviour is present exactly as claimed: a dead, divergent duplicate of the plumbing path that omits ~15 params, with a misleading comment inviting reuse. The function is a plain void free function in an anonymous namespace with no [[maybe_unused]], so it is a legitimate -Wunused-function candidate under Clang/GCC.

Severity correction to LOW (down from the report's framing): under the CORRECTNESS lens this produces NO incorrect audio today because the function is never invoked — the live path is complete and correct. It is a code-quality / single-source-of-truth / regression-risk / unused-warning issue, not an active runtime correctness defect. The "presets sound the same" symptom is NOT caused by this (the live applyPadConfigToSlot correctly forwards all params). Real finding, but its impact is latent/maintainability, hence low.

Verified directly in code. processor.cpp:53-133 defines applyPadConfigToVoice() in an anonymous namespace. Grep across plugins/membrum confirms the name appears ONLY in: its definition (processor.cpp:53), a CHANGELOG note, and two explanatory comments in voice_pool.cpp (lines 761, 833). It is never called from anywhere — confirmed dead. Its banner comment (lines 51-52) does falsely claim "This is called after loading state to push values into the DSP voice."

The live path is VoicePool::applyPadConfigToSlot() in plugins/membrum/src/voice_pool/voice_pool.cpp. I read lines 740-837: it forwards exactly the params the dead helper omits — setFMRatio, setFeedbackAmount, setFrictionPressure (741-743), setBodyDampingB1/B3 (746-747), setAirLoading/setModeScatter (749-750), setCouplingStrength/setSecondaryEnabled/Size/Material (752-755), setTensionModAmt (757) — plus the full tone-shaper/pitch-env/filter-env/unnatural-zone/material-morph blocks (769-836). The dead helper in processor.cpp stops after the morph block and never touches any of the ~15 Phase 8 fields. So the divergence and ~15-param omission claim is accurate.

The structural/hygiene claims hold: dead code, misleading comment inviting re-wiring, latent regression risk, and (anonymous-namespace free function with no [[maybe_unused]]) a plausible -Wunused-function candidate.

AUDIBILITY lens: zero audible impact today. The function is never invoked, so it cannot flatten dynamics or homogenize timbre. The live applyPadConfigToSlot() already forwards everything correctly; the comment at voice_pool.cpp:760-768 documents that the historical "everything sounds identical / 808 toms identical" bug was ALREADY fixed by adding that live block. Thus this finding does NOT contribute to the current "presets sound the same/boring" symptom — that lies elsewhere in the path. This is a genuine maintainability/regression-risk issue, not an audio bug, so under the audibility lens it is low severity.

finding.title: PadConfig DSP default for noiseBurstDuration (8.5 ms) disagrees with the documented 5 ms default
finding.problem: PadConfig::noiseBurstDuration defaults to 0.5f. The NoiseBurstExciter denormalizes it as 2 + clamped*13 = [2,15] ms (noise_burst_exciter.h:80-83), so 0.5 -> 8.5 ms. The global proxy kExciterNoiseBurstDurationId is documented in plugin_ids.h:59 as '(2..15, default 5)'. A 5 ms default corresponds to norm (5-2)/13 = 0.231, not 0.5. RegisteredDefaultsTable also seeds kPadNoiseBurstDuration = 0.5 (processor.cpp:184), so the MacroMapper neutral point and the DSP default agree with each other but both disagree with the stated 5 ms.
finding.why_wrong: The advertised default for a parameter should match the value the DSP actually receives when nothing has been edited. Here the contact-time default is ~70% longer (8.5 ms vs 5 ms) than documented, softening the attack transient of the NoiseBurst exciter relative to spec.
finding.sound_impact: Minor: a slightly longer/softer default noise-burst attack than intended for pads using the NoiseBurst exciter. Does not by itself explain preset sameness, but it is a default-equivalence drift worth correcting.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified all cited code. pad_config.h:215 sets `float noiseBurstDuration = 0.5f`. NoiseBurstExciter::setContactMs (noise_burst_exciter.h:82-83) computes `contactMsCached_ = 2.0f + clamped * 13.0f`, i.e. range [2,15] ms, so the 0.5 default denormalizes to 2 + 0.5*13 = 8.5 ms. plugin_ids.h:59 documents kExciterNoiseBurstDurationId as "(2..15, default 5)". A 5 ms default would require norm (5-2)/13 = 0.2308, not 0.5. The discrepancy is confirmed: documented 5 ms vs actual 8.5 ms (~70% longer). processor.cpp:184 seeds the RegisteredDefaultsTable byOffset[kPadNoiseBurstDuration] = 0.5f, and controller.cpp:188 registers the controller-side default as 0.5 with formatting over [2,15] ms (controller.cpp:2441), so the DSP default, registered default, and macro neutral point all agree at 0.5 (8.5 ms) but disagree with the documented 5 ms. The bug is therefore real: a genuine default-equivalence/documentation drift. Impact is minor and correctly characterized as Low: it only affects pads using the NoiseBurst exciter at the untouched default, slightly softens the attack transient, does not clamp/collapse the parameter range, and does not contribute to the preset-sameness symptom (presets that set noiseBurstDuration explicitly, e.g. default_kit.h:94/152/176, get their own values). Confidence high because every cited line was read directly and the arithmetic is unambiguous.

Confirmed by reading the actual code.

(1) pad_config.h:215 — `float noiseBurstDuration = 0.5f;` confirmed.
(2) noise_burst_exciter.h:80-83 (file is at src/dsp/exciters/, the finding's cited path was slightly off but the code matches): `setContactMs` does `contactMsCached_ = 2.0f + clamped * 13.0f;` so 0.5 -> 8.5 ms. Confirmed.
(3) plugin_ids.h:59 — `kExciterNoiseBurstDurationId = 204, // Noise Burst duration ms (2..15, default 5)`. A 5 ms default is norm (5-2)/13 = 0.230769, not 0.5. Confirmed documentation mismatch.
(4) processor.cpp:184 — `t.byOffset[kPadNoiseBurstDuration] = 0.5f;` (RegisteredDefaultsTable / MacroMapper neutral) agrees with the DSP default 0.5, both disagreeing with 5 ms. Confirmed.
(5) controller.cpp:188 per-pad default = 0.5 (8.5 ms); but the GLOBAL PROXY default at controller.cpp:317 was already corrected to 0.230769 (5 ms) per "audit finding 10". So there is now an internal inconsistency: the proxy displays 5 ms while the per-pad DSP value used at note-on is 8.5 ms (voice_pool.cpp:738 -> exciter_bank.h:129 -> setContactMs). The finding's core claim — the DSP-received default is 8.5 ms, ~70% longer than the documented/displayed 5 ms — is accurate and verified end-to-end.

AUDIBILITY: Low. The bare PadConfig default only governs the NoiseBurst exciter type AND only on pads not configured by a kit/preset. The factory kit (default_kit.h:94/152/176) and every saved preset explicitly write noiseBurstDuration via state load, overriding 0.5. So this default never reaches presets and cannot contribute to the preset-sameness symptom. Its only effect is a ~3.5 ms longer/softer raised-cosine attack on a freshly-instantiated NoiseBurst pad — a subtle transient difference, audible only on isolated A/B, not a dynamics-flattener or timbre-homogenizer. Real default-equivalence drift worth fixing for consistency (and it now contradicts the corrected proxy), but minor. Matches the finding's own Minor/low rating.

finding.title: FM modulation index is clamped to [0,1] by FMOperator::setLevel, killing the velocity->brightness mapping (intended 0.5..3.0 radians)
finding.problem: FMImpulseExciter computes modulationIndex_ = 0.5 + 2.5*velocity (range [0.5, 3.0] radians, per the docstring at lines 88-90) and applies it as the modulator's modulation depth via modulator_.setLevel(modulationIndex_ * modIndexEnv_) at line 123. But FMOperator::setLevel() hard-clamps its argument to [0,1] (dsp/include/krate/dsp/processors/fm_operator.h:222), and the modulator output that is fed to the carrier as the PM input (radians) is rawOutput*level_, so |modOut| can never exceed ~1.0 radian. The carrier therefore never receives more than ~1 radian of phase modulation regardless of velocity or the intended index.
finding.why_wrong: In Chowning FM the modulation index is the dominant control of sideband count / spectral brightness; the design (FR-014, docstring lines 88-90) calls for up to 3.0 radians at full velocity. Solving 0.5 + 2.5*v = 1.0 gives v = 0.2, so for every strike with velocity >= 0.2 (essentially all real strikes) the attack-time modulation index pins to exactly 1.0. The upper ~75% of the FM brightness range, and all velocity sensitivity of FM brightness above v=0.2, is dead. Correct behaviour is to scale the carrier's PM input up to 3.0 radians (e.g. multiply a unit-level modulator output by modulationIndex_) instead of routing the index through the [0,1]-clamped setLevel.
finding.sound_impact: FM voices lose velocity-dependent brightness and their bright/metallic high-index character entirely; all medium-to-hard hits produce the same dull low-index timbre. Presets differing only in FM index/velocity response sound identical, directly contributing to the 'presets mostly sound the same and boring' symptom for the FMImpulse exciter.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against the actual code. fm_impulse_exciter.h:90 computes modulationIndex_ = 0.5f + (3.0f - 0.5f) * velocity, i.e. range [0.5, 3.0] radians, matching the docstring at lines 88-90 which explicitly says "velocity 0 -> 0.5 rad, velocity 1 -> 3.0 rad". Line 123 applies it via modulator_.setLevel(modulationIndex_ * modIndexEnv_). FMOperator::setLevel at fm_operator.h:217-222 does level_ = std::clamp(level, 0.0f, 1.0f), hard-clamping to [0,1] (the comment at line 214 even documents "clamped to [0, 1]"). The modulator's process() output is rawOutput * level_ (fm_operator.h:269, "output = rawOutput * level"), and that output is fed to the carrier as phaseModInput in radians (exciter line 124-128; fm_operator.h:255-257 confirms "A modulator output of +/-1.0 represents +/-1.0 radians of PM"). Therefore |modOut| <= level_ <= 1.0, so the carrier never receives more than ~1 radian of phase modulation. Since modIndexEnv_ starts at 1.0 (line 97), solving 0.5 + 2.5*v = 1.0 gives v = 0.2: every strike with velocity >= 0.2 pins the attack-time index to exactly 1.0. The upper portion of the intended [1.0, 3.0] radian range and all velocity sensitivity of FM brightness above v=0.2 are dead. In Chowning FM the modulation index is the primary control of sideband count/brightness, so this genuinely flattens the FM exciter's velocity->brightness mapping. The described code behaviour is present and is wrong. Severity high rather than critical because it is scoped to the FMImpulse exciter (one of several exciter types) and is a contributing factor to the global 'presets sound the same' symptom rather than the sole cause.

Confirmed by reading the actual code at both cited locations.

fm_impulse_exciter.h:
- Line 90: modulationIndex_ = 0.5f + (3.0f - 0.5f) * velocity  → range [0.5, 3.0] rad, matching the docstring (lines 88-90) and FR-014.
- Line 123: modulator_.setLevel(modulationIndex_ * modIndexEnv_)  → routes the index (up to 3.0) through setLevel.
- Line 124/128: modOut = modulator_.process(0.0f); carrier_.process(modOut)  → the modulator output is the carrier's phase-mod input in radians.

fm_operator.h:
- Line 222: level_ = std::clamp(level, 0.0f, 1.0f)  → setLevel hard-clamps to [0,1].
- Line 312: output = rawOutput * level_  → modulator output = sine(±1) * level(≤1.0), so |modOut| ≤ ~1.0 radian.
- Lines 299/303: totalPM = phaseModInput + feedbackPM; osc_.setPhaseModulation(totalPM)  → carrier receives this capped PM in radians.

So the bug is real: the carrier never receives more than ~1 radian of phase modulation, not the intended 3.0. Velocity threshold math holds: 0.5 + 2.5v = 1.0 at v=0.2, and since modIndexEnv_ starts at 1.0, every strike with v≥0.2 pins the attack-time index to exactly 1.0 rad. The upper ~67% of the index range and all FM-index velocity sensitivity above v=0.2 are dead.

AUDIBILITY: Modulation index is the dominant control of FM sideband bandwidth (per Bessel functions, significant sidebands extend to ~index+1 orders). Index 1.0 (~2 meaningful sidebands per side) vs 3.0 (~4) is a clearly audible brightness/metallic-richness difference, especially in the attack transient where it is pinned. This caps how bright FM voices can get and makes presets that differ only in intended FM index sound the same — directly supporting the 'presets sound the same and boring' symptom for FM voices.

Why not critical: FM voices are NOT fully velocity-flat. trigger() also sweeps the carrier base frequency 400→2500 Hz via 400*6.25^velocity and scales carrier amplitude by velocity, so velocity still shifts the whole spectrum and loudness. The SC-004 test (test_fm_impulse_exciter.cpp:39, cHi/cLo>=2.0) passes precisely because of this frequency sweep, masking the dead index control. Also modIndexEnv_ (30ms tau) decays below 1.0 quickly, so only the attack peak is clamped. The bug removes a major timbral dimension (high-index brightness + index-based preset differentiation) but not all FM velocity response — hence high, not critical.

finding.title: SC-004 FM centroid-ratio test passes despite the dead modulation-index mapping (false confidence)
finding.problem: The FMImpulse SC-004 test compares spectral centroid at velocity 0.23 vs 1.0 and requires cHi/cLo >= 2.0. Because both velocities drive modulationIndex_ to >= 1.075, which is clamped to 1.0 (see the FM clamp finding), the modulation index is identical at both endpoints and contributes zero to the measured centroid difference. The test passes solely on the carrier base-frequency sweep baseFreq = 400 * 6.25^velocity (fm_impulse_exciter.h:83), which moves the carrier from 400 Hz to 2500 Hz.
finding.why_wrong: The test was intended to validate that velocity drives FM spectral content (per FR-014, which includes the modulation index). It instead silently validates only the carrier pitch sweep, so the index clamp bug is invisible to the suite. A correct test would hold base frequency constant and vary only the velocity-driven index, or measure sideband-to-carrier energy ratio vs velocity, to catch the clamp.
finding.sound_impact: No direct audible impact, but it explains why the clamp bug shipped undetected: existing coverage cannot detect that FM brightness no longer responds to velocity, so the homogenized FM timbre passes CI unnoticed.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading test_fm_impulse_exciter.cpp:39-58, fm_impulse_exciter.h, and fm_operator.h.

CONFIRMED facts:
- SC-004 test (lines 48-57) renders 10 ms at velocity 0.23 vs 1.0 and asserts spectralCentroid(hi)/spectralCentroid(lo) >= 2.0.
- trigger() sets baseFreq = 400 * 6.25^velocity (line 83): v=0.23 -> 609.7 Hz, v=1.0 -> 2500 Hz. Carrier ratio alone = 4.10x, which by itself exceeds the 2.0 centroid threshold regardless of any FM sideband content.
- modulationIndex_ = 0.5 + 2.5*velocity (line 90): 1.075 at v=0.23, 3.0 at v=1.0. This index is applied via modulator_.setLevel(modulationIndex_ * modIndexEnv_) (line 123), and FMOperator::setLevel clamps to [0,1] (fm_operator.h:217-222). So at the envelope peak both velocities are clamped to a modulator level of 1.0 -> the intended index range (up to 3.0 rad) is unreachable.

The finding's HEADLINE claim is correct: the SC-004 test gives false confidence because the 4.1x carrier-frequency sweep alone drives the centroid ratio past 2.0, so the test cannot detect that the velocity->modulation-index mapping is flattened by the [0,1] clamp. A correct test (hold base freq constant, vary only index, or measure sideband/carrier ratio) would expose the clamp.

MINOR INACCURACY (does not invalidate the finding): the finding states the modulation index is 'identical at both endpoints and contributes zero.' That is overstated. Because the modulator level = modulationIndex_ * modIndexEnv_, the clamp only pins the PEAK to 1.0; during decay the v=1.0 case stays clamped at 1.0 for ~15 ms while v=0.23 falls below 1.0 after ~2 ms, so the index trajectories diverge and contribute a nonzero (but small, and dominated by the carrier sweep) centroid difference. The substantive correctness claim - test is blind to the index clamp, dominant driver is the carrier sweep - holds.

This is a test-coverage / false-confidence issue with no direct audible impact (as the finding itself states), so Low is the correct severity rather than the implied higher concern.

VERIFIED the mechanism by reading the actual code. fm_impulse_exciter.h:83 sets baseFreq = 400 * 6.25^velocity (400 Hz at v=0.23-ish... actually pow(6.25, velocity): v=0.23 -> ~400*1.55=620 Hz, v=1.0 -> 2500 Hz). Line 90 sets modulationIndex_ = 0.5 + 2.5*velocity (1.075 at v=0.23, 3.0 at v=1.0). Line 123 calls modulator_.setLevel(modulationIndex_ * modIndexEnv_), and FMOperator::setLevel (fm_operator.h:217-222) does level_ = std::clamp(level, 0.0f, 1.0f). So the index passed to the modulator IS clamped to [0,1]. The SC-004 test (test_fm_impulse_exciter.cpp:39-58) compares spectral centroid at v=0.23 vs v=1.0 with the velocity-coupled carrier sweep active, so the dominant centroid driver is the ~4x carrier frequency shift, which by itself exceeds the >=2.0 ratio requirement. I scanned all 7 test cases in the file: none holds baseFreq constant while varying only velocity, and the sideband test (132-170) and envelope-decay test (172-198) each use a single velocity. So the suite genuinely cannot detect that velocity-driven FM brightness (the index) is non-responsive due to the clamp. The finding's CORE claim (false-confidence / coverage gap) is REAL and correctly described.

One inaccuracy in the finding: it overstates that the index is 'identical at both endpoints' and 'contributes zero.' The clamp pins the index to 1.0 only at the envelope peak; modIndexEnv_ (tau=30ms, coeff~0.99479) decays to ~0.10 over the 441-sample/10ms window, so the product modulationIndex_*modIndexEnv_ drops below 1.0 mid-window and the two velocities diverge (v=1.0 holds 1.0 for ~4.8ms and ends at 3.0*0.10=0.30; v=0.23 holds 1.0 for ~0.3ms and ends at 1.075*0.10=0.108). So there IS some residual velocity-dependent FM contribution within the window — it is just small and swamped by the carrier sweep. This does not change the conclusion that the test cannot isolate/detect the index clamp.

AUDIBILITY LENS: This is a test-quality finding, not a runtime DSP defect. The finding itself states 'No direct audible impact.' Nothing in the audio path is flattened or homogenized by this finding; it only describes why a SEPARATE clamp bug (the FM clamp finding) escapes CI. A test-coverage gap with zero audible effect maps to not-a-bug under the audibility lens. It is a legitimate observation worth noting for the suite, but on its own it does not contribute to the 'presets sound the same' symptom and is not an audio bug.

finding.title: Material Morph and per-sample Material/pitch refresh are silently dropped for every body except Membrane
finding.problem: updateModalParameters(), updateBodyFundamental(), and refreshBodyForMaterial()/refreshBodyForMaterialOnBank() all begin with `if (bodyBank_.getCurrentType() != BodyModelType::Membrane) return;`. The Material Morph engine (lines 405-409, 631-634, 829-831) and the per-sample pitch-envelope sweep (line 400) therefore only re-configure the modal bank when the active body is Membrane. For Plate, Shell, Bell, and NoiseBody, moving the Material Morph XY pad over the duration of a hit produces NO change to the mode set at all, and the pitch envelope never sweeps the body.
finding.why_wrong: Material Morph is a headline timbral control whose whole purpose is to sweep material over the ~100-200 ms perceptual window of a drum hit. The bodies' own mappers (plate/shell/bell/noise) all consume params.material (brightness, damping, noise cutoff), so the morph CAN affect them — it just is never re-applied after noteOn for non-membrane bodies. The early-return is documented as a Phase 2 scope limitation ('SC-009 tests only Membrane'), not a physical requirement. The result is that on the majority of body types, the morph and the pitch glide are inert.
finding.sound_impact: Directly contributes to 'presets mostly sound the same and boring': any preset built on a Plate/Shell/Bell/Noise body that relies on Material Morph or a pitch sweep will render identically regardless of the morph endpoints or pitch-env settings, collapsing an entire expressive axis to a no-op for 4 of the 6 bodies.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly in plugins/membrum/src/dsp/drum_voice.h.

EARLY-RETURN GUARDS CONFIRMED:
- updateModalParameters() line 1160-1161: `if (getCurrentType() != Membrane) return;`
- updateBodyFundamental() lines 1190-1200: only calls updateMembraneFundamental for Membrane; explicit comment "Other body types: no-op in Phase 7."
- refreshBodyForMaterialOnBank() lines 1245-1246: `if (getCurrentType() != Membrane) return;`

CALLERS CONFIRMED:
- process() (per-sample/slow caller): pitch env -> updateBodyFundamental (line 400), morph -> refreshBodyForMaterial (line 409). Both early-return for non-membrane.
- processBlockFast: pitch refresh gated to Membrane (lines 612, 620), morph refresh gated to Membrane (line 629). The else-if at 636-643 explicitly advances the morph counter for non-Membrane bodies but does NOT re-run the mapper ("do NOT re-run the mapper").
- processBlockSlow: morph refresh gated to Membrane (line 828); pitch gated by pitchEnvForMembrane flag (line 823).

BODIES DO CONSUME material: PlateMapper (plate_mapper.h lines 56, 66) uses params.material for brightness and decay time, so re-application WOULD be audible. The claim that the morph "can affect them" is correct.

TWO REFINEMENTS to the finding (it slightly overstates, but the core mechanism is real and wrong):
1. The morph's START endpoint DOES reach non-Membrane bodies at noteOn: line 143-148 sets params_.material = effectiveMaterial (morph's initial process() value), and configureForNoteOn -> PlateMapper::map applies it once. So non-membrane renders are NOT fully independent of the morph start value; it is the time-varying SWEEP and the END endpoint that are inert. The finding's phrase "render identically regardless of the morph endpoints" is therefore mildly overstated, but its central claim ("moving the XY pad over the duration of a hit produces NO change to the mode set") is exactly correct.
2. Pitch envelope is even more inert than claimed for Plate: PlateMapper::map ignores pitchHz entirely (parameter commented out `/*pitchHz*/`, f0 derived solely from params.size, plate_mapper.h line 33-39). So for Plate the pitch envelope has zero effect, not even an initial offset — strengthening that half of the finding.

Severity: HIGH not critical. It is a genuine correctness/expressiveness defect that silently disables two headline modulation axes (Material Morph sweep, pitch-env glide) for 4-5 of 6 body types and plausibly contributes to the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom. It is not critical because it does not crash, corrupt audio, or violate RT-safety, and the static (start-of-note) material value still differentiates presets on those bodies. The behavior is explicitly documented as a Phase-2 scope limitation, confirming it is unintended-for-shipping rather than a physical requirement.

CODE CONFIRMED. Read drum_voice.h:1158-1269 and the call sites at 397-410, 615-643, 823-832.

- updateModalParameters() (1160), refreshBodyForMaterialOnBank() (1245), and updateBodyFundamental() (1190-1200) all gate on BodyModelType::Membrane and return/no-op otherwise. updateMembraneFundamentalOnBank (1212) is only reached for Membrane.
- The per-sample/processBlockFast/processBlockSlow morph refresh (lines 406-409, 629-643, 828-832) and pitch-env glide (lines 397-401, 823-827) only re-run the mapper when getCurrentType()==Membrane. For Plate/Shell/Bell/NoiseBody the morph counter is advanced (636-642) but the mapper is never re-applied, and the pitch glide is a no-op. The Membrane-only restriction is explicitly documented as a Phase 2/Phase 7 scope limitation (1183-1189, 1197-1199, 1233-1236), not a physical requirement. So the SWEEP of material and the per-hit pitch GLIDE are genuinely dropped for 4 of 6 bodies. The structural claim is accurate.

BUT the AUDIBILITY framing is overstated:
1. Static values ARE applied to every body at noteOn. noteOn() computes effectiveMaterial = morph.process() START value (143-145) into params_.material and calls bodyBank_.configureForNoteOn(params_, initialPitchHz) (198). plate_body.h:32-43 (and shell/bell/noise by symmetry) consume both params.material and pitchHz via their mapper.map(). So different Material values and different pitch-env START values DO change non-Membrane timbre/pitch. Only the intra-hit time-variation is lost, not the whole expressive axis the finding claims ("render identically regardless of morph endpoints / pitch-env settings" is false for the static endpoint).
2. Contribution to the reported 'presets sound the same and boring' symptom is weak-to-none for the factory product: MaterialMorph defaults DISABLED (material_morph.h:130 enabled_=false), and no default_kit.h template enables it on any body. Pitch-env is configured ONLY on the Kick (Membrane, default_kit.h:57-59), where the path works. NoiseBody (Hat/Cymbal) and Plate (Perc) templates set neither morph nor pitch-env, so the dropped code paths are never driven out of the box.

Verdict: real, confirmed functional limitation, but opt-in, intra-hit-only, and it does NOT explain the sameness symptom (static parameter range is fully preserved across bodies). Audible only if a user/preset explicitly enables morph or a pitch sweep on a non-Membrane body, and even then it loses only the motion within the ~100-200ms window, not the differentiation between presets. Hence corrected_severity=low rather than the implied high.

finding.title: decaySkew degrades to an inaudible +/-30% global decay nudge for Plate/Shell/Bell/NoiseBody
finding.problem: For these four bodies decaySkew is applied ONLY as `skewBias = 1.0f - 0.3f * params.decaySkew`, a single scalar multiplier on the global decayTime. With the processor mapping decaySkew to [-1,1] (processor.cpp:118), skewBias spans just [0.7, 1.3]. The membrane mapper additionally applies a per-mode amplitude tilt (membrane_mapper.h:150-161, ratio^(-decaySkew)) that actually re-orders perceived t60, but the other four bodies have no per-mode term.
finding.why_wrong: The decay-skew parameter is meant to invert/redistribute decay across the partial series (the membrane path does this; research.md §9 explicitly notes the scalar-bias form is a weak fallback that 'attenuates global decay time rather than applying a per-mode inversion'). A uniform +/-30% scaling of the overall decay time is near the JND for tail-length on a percussive sound and produces no spectral-tilt change, so the knob barely registers on plates, shells, bells and noise bodies.
finding.sound_impact: A control that users expect to reshape the decay envelope produces almost no audible difference across most bodies, reinforcing the 'parameters move but nothing changes' sameness complaint.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading all four cited sites plus the membrane contrast and processor mapping.

Confirmed code facts:

None of these four mappers contain any per-mode/per-partial term involving decaySkew. The amplitudes loops above them use strikePos only, not decaySkew.
- processor.cpp:118 maps decaySkew via `std::clamp(cfg.decaySkew,0,1)*2-1` => [-1,1]. Therefore skewBias spans exactly [0.7, 1.3], i.e. a uniform +/-30% global decay-time scale, as claimed.
- membrane_mapper.h:140-161 uses the SAME scalar skewBias BUT additionally applies a per-mode amplitude tilt `r.amplitudes[k] *= exp(-decaySkew*log(ratio)) = ratio^(-decaySkew)` (only when decaySkew != 0), which re-orders perceived t60 across the partial series. The four other bodies genuinely lack this per-mode redistribution. The code comments themselves cite research.md §9 and label the scalar form a fallback / the membrane form the "more-accurate" option, corroborating the finding's framing.

Correctness assessment: The described behaviour is exactly present. The parameter's intended purpose (reshape/redistribute decay across partials) is achieved only on the membrane body; on Plate/Shell/Bell/NoiseBody it collapses to a +/-30% uniform decay-time nudge with no spectral-tilt change. That is a real parameter-effectiveness defect that homogenizes timbre and supports the user-reported sameness symptom.

Severity downgraded from any crash/RT-safety tier to medium: this is not a real-time-safety, NaN/Inf, or denormalization bug, and the code is intentional (documented fallback), so it is a perceptual-quality / parameter-range weakness rather than a hard logic error. The "barely audible" claim rests on a JND argument that is reasonable but not measured here; the objective fact (scalar-only, [0.7,1.3], no per-mode term, inconsistent with membrane) is fully confirmed, hence high confidence on the code, medium severity on impact.

finding.title: 1/sqrt(N) output normalize uses total configured mode count, not active modes, decoupling level from the actual sounding spectrum
finding.problem: The non-feedback body output gain is set to `1.0f / sqrt(bank.getNumModes())`, where getNumModes() returns numModes_ = the total partial count passed to setModes (48 for Membrane/Plate, 32 Shell/Noise, 16 Bell) and does NOT exclude modes culled at configure time for exceeding Nyquist or falling below the -80 dB amplitude threshold (modal_resonator_bank.h:434, 654; culling at 678-699). getNumActiveModes() (the real sounding count) is available but unused here.
finding.why_wrong: The normalization is justified in the comments as an energy-equalisation norm for N uncorrelated modes, but when a small-size/high-f0 body Nyquist-culls many of its partials the divisor stays at the full table size, so the surviving modes are scaled down more than their count warrants. The scaling is also amplitude-independent by design, so it does not track the actual peak. The net effect is a body whose perceived loudness drifts with Size in a way unrelated to how many modes are actually ringing.
finding.sound_impact: Minor: contributes to flattened dynamics/loudness inconsistency across Size settings; combined with the downstream softClip on shaped*env*level it can leave high-Size presets sitting at similar perceived loudness regardless of their true modal content.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against actual code. drum_voice.h:247-248 sets the non-feedback body output gain to 1.0f / sqrt(bank.getNumModes()). getNumModes() (modal_resonator_bank.h:434) returns numModes_, which setModes() sets to numPartials = the full configured table size (line 654), NOT reduced by culling. Modes are culled at configure time: amplitude culling (line 678, below kAmplitudeThresholdLinear) and Nyquist culling (line 693, f_w >= kNyquistGuard*sampleRate) both `continue` WITHOUT incrementing numActiveModes_. Only surviving modes increment numActiveModes_ (line 721). getNumActiveModes() (line 394) returns the true sounding count and is available but unused at the normalization site. So when a small/high-f0 body Nyquist-culls partials, the divisor sqrt(numModes_) stays at full size (48/32/16) and over-attenuates the surviving modes by a factor up to sqrt(numModes/numActive). The comment block (lines 235-247) explicitly frames this as a 1/sqrt(N) energy-equalisation norm but uses the wrong N. The claimed downstream interaction also checks out: softClip(shaped*env*level) exists (drum_voice.h:446, 769, 782, 885) and bounds output, so combined with this gain drift it can leave loudness less correlated with actual modal content. The behavior described is genuinely present and is a correctness mismatch (configured vs active count). Severity is low/minor as claimed: it is a continuous, bounded gain offset that varies with Size rather than a large or catastrophic error, and is at most a contributing factor to the sameness symptom, not a primary cause.

VERIFIED in actual code. drum_voice.h:247-248: the non-feedback body output gain is `1.0f / std::sqrt(getNumModes())`. modal_resonator_bank.h:434 getNumModes() returns numModes_, which is set to the full configured partial count (numModes_ = numPartials, line 654) BEFORE culling. Membrane mapper sets 48, bell 16, shell/noise 32. numActiveModes_ (line 567/655/721) counts only modes that survive amplitude culling (-80dB, line 678) and Nyquist culling (0.49*sr, line 693). getNumActiveModes() (line 394) exists and is genuinely unused at the call site, so the divisor uses the stale full count. bodyGainCompensation_ stays hardcoded at 1.0 (drum_voice.h:251), so the over-attenuation is never compensated downstream. The mechanism the finding describes is therefore real.

HOWEVER the finding's specific causal claim ("loudness drifts with Size because small-size/high-f0 Nyquist-culls many partials") only partly holds. I computed top partial frequencies vs the 0.49*44100=21609Hz guard: Membrane f0=500*0.1^size, max ratio 8.16 -> top ~4080Hz at size=0; Bell f0=800*0.1^size, max ratio 12 -> ~9600Hz; Plate-16 f0=800, max ratio 20.5 -> ~16400Hz. NONE of these Nyquist-cull at 44.1kHz at any Size, so for the three main pitched bodies the divisor does NOT drift with Size at all. The mechanism only bites for Shell (f0=1500*0.1^size) and the Noise body (Plate ratios up to 65x): at small Size their top partials exceed Nyquist and ~half of 32 modes get culled, giving 1/sqrt(32) vs 1/sqrt(16) ~= 3dB over-attenuation that does vary with Size. Amplitude culling (strikePos near a Bessel/Chladni node) also reduces the active count on all bodies, but that varies with strikePos, not Size.

AUDIBILITY: bounded at roughly 1-3 dB, uncompensated, and only Size-dependent on Shell/Noise bodies; on the dominant Membrane/Bell/Plate bodies it produces no Size-dependent variation at standard sample rates. It is one of several output-flattening stages and a genuine minor loudness inconsistency, but it is not a dominant driver of the "presets all sound the same/boring" symptom (that points more at the per-voice softClip, the 1/sqrt(N) amplitude-independent norm collapsing dynamics generally, and the master energy limiter). Real bug, low severity.

finding.title: Secondary shell bank (8D coupling) is completely absent from the slow path — FeedbackExciter pads get zero head<->shell coupling
finding.problem: processBlockSlow() (used whenever the exciter is FeedbackExciter, selected at line 490-500) contains no reference to secondaryBank_, effectiveCoupling_, or the shell mix. Only processBlockFast (line 691-734) processes the secondary bank. The path is chosen purely by exciter type (useSlowPath = feedbackExciter), independent of whether the pad enabled coupling.
finding.why_wrong: The two block paths are required to be perceptually equivalent (the prompt and the file's own comment at line 459-468 state this). A pad with secondaryEnabled>=0.5 and couplingStrength>0 that also uses the FeedbackExciter will silently produce no shell coupling at all: the Coupling Strength / Secondary parameters never reach the DSP for that pad. This is a fast-vs-slow divergence and a parameter that fails to affect the DSP for an entire exciter class.
finding.sound_impact: Pads built on the feedback exciter cannot get any shell character from 8D no matter how the coupling/secondary knobs are set, so that timbral dimension is dead for them. Two presets that differ only in coupling sound identical on those pads — a direct contributor to 'presets sound the same'.

A grep over the file confirms secondaryBank_/effectiveCoupling_ appear only in prepare/noteOn/reset (lines 94, 254-307, 363-365) and in processBlockFast (lines 691-734); they never appear anywhere inside processBlockSlow.
