Severity medium (a whole timbral dimension is dead for an exciter class, but only in a non-default, non-shipped configuration), not high.

finding.title: Shell output is structurally too quiet and too short-lived to ever be an audible, distinct timbre
finding.problem: The shell is excited by bodyScratch * effectiveCoupling_ where effectiveCoupling_ = clamp(couplingStrength,0,1)*0.25 (max 0.25, line 1155). Its output is normalized by 1/sqrt(24)~=0.204 (line 254-256) and then mixed purely additively into the body (line 730-731). Simultaneously configureSecondaryBank forces shell b1 = max(25, 2.5*bodyB1) + (1-mat)*15 (line 1141-1142), i.e. shell t60 is deliberately held to <=~40% of the body t60 (line 1137-1138 comment).
finding.why_wrong: Combined, the shell can contribute at most ~coupling(0.25) * amp * (1/sqrt(24)) ~= a few percent of the body's amplitude, only during the body's attack (it is force-damped to die before the body). The body input itself is already 1/sqrt(N_body)-normalized, so the shell drive is small to begin with, and most of the 24 free-free beam modes (ratios up to 466x f0, shell_modes.h line 36-43) are Nyquist-culled, leaving ~2-4 audible modes at amp>=0.03. There is no longer any stability reason to crush the shell decay: the body<->shell feedback path was removed (the mix is now feedforward + additive only, line 707-729), so the entire 'shell must decay faster than the body' constraint is an artifact of dead requirements.
finding.sound_impact: The secondary shell bank is effectively inaudible / always swamped: it adds a tiny transient tint that decays before the body, never the sustained snare/tom shell ring it is supposed to model. The Coupling/Secondary parameters produce no perceptible variation, so presets that rely on them for distinction sound the same and boring.

verdicts.reasoning:
I read the actual code and every factual claim holds.

AMPLITUDE PATH (drum_voice.h):
- effectiveCoupling_ = stabilityClampedCoupling() = clamp(couplingStrength_,0,1)*0.25, hard max 0.25 (line 1153-1156). Set at noteOn only when secondaryEnabled_>=0.5 && couplingStrength_>0 (line 304-307); otherwise 0.
- Shell drive secExc[i] = bodyScratch[i] * effectiveCoupling_ (line 698) — feedforward from the already-1/sqrt(N_body)-normalized body output.
- secondaryBank_ output gain = 1/sqrt(numModes). kSecondaryModes=24 (line 1070), shellCount=min(24, kShellModeCount=32)=24, so getNumModes()=24 and outputGain=1/sqrt(24)≈0.204 (lines 254-256; bank multiplies modeSum*=outputGain_ at modal_resonator_bank.h:752). secondaryGainCompensation_ stays 1.0 (line 252, default 1361) so the *=g branch is skipped.
- Shell mixed PURELY ADDITIVELY: bodyScratch[i] += secOut[i] (line 731). No secOut term is fed back into the body's excitation in either block path. Verified the feedback path is gone everywhere (grep of secOut/secondaryBank_), so the body↔shell loop the stability comments describe no longer exists.
Net: shell contribution caps at ~0.25 * body * 0.204 ≈ ~5% of body amplitude before its own modal response — a few percent, as claimed.

DECAY PATH:
- configureSecondaryBank forces shell b1 = max(25, 2.5*bodyB1) + (1-mat)*15 (lines 1141-1142). bodyB1=0.2+bodyN*49.8. The comment at 1137-1138 explicitly states shell t60 is held to <=~40% of the body's. Since t60∝1/b1, 2.5×-higher b1 = shell decays >=2.5× faster, dying before the body. Confirmed.

MODE CULLING:
- kShellRatios go to 466.13× f0 (shell_modes.h:42); amp floor 0.03 (line 1088). At typical fundamentals most of the 24 modes exceed Nyquist and are culled, leaving only a few audible modes — consistent with the claim.

DEAD-REQUIREMENT POINT:
- The stability rationale for crushing shell decay (lines 1109-1124, 1149-1156) is framed entirely around a body↔shell FEEDBACK loop (coupling²·0.5·Q_body·Q_shell). The current topology is feedforward + additive only (comments at 707-729 confirm the loop was deliberately removed). So the "shell must decay faster than the body" constraint is indeed an artifact of removed requirements, as the finding states.

Correctness conclusion: the described behaviour is present and is wrong in the sense that the Coupling/Secondary parameters cannot produce a perceptible, sustained shell ring — the shell is structurally attenuated to a few percent of the body and force-damped to die before it. This directly supports the user's "presets sound the same/boring" symptom for any preset relying on these params. Additional (separate) reinforcing observation: processBlockSlow (FeedbackExciter path, lines 813+) contains NO shell processing at all, so coupling is entirely silent there. Severity is medium (a parameter-range/dynamics homogenization defect, not an RT-safety/crash bug).

I read drum_voice.h:254-258, 691-734, 1068-1146, 1153-1156, 303-307, the default member values (1320-1331), shell_modes.h, and modal_resonator_bank.h (setModes/culling at 637-731, getNumModes at 434, processSampleCore at 775-824).

STRUCTURAL claims are all accurate:
- effectiveCoupling_ = clamp(couplingStrength_,0,1)*0.25, hard max 0.25 (line 1155). Confirmed.
- Shell output normalized by 1/sqrt(numModes). getNumModes() returns numModes_ = numPartials (configured count, line 654), NOT the active/non-culled count, so the divisor is sqrt(24)=>~0.204 even after Nyquist culling (lines 254-256). Confirmed.
- Mix is purely feedforward + additive; the body<->shell feedback path was removed (lines 707-731). Confirmed.
- Shell b1 = max(25, 2.5*bodyB1) + (1-mat)*15, deliberately holding shell t60 to ~40% of body t60 (lines 1141-1142, comments 1095-1138). With default bodyN=0.4 -> bodyB1~20.1, mat=0.4 -> shell b1~59 s^-1 (t60~0.117s) vs body t60~0.344s. Confirmed, and the justifying comments still cite the now-removed feedback loop -> dead-requirement observation is fair.

But the AUDIBILITY conclusion ("too quiet AND too short-lived to EVER be audible / produces no perceptible variation") is NOT supported:
1. The amplitude estimate "at most coupling*amp*(1/sqrt24) = a few percent" treats the modal bank as a passive multiplier. It is a resonator: at a mode peak the gain is ~1/(1-R). With shell b1~59, R~0.99866 => ~745x; body R~0.99954 => ~2193x. Shell-to-body output ratio at shell resonances ~ 0.25 * (745/2193) * (0.204/0.158) ~ 0.11, i.e. ~ -19 dB at MAX coupling at shell-mode frequencies (where the body may have no mode). That is an audible tint, not inaudible, and scales with coupling.
2. "most modes Nyquist-culled, leaving ~2-4 at amp>=0.03" is wrong for typical drum f0. Shell f0 ~0.625*f_head; at f_head=100-300 Hz, ~15-22 of 24 modes survive the 0.49*sr Nyquist guard. Only very high-pitched pads leave ~2-4. Also the 0.03 amp FLOOR (line 1088) sits far above the cull threshold 0.0001 (line 678), so amplitude culling never removes shell modes; the framing conflates the floor with audibility.
3. "dies before the body / only attack": shell t60 ~117ms vs body ~344ms is a real audible ring (~34-40% of body), not merely an attack transient.

Additionally the stage is OFF by default: couplingStrength_ defaults to 0 and the secondary path is gated by secondaryEnabled_>=0.5 AND couplingStrength_>0 (lines 303-307). So most presets get no shell at all -> it is a weak contributor to the broad "presets sound the same" symptom. (Note: the kGlobalCouplingId tests are the master SympatheticResonance engine, a different mechanism.)

Net: the code description is correct, but the central too-quiet/too-short/inaudible claim and the sameness attribution are overstated. There IS a legitimate, modest observation that this stage is conservatively scaled and its decay-shortening rationale is now a dead requirement, so it under-delivers the intended sustained shell ring -- but it is audible when enabled and is not a meaningful driver of the sameness symptom. Under the AUDIBILITY lens this is low / borderline not-a-bug, not a real high/medium finding as framed.

finding.title: stabilityClampedCoupling 0.25 hard clamp is no longer justified by any feedback loop and collapses the usable coupling range
finding.problem: effectiveCoupling_ = clamp(couplingStrength,0,1) * 0.25. The justification (comments line 1109-1131, 1149-1152) is the eigenvalue/Nyquist stability of a body<->shell feedback loop whose closed-loop gain was coupling^2 * 0.5 * Q_body * Q_shell.
finding.why_wrong: That feedback loop was deliberately deleted (line 707-731: the shell now only receives body output and only emits into the additive mix, 'never back through the body bank'). With a pure feedforward + additive topology the system is unconditionally passive — there is no eigenvalue to keep inside the unit circle, so the 0.25 ceiling has no remaining purpose. It just maps the user's full 0..1 Coupling knob into a 0..0.25 internal range, quartering the dynamic range of an already-quiet effect.
finding.sound_impact: The user's Coupling knob can never push the shell beyond a barely-there level; the top 75% of the knob's travel is mathematically unreachable. Another instance of a clamp crushing a parameter's audible range, reinforcing the 'knobs do nothing' impression.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED against the actual code.

Factual claims confirmed:
- drum_voice.h:1153-1156: stabilityClampedCoupling() returns std::clamp(couplingStrength_,0,1) * 0.25f. Confirmed.
- This value is cached into effectiveCoupling_ at noteOn (line 307) and is the only multiplier on the body→shell excitation (line 698: secExc[i] = bodyScratch[i] * effectiveCoupling_).
- The feedback loop IS gone. Lines 707-731 explicitly state the topology is now additive-only: the shell receives body output forward and its output is mixed into bodyScratch ("never back through the body bank"), and the old closed-loop line `excScratch += secOut * coupling/2` survives only as commented-out historical code. secondaryBank_ is processed ONLY in the fast path; the slow path (lines 837-889) never touches it.
- The 0.25 justification (comments 1109-1131, 1149-1152) rests entirely on the eigenvalue/Nyquist stability of that deleted loop (coupling²·0.5·Q_body·Q_shell < 1). With pure feedforward+additive topology that closed-loop eigenvalue no longer exists, so the stability *rationale* for the 0.25 ceiling is genuinely stale/dead. The justifying comment is now incorrect documentation.

So the CORRECTNESS observation holds: the clamp's stated justification no longer applies, and the 0.25 does compress the Coupling knob's internal effect into a 0..0.25 coefficient range.

BUT the finding overstates the impact in two ways, which is why I downgrade severity to low rather than the implied high:
1. "the top 75% of the knob's travel is mathematically unreachable" is FALSE. The mapping is linear and monotonic: knob 0..1 maps to coefficient 0..0.25, so every knob position produces a distinct, increasing coupling level. No part of the travel is dead/unreachable — the knob spans its full range of effect; only the maximum gain coefficient is capped at 0.25.
2. In a feedforward topology the coupling coefficient is simply a mix/level scaler. 0.25 as a maximum is a legitimate gain-staging/voicing choice, not a correctness defect. There is no NaN/Inf, no RT-safety violation, no parameter that fails to denormalize, and no clamp that collapses the range to a constant. The shell still responds audibly across the whole knob.

Net: the described code behavior (0.25 clamp + now-obsolete stability justification) is real and the justifying comment is genuinely wrong/stale — a documentation-and-voicing issue. It is plausibly a minor contributor to "knobs feel weak" (shell maxes out quieter than it could), but it is not a hard correctness bug and does not make the parameter inert. Hence is_real=true, severity=low.

VERIFIED against drum_voice.h. The structural claim holds: (1) effectiveCoupling_ = std::clamp(couplingStrength_,0,1) * 0.25f (lines 307, 1153-1156); (2) the body<->shell feedback loop was deliberately deleted — lines 707-731 explicitly state the shell now only receives body output (secExc[i] = bodyScratch[i] * effectiveCoupling_, line 698, "feedforward (body -> shell), not feedback" line 696) and emits only into the additive mix (bodyScratch[i] += secOut[i], line 731, "passive (additive only, no return path)"); the old closed-loop term `excScratch += secOut * coupling/2` is referenced only as removed (line 713). (3) The 0.25 ceiling's stated rationale (lines 1109-1131, 1149-1152) is precisely the eigenvalue/Nyquist stability of that now-deleted loop (closed-loop gain coupling^2 * 0.5 * Q_body * Q_shell). So the agent is correct that the 0.25 ceiling is vestigial: two cascaded passive modal banks summed feedforward cannot self-oscillate, there is no loop eigenvalue to bound, and the ceiling no longer serves any stability purpose. Raising it would be a legitimate improvement to the Coupling knob's authority.

HOWEVER the AUDIBILITY framing is materially wrong, which caps severity at low: (a) The 0.25 is a LINEAR SCALE, not a clamp. The full 0..1 knob still maps proportionally and monotonically to 0..0.25 — every knob position remains distinct. The finding's claims that "the top 75% of knob travel is mathematically unreachable" and that a "clamp [is] crushing audible range" are false; nothing is collapsed to a single value or rendered unreachable. The real effect is a uniform ~4x (~12 dB) attenuation of the shell contribution, i.e. a subtle effect made more subtle, not a destroyed range. (b) The secondary shell bank is OPT-IN, gated behind secondaryEnabled_ >= 0.5 AND couplingStrength_ > 0 (line 304); it is off by default. It therefore does NOT contribute to the user's "all presets sound the same/boring" symptom on the default path — only presets that explicitly enable 8D coupling are affected. (c) Additional note: processBlockSlow (lines 813-890, used for FeedbackExciter) processes no secondary bank at all, so coupling is entirely absent there — a separate fast/slow divergence, but it reinforces that no feedback loop exists anywhere. Net: a real but minor over-conservative scaling on an opt-in feature, mischaracterized as a range-collapsing clamp; low audibility impact, not a driver of the sameness symptom.

finding.title: configureSecondaryBank / processBlockFast comments describe a body<->shell feedback loop that no longer exists (stale rationale driving live tuning)
finding.problem: The damping-tuning comment block states 'because processBlock feeds the shell's output back into the body's excitation bus' (line 1098-1099) and derives the b1 floor from closed-loop stability (line 1115-1131). The actual code at line 707-731 explicitly removed that feedback path and is feedforward-only.
finding.why_wrong: The damping law that is still applied (b1 floor of 25, >=2.5x body) is computed from a stability requirement that no longer applies, so the live tuning of the shell's decay is governed by an obsolete constraint. The contradiction between the comment and the code makes the (overly aggressive) damping look intentional when it is actually a leftover.
finding.sound_impact: Indirect: it locks in the force-fast shell decay from the audibility finding, perpetuating the inaudible shell. Also a maintenance hazard — a future editor will 'preserve' stability that isn't needed.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly against drum_voice.h.

THE CONTRADICTION IS REAL AND PRESENT:
- Comment at lines 1098-1102 states "Because processBlock feeds the shell's output back into the body's excitation bus, a long shell tail keeps re-energising body modes... the voice never retires" — describes a closed-loop feedback topology.
- Comment at lines 1109 ("tuned to keep the body↔shell feedback loop stable") and 1115-1131 explicitly derive the b1 floor from a closed-loop stability requirement: "the closed-loop gain coupling²·0.5·Q_body·Q_shell must stay below unity."
- The damping law is LIVE and applied: line 1141 `const float b1 = std::max(25.0f, 2.5f * bodyB1) + (1.0f - mat) * 15.0f;` with the floor justified at 1138-1140 as keeping "the loop stable."
- BUT the actual mix code at lines 707-731 explicitly removed that feedback path: line 696 "This is feedforward (body → shell), not feedback"; lines 713-729 "The previous closed-loop topology (excScratch += secOut * coupling/2) created a feedback loop... we drop the feedback path entirely"; line 731 is `bodyScratch[i] += secOut[i]` — additive into the audible output only, "never back through the body bank."

So the stability constraint that governs the shell's b1 floor (≥25 s⁻¹, ≥2.5× body) no longer applies to the live topology. The comment is internally contradictory and drives over-conservative tuning. (The redundant stabilityClampedCoupling() at 1149-1156 likewise still references "the feedback loop eigenvalue.")

CORROBORATING THE SOUND-IMPACT CLAIM: The voice-retire logic (lines 524-549) keys solely on the body bank's active modes (sharedBank.getNumActiveModes()==0), NOT on the shell bank. Therefore the "long shell tail → voice never retires" problem cited in the comment was strictly a feedback consequence; in the current feedforward path a long shell tail would merely add audible ring (which lines 707-711 call desirable) and could NOT keep body modes alive. This shows the aggressive damping is justified entirely by an obsolete closed-loop constraint, plausibly contributing (indirectly) to the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom by collapsing the shell's decay range.

SEVERITY: low (not critical/high). The damping math is numerically valid and stable — there is no NaN/Inf, allocation, lock, denormal, or parameter-denorm bug, and no runtime miscompute. It is a stale-rationale / maintainability defect with an indirect, modest timbral consequence (force-fast, less-audible shell), not a functional failure. Confidence high: both the comment text and the contradicting feedforward code are unambiguous and the retire-path corroboration is concrete.

CODE CONFIRMS THE CONTRADICTION. I read drum_voice.h:1095-1147 (configureSecondaryBank) and :690-734 (processBlockFast).

The comment block at 1095-1131 is genuinely stale. It states: "Because processBlock feeds the shell's output back into the body's excitation bus, a long shell tail keeps re-energising body modes" (1098-1102); "Shell damping tuned to keep the body↔shell feedback loop stable" (1109); and a full closed-loop "Stability requirement: ... coupling²·0.5·Q_body·Q_shell must stay below unity" derivation (1115-1131) that drives the multiplier choice.

The actual processing at 690-734 is explicitly feedforward-only. Line 696: "This is feedforward (body → shell), not feedback." Lines 707-731 explicitly document REMOVING the old closed-loop topology ("we drop the feedback path entirely", "no return path into the body's modal excitation") and the mix is purely additive: bodyScratch[i] += secOut[i] (line 730-731). The redundant stabilityClampedCoupling() comment at 1149-1156 also still references the dead "feedback loop eigenvalue."

So the finding's factual core is correct: the live damping law applied at 1141 — b1 = max(25, 2.5*bodyB1) + (1-mat)*15 — and fed into secondaryBank_.setModes (1144-1146) is justified entirely by a closed-loop stability constraint that no longer exists. The value is real and applied (audible fast shell decay), so this is not a no-op.

AUDIBILITY LENS — why severity is LOW, not higher:
1. The finding is fundamentally about an obsolete RATIONALE / stale comment, not a demonstrated audible defect. Nothing shows b1>=25 (shell t60 ~0.17-0.28s) is the WRONG value — only that its written justification is dead.
2. With feedback removed, a short passive shell ring is a legitimate design choice; the code itself notes the shell "still contributes audible ring during the attack." There is no rendered evidence of an inaudible/incorrect shell.
3. Shell damping still varies per preset via secondaryMaterial_ and bodyDampingB1_, so it is not a stage that homogenizes timbre across presets. As a single parallel-mixed, fast-decaying component, its contribution to the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom is marginal — far less than the per-voice softClip, energy limiter, or 1/sqrt(N) normalize the brief flags as prime suspects.

Conclusion: real (the comment-vs-code contradiction is verified and the obsolete constraint does govern live tuning), but it is a maintenance/documentation hazard with at most marginal audibility — not a primary driver of the sameness symptom. Corrected severity: low.

finding.title: No test verifies the secondary shell bank is ever audible or that coupling produces an audible change
finding.problem: The only test code that touches setCouplingStrength/setSecondaryEnabled/setSecondarySize/setSecondaryMaterial just wires the parameters through for a polyphony regression. The entire infinite-ring suite (test_kit_switch_infinite_ring.cpp) only asserts the output decays to silence — it would happily pass if the shell contributed nothing.
finding.why_wrong: The 8D feature's purpose is to add an audible shell character that varies with the coupling/secondary parameters. There is no test asserting that enabling coupling changes the output (energy/spectrum) versus coupling off, nor that the slow path matches the fast path for a coupled voice. So the slow-path divergence and the inaudibility are completely untested and could regress/ship unnoticed.
finding.sound_impact: Allows the 'coupling does nothing audible' state to persist undetected; no guardrail catches that the parameter has no perceptual effect.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified by reading the actual code. test_phase2_regression_maxpoly1.cpp:86-89 calls setCouplingStrength/setSecondaryEnabled/setSecondarySize/setSecondaryMaterial purely from kCfg defaults to wire params through a polyphony regression test — there is no assertion about audibility. A repo-wide grep confirms these four setters appear only in voice_pool.cpp (production wiring) and that one test file. The other test_coupling_* files (test_coupling_phase9_verification, test_coupling_matrix, test_coupling_integration, test_coupling_energy, etc.) target a DIFFERENT feature: the master-bus sympathetic-resonance / CouplingMatrix engine (Phase 5/9, globalCoupling via sympathetic_resonance.h), not the per-voice 8D head/shell secondary bank. The infinite-ring suite (test_kit_switch_infinite_ring.cpp) only asserts decay-to-silence (peak < kSilenceThreshold) and non-infinite ring (winPeaks[9]*100 < winPeaks[0]); its only references to the secondary-shell parameters (lines 1550-1553) are WARN prints, not assertions — so it would pass with zero shell contribution. No test compares coupling-on vs coupling-off output energy/spectrum, and none asserts the secondary shell is audible. I additionally confirmed the slow-path divergence the finding mentions is real: secondaryBank_.processBlock and effectiveCoupling_ are used ONLY in processBlockFast (drum_voice.h:691-734); processBlockSlow (the FeedbackExciter path, lines 813-890) contains no secondary-bank processing at all, so a coupled FeedbackExciter voice contributes zero shell and nothing tests this. The described behaviour is accurately present. Severity corrected to low: this is a test-coverage/guardrail gap (and a latent fast/slow divergence for the FeedbackExciter+coupling combination), not itself a confirmed audible-failure in shipping DSP. It is a real, accurate finding under the correctness lens but its concrete impact is missing test coverage rather than a definite runtime defect, hence low rather than medium/high.

VERIFIED the factual claim by reading the actual code. At test_phase2_regression_maxpoly1.cpp:86-89 the four 8D setters (setCouplingStrength/setSecondaryEnabled/setSecondarySize/setSecondaryMaterial) only wire kCfg defaults through so the bare DrumVoice reference stays bit-identical to the VoicePool path at maxPolyphony=1; there is no assertion about coupling producing an effect. I checked every other test that touches these params: (1) test_kit_switch_infinite_ring.cpp builds a makeCouplingHeavyKit (globalCoupling=0.7, etc.) but its assertions are exclusively silence/decay (peak < kSilenceThreshold), NaN-run length (longestRun < N), and tonality (CHECK_FALSE(s.tonal)) — it would pass identically whether the shell adds audio or not. (2) test_orchestral_distortion.cpp has explicit no-secondary/no-coupling scenarios but only std::printf's peak/rms with ZERO CHECK/REQUIRE on the coupling delta, so it always passes. (3) test_param_reachability_in_editor.cpp is purely controller/editor tag-wiring, not audibility. So it is true that NO test asserts coupling-on vs coupling-off changes the output (energy/spectrum), nor that the slow path matches the fast path for a coupled voice. The factual coverage-gap claim holds.\n\nHOWEVER, applying the AUDIBILITY lens: this is a missing-test finding, not a code defect. The finding does not demonstrate that 8D coupling is actually inaudible — it only argues the gap could let an inaudible state ship undetected. A coverage gap has zero direct audible impact and cannot itself cause the 'presets sound the same/boring' symptom; if coupling were in fact silently doing nothing, the root-cause bug would live in the DSP, and this missing test would be a contributing process risk, not the bug. Per the task rubric (zero audible impact => low/not-a-bug), this is a real but low-severity test-coverage gap, not a critical/high audibility bug.

finding.title: NonlinearCoupling applies an unconditional recipSqrt saturator to the ENTIRE voice signal whenever amount>0, flattening level/dynamics
finding.problem: When amount_ != 0, every sample is run through Krate::DSP::Sigmoid::recipSqrt(modulated) = modulated/sqrt(modulated^2+1). This is a tanh-like soft-clip applied to the full body+noise+click+modeInject sum, not just to the modulation. At amount_==0 the function is an exact bypass (returns bodyOutput), so enabling coupling at any non-zero amount discontinuously inserts a compressor on the whole signal even when the AM term (couplingStrength*dEnv) is ~0.
finding.why_wrong: recipSqrt is not transparent below 1.0: recipSqrt(0.4)=0.371 (~7% reduction), recipSqrt(0.7)=0.573 (~18%), recipSqrt(1.0)=0.707 (~29%). The combined signal frequently exceeds 0.3-1.0 (noise layer carries 3x kStandaloneOutputGain, click adds full amplitude, modeInject adds up to ~1.0 peak), so turning coupling on permanently squashes peaks and compresses the timbre. The contract (unnatural_zone_contract.md line 90/99) sanctions this limiter as a peak-safety net, so it is design-as-specified, but it is a genuine dynamics-flattening stage gated only on amount!=0 (amount=0 remains exact bypass and is unaffected).
finding.sound_impact: Any preset that enables Nonlinear Coupling gets its transients and overall level pulled down and its waveform softened regardless of dialed character, homogenising loud presets toward the same ~0.7 ceiling shape. Directly contributes to the 'mostly sound the same and boring' symptom for presets with coupling on.

verdicts.reasoning:
I read nonlinear_coupling.h:69-98 and confirmed the exact behavior. When amount_==0 (lines 71-72) the function is a bit-identical bypass returning bodyOutput. When amount_!=0, lines 92-97 compute modulated = bodyOutput*(1+couplingStrength*dEnv) then return Krate::DSP::Sigmoid::recipSqrt(modulated) UNCONDITIONALLY — the saturator is applied to the whole signal regardless of whether the AM term (couplingStrength*dEnv) is ~0. So enabling coupling at any non-zero amount does insert a saturator on the entire voice signal, exactly as the finding claims.

recipSqrt is confirmed in sigmoid.h:188-194 as x/sqrt(x*x+1). The cited numbers are correct: recipSqrt(0.4)=0.371 (~7% cut), 0.7=0.573 (~18%), 1.0=0.707 (~29%). The signal fed in is large: in drum_voice.h:435-443 the input is body + noise(×3.0, noise_layer.h:218) + click(×2.0, click_layer.h:153) + modeInject, so it routinely reaches the 0.3-1.0+ region where recipSqrt meaningfully squashes peaks. The processBlockFast path (lines 755-780) gates identically on getAmount()!=0. So the described code behavior is genuinely present.

HOWEVER, on the 'is it actually wrong' half of the CORRECTNESS lens: this is design-as-specified, not a defect. The contract (unnatural_zone_contract.md:90,99) MANDATES a saturating energy limiter (FR-053/SC-008: |output|<1.0 for any input) and explicitly specifies the amount==0 exact-bypass (line 102-103). recipSqrt replaced the contract's TanhADAA as a cheaper equivalent saturator with the same bounded-output guarantee. The finding's own text concedes 'it is design-as-specified.' So there is no logic error, no RT-safety issue (stateless, no alloc), no NaN/Inf risk (recipSqrt handles Inf at sigmoid.h:190). It is a deliberate peak-safety limiter whose perceptual side-effect (dynamics flattening) is a tradeoff, not a correctness bug.

Verdict: the code behavior is real and accurately described, so is_real=true, but under the correctness lens it is functioning as specified — not wrong. It is a legitimate contributor to the 'sound the same/boring' tone-flattening symptom, so it has real perceptual relevance, hence low severity rather than not-a-bug. It would warrant a design discussion (e.g. only saturate when the signal actually exceeds threshold, or apply only to the AM-added component) rather than a correctness fix.

Verified against the actual code. nonlinear_coupling.h:69-98: processSample early-outs to an exact bypass when amount_==0 (line 71-72), but for any amount_!=0 it unconditionally runs the FULL signal through Krate::DSP::Sigmoid::recipSqrt(modulated) at line 97, where modulated = bodyOutput*(1 + couplingStrength*dEnv). The recipSqrt math in sigmoid.h:188-194 is exactly x/sqrt(x*x+1), so the cited gain-reduction figures are correct: recipSqrt(0.4)=0.371 (~7%), recipSqrt(0.7)=0.573 (~18%), recipSqrt(1.0)=0.707 (~29%). It is NOT transparent below 1.0 — it is a tanh-like soft-clip on the entire body+noise+click+modeInject sum, applied even when the AM term (couplingStrength*dEnv) is near zero.

Signal-level claim confirmed: drum_voice.h:435 builds combinedBody = body + noiseSample + clickSample where noiseSample carries NoiseLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain=3.0 (noise_layer.h:218) and clickSample carries ClickLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain=2.0 (click_layer.h:153). modeInject is then added at line 442 before nonlinearCoupling.processSample at line 443. So the input to recipSqrt routinely sits in / above the 0.3-1.0 range, exactly where recipSqrt is lossy. This per-sample stage runs in both block paths (lines 443 fast/slow-shared inner and 779/882), gated only on the coupling amount toggle.

AUDIBILITY: This is genuinely audible — turning coupling on at any non-zero amount discontinuously inserts a compressor that pulls transient peaks down ~3dB+ and softens the waveform regardless of the dialed character, pushing loud coupling-on presets toward a common ~0.7 ceiling shape. It is a real dynamics-flattening stage that contributes to the 'mostly sound the same' symptom. Two factors keep it at medium rather than high: (1) it only affects presets that enable coupling (amount!=0), not all presets — amount==0 is bit-exact bypass; and (2) a downstream toneShaper plus the per-voice softClip([-1,1]) at drum_voice.h:446 already shape/clip the signal, so recipSqrt is an additional flattening layer rather than the sole homogenizer. The finding also correctly notes the contract sanctions this as a peak-safety net, so it is design-as-specified, but the discontinuous full-signal compression gated on a character knob is a legitimate audibility/dynamics issue.

finding.title: NonlinearCoupling AM modulation is near-inaudible: dEnv per sample is tiny so even amount=1 produces only a few-percent transient AM
finding.problem: modulated = bodyOutput * (1 + velocity_*amount_*8*dEnv) where dEnv = env - previousEnv_ is the per-sample change of an RMS envelope follower (attack 5ms, release 50ms). The one-pole attack coefficient at 44.1k is exp(-2pi/(0.005*44100)) ~= 0.972, so env moves only ~2.8% toward target per sample; the per-sample dEnv during the strongest transient is on the order of 0.008-0.02. With kCouplingScale=8 and amount=1, velocity=1 that yields a modulation factor of only ~1.06-1.16, and only during the brief attack/release; in steady state dEnv->0 so the modulation vanishes entirely.
finding.why_wrong: The parameter is supposed to produce 'time-varying spectral centroid character' (contract US6-4). A few-percent amplitude wiggle confined to the attack edge is barely perceptible and does not change timbre across the parameter sweep. Combined with the level-dependent recipSqrt squash, the NET audible effect of raising the knob is often a slight overall level/peak reduction rather than obvious brightening, so the knob fails to deliver an audible, parameter-proportional change commensurate with its range.
finding.sound_impact: The Nonlinear Coupling knob does little audibly distinct across presets; presets that differ only in this value sound essentially identical. Contributes to the 'presets sound the same' diagnosis.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against actual code.

CODE CONFIRMED (nonlinear_coupling.h:69-98): processSample computes env = envFollower_.processSample(bodyOutput); dEnv = env - previousEnv_; couplingStrength = velocity_*amount_*kCouplingScale with constexpr kCouplingScale=8.0f (line 89); modulated = bodyOutput*(1.0f + couplingStrength*dEnv) (line 92); then recipSqrt. So the modulation factor is exactly (1 + velocity*amount*8*dEnv) as the finding states.

ENVELOPE FOLLOWER CONFIRMED (envelope_follower.h): prepared as RMS, attack 5ms, release 50ms (nonlinear_coupling.h:43-46). Coefficient formula coeff = exp(-2π/timeSamples) (line 359-365). For attack 5ms @44.1k: timeSamples=220.5, attackCoeff=exp(-6.283/220.5)=exp(-0.0285)≈0.972, so the envelope moves only ~2.8% of the gap per sample. The finding's coefficient and per-step figures are correct.

CORE LOGIC HOLDS:
1. Steady-state: once env settles, dEnv = env - previousEnv_ → 0, so the modulation factor → exactly 1.0 (no modulation). Mathematically certain. The effect is confined to attack/release edges.
2. Magnitude: with k=8, amount=1, vel=1, factor = 1 + 8*dEnv. For sustained/decaying modal content dEnv is small (a few thousandths to ~0.02), giving factors ~1.04–1.16, i.e. a few-percent-to-~16% transient AM. (Caveat: the very first onset samples can spike dEnv larger than the finding's stated 0.008–0.02 because sqrt has steep slope near zero, e.g. a step toward power 1 gives env≈sqrt(0.028)≈0.17 on sample one — so the AM is briefly larger than the finding implies. But it is still a transient-only edge effect that vanishes in sustain, which is the substance of the claim.)
3. Not spectral/timbral: bodyOutput*(1+k*dEnv) is pure amplitude scaling, not filtering. The header itself (lines 8-11) explicitly admits "This is NOT a von Karman cubic coupling... It's a character knob, not a physical model." The contract's "time-varying spectral centroid character" (US6-4, cited in the code comment lines 78-79) is not actually delivered by an envelope-delta AM.
4. recipSqrt level interaction (sigmoid.h:188-194): x/sqrt(x²+1) ≈ x for |x|<<1 (minimal effect at typical sub-unity body levels) and compresses peaks near/above 1. So raising the knob can slightly reduce peaks rather than brighten — consistent with the finding's "net effect is a slight level/peak reduction."

PARAM PLUMBING CHECKED: kPadNonlinearCoupling → cfg.nonlinearCoupling → setAmount with clamp(value,0,1) (voice_pool.cpp:602,823-824; processor.cpp:121-122). Normalized [0,1] is used directly as amount, so denormalization is correct and amount=1 is genuinely the max — there is no boundary bug masking the issue; the weak effect is inherent to the algorithm.

CONCLUSION: The described behaviour is actually present and the parameter genuinely fails to deliver an audible, parameter-proportional timbral change across its range; the effect is a transient-only amplitude wiggle that vanishes in steady state, plus a mild peak squash from recipSqrt. This is a real efficacy/voicing bug and a legitimate contributor to "presets sound the same." It is NOT a real-time-safety, NaN/Inf, or denormalization bug (the code is bounded, stateless math, denormal-flushed in the follower), so it is not critical/high; medium severity is appropriate given its contribution to the sameness diagnosis but limited individual audibility.

Verified against the actual code at plugins/membrum/src/dsp/unnatural/nonlinear_coupling.h:69-98 and the envelope follower it depends on (dsp/include/krate/dsp/processors/envelope_follower.h:312-326).

The formula matches the finding exactly: line 92 `modulated = bodyOutput * (1.0f + couplingStrength * dEnv)` with `couplingStrength = velocity_*amount_*8` (kCouplingScale=8, line 89), `dEnv = env - previousEnv_` (line 80), and `env` is a per-sample RMS envelope follower (mode RMS, attack 5ms, release 50ms; lines 44-46). The follower smooths in the SQUARED domain then takes sqrt, with attackCoeff = exp(-2pi/(0.005*44100)) ≈ 0.972 (confirmed), so the envelope moves only ~2.8% toward target per sample. Output is then passed through Sigmoid::recipSqrt (line 97), a bounded x/sqrt(x^2+1) ceiling that slightly squashes peaks.

I numerically simulated the stage on a representative decaying 200 Hz modal tone (peak 0.8, 300ms decay) at amount=velocity=1: the AM factor peaks at ~1.10 (a +10% boost confined to the brief attack edge) and returns to ~1.0 in sustain as dEnv->0. A sustained-tone test showed only ~2.7% peak-to-peak within-cycle ripple. The finding slightly understated the transient peak (~10%, not 'a few percent'), but its substance holds: the modulation is a short-lived amplitude bump at the onset with essentially no steady-state effect and NO waveshaping/harmonic generation — so it cannot deliver the 'time-varying spectral centroid character / brightening' that contract US6-4 promises. The header even admits (lines 6-11) this is 'a character knob, not a physical model' and not the w^3 von Karman cubic. Combined with the recipSqrt squash, raising the knob can net to a slight level reduction rather than audible brightening.

Conclusion: real audibility/effectiveness defect, not a crash or numerical bug. The knob under-delivers relative to its contract and genuinely contributes to presets differing only in this value sounding nearly identical. It is one of several contributors and not a dominant dynamics-flattener (unlike a master limiter or the per-voice softClip), so severity = low.

finding.title: NoiseLayer resonance maps to Q up to 5.0 on the DrumVoice Lowpass path, reintroducing the resonant pitched-peak the Lowpass switch was meant to remove
finding.problem: denormResonance maps the normalized resonance param to Q in [0.3, 5.0] and configure() applies it via filter_.setResonance(reso). DrumVoice::prepare() forces the parallel noise layer to SVFMode::Lowpass (drum_voice.h:112) with the documented justification that 'a Lowpass with the same cutoff gives broadband-rolloff hash with no spectral peak'. That justification only holds at low Q; a TPT lowpass at Q=5 has a pronounced resonant peak at cutoff.
finding.why_wrong: The stated rationale for switching from Bandpass to Lowpass ('no spectral peak') is false for the upper half of the resonance range. For cymbal/hat/snare presets that set a non-trivial noise resonance, the lowpass develops a sharp peak at cutoff and the noise reads as a pitched tone again -- the exact 'monotonous beep' the mode switch was intended to cure.
finding.sound_impact: High-resonance noise presets get a tonal peak (pitched whine) instead of broadband hash, and many presets land near the same cutoff frequency, so their noise layers can ring at a similar pitch -- another route to perceived sameness/boring tonal coloration.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against actual code. noise_layer.h:186-189 denormResonance() maps norm[0,1] LINEARLY to Q in [0.3, 5.0]: `return 0.3f + clamp(norm,0,1) * (5.0f - 0.3f)`. configure() (line 84-86) computes reso=denormResonance(p.resonance) and applies filter_.setResonance(reso). DrumVoice::prepare() (drum_voice.h:112) forces this parallel layer to SVFMode::Lowpass, with the comment (lines 102-109) explicitly justifying the switch by claiming "A Lowpass with the same cutoff gives broadband-rolloff hash with no spectral peak." I confirmed DrumVoice's standalone noise path uses configure() (drum_voice.h:281), NOT the NoiseBody configureRaw() path (noise_body.h:73) whose hardcoded Q≈0.94 is genuinely Butterworth-flat.

The physics claim holds: the SVF here is a 2nd-order TPT lowpass (svf.h, Cytomic topology). A 2nd-order lowpass develops a resonant peak whenever Q > 1/sqrt(2)≈0.707, with peak gain ≈ Q at high Q. At Q=5 (norm=1.0) the lowpass has a pronounced ~14 dB peak at cutoff. Even the default resonance norm=0.2 maps to Q≈1.24 (peak ~3 dB), already above the no-peak threshold. So the documented rationale ("no spectral peak") is FALSE for roughly the upper ~91% of the resonance range (any norm > ~0.085 gives Q>0.707). The bug is real: the Bandpass->Lowpass switch does NOT eliminate the spectral peak it was meant to cure; it merely reduces it, and at high resonance the noise reads as a pitched/colored tone again. The controller even formats this param as a Q value 0.3-5.0 (controller.cpp:2445,2522) and exposes it as "Noise Resonance."

Severity corrected from the implied tone to medium rather than high: I checked default_kit.h - factory/default presets cluster noise resonance at 0.15-0.3 norm (Q ~1.0-1.7), where the lowpass peak is only ~1-4.5 dB. That is audible tonal coloration that contributes to perceived sameness (mild peak near similar cutoffs across presets), but it is NOT the "sharp pitched whine" the finding describes for the upper range, because shipped presets don't set high resonance. No NaN/crash/RT-safety issue. So: genuine correctness defect (false documented rationale + incomplete fix), plausibly a minor contributor to homogenized timbre, but overstated as a dominant cause and as producing a sharp whine in practice.

VERIFIED THE CODE MECHANISM (it is exactly as described):
- noise_layer.h:186-189 denormResonance maps norm[0,1] linearly to Q in [0.3, 5.0].
- configure() (line 84-86) applies it via filter_.setResonance(reso) on the parallel layer.
- drum_voice.h:112 forces noiseLayer_ to SVFMode::Lowpass with the comment (lines 104-111) justifying it as "a Lowpass with the same cutoff gives broadband-rolloff hash with no spectral peak."
- svf.h is a Cytomic TPT SVF; its lowpass mode magnitude at cutoff scales ~ with Q, so at Q=5 there IS a pronounced resonant peak (~+14 dB). The comment's "no spectral peak" rationale is therefore genuinely false at high Q. To that extent the finding is correct: the documented justification does not hold across the full resonance range.
- Parameter plumbing is correct: kNoiseLayerResonanceId -> setPadConfigField -> configure() denormalizes. No NaN/denorm/normalization bug. SVF flushes denormals and guards NaN/Inf.

AUDIBILITY (the lens) — the finding's IMPACT claim is overstated and not supported by the shipped presets:
- I read every default template in default_kit.h. The maximum noiseLayerResonance any factory template uses is 0.30 (Cymbal); others: Kick 0.15, Snare 0.25, Tom 0.25, Hat 0.20, Clap 0.25. NONE reach the upper half of the range the finding relies on ("for cymbal/hat/snare presets that set a non-trivial noise resonance" / "the upper half of the resonance range").
- denormResonance(0.30) = 0.3 + 0.30*4.7 = 1.71. denormResonance(0.20)=1.24, (0.25)=1.475. A 2nd-order lowpass at Q~1.2-1.7 has only a ~+2 to +5 dB hump at cutoff — a mild coloration, not the "pronounced resonant peak / pitched whine / pitched tone" the finding describes. The dramatic peak only appears near Q=5 (norm~1.0), which no factory preset sets.
- Also, even at high Q a lowpass peak rolls off above cutoff, so it is far less tonal than the bandpass it replaced; it does not read as a pure pitched tone.
- The parallel noiseLayer_ IS active for all body models (configured per-note at drum_voice.h:281 regardless of NoiseBody), so the routing claim is correct; but the magnitude of the effect at preset values is small.

CONCLUSION: The code matches the description and the comment's blanket "no spectral peak" claim is technically inaccurate at high Q, so is_real=true. But under the AUDIBILITY lens, with factory presets capped at norm<=0.30 (Q<=1.71), this produces at most a mild lowpass hump, not a pitched tone, and does not credibly contribute to the reported preset sameness/boring symptom. It is a doc/rationale imprecision plus a theoretical high-Q edge a user could reach manually. Severity: low.

finding.title: NoiseLayer kStandaloneOutputGain (3x) is calibrated for the internal Bandpass chain but DrumVoice forces Lowpass, so the parallel noise level is mis-calibrated
finding.problem: The kStandaloneOutputGain=3.0 comment states it compensates for 'the bandpass SVF + ADSR chain [attenuating] white noise to ~0.1 RMS for typical Q values'. DrumVoice's parallel layer is switched to Lowpass (drum_voice.h:112), and a lowpass passes far more (especially low-frequency) energy than a normalized 0 dB-peak bandpass, so the actual layer RMS at a given mix is higher than the value the 3x boost was tuned against.
finding.why_wrong: The amplitude-calibration constant assumes a filter response (bandpass) that the DrumVoice path no longer uses. The 3x boost is now applied on top of a hotter (lowpass) signal, so the noise layer is louder than the 'comparable to the modal body without exceeding 0 dBFS' target the constant documents.
finding.sound_impact: The parallel noise layer sits louder than intended in the per-voice mix, pushing more presets toward a noise-dominated, similar-sounding character and eating headroom before the per-voice softClip -- consistent with presets sounding flat/boring.

verdicts.reasoning: Verified against actual code. (1) noise_layer.h:210-218: kStandaloneOutputGain=3.0f with a comment stating it compensates for "the bandpass SVF + ADSR chain [attenuating] white noise to ~0.1 RMS for typical Q values" so mix=1.0 yields a layer peak "comparable to the modal body without exceeding 0 dBFS (SC-008)". (2) drum_voice.h:112: noiseLayer_.setFilterMode(SVFMode::Lowpass) explicitly switches the parallel layer away from its default Bandpass (with a comment acknowledging the switch). (3) drum_voice.h:433-435: the 3x boost is applied directly on top of the lowpass output: noiseSample = noiseLayer_.processSample() * kStandaloneOutputGain, then combinedBody = body + noiseSample + clickSample. The same pattern appears in the slow path (lines 743-745) and per-sample path (873-875). (4) svf.h confirms the physics disparity: Bandpass is normalized to constant-0dB-peak (m1=k=1/Q, lines 565-571, comment lines 543-546/63), passing only a narrow band of white-noise energy; Lowpass (m2=1, lines 555-559) passes the entire 0-to-cutoff band including all low-frequency energy at 12 dB/oct. For the same cutoff/Q, lowpass output RMS is substantially higher (several dB, often 6+ dB depending on cutoff) than a normalized 0dB-peak bandpass. So the 3x constant, calibrated against the ~0.1 RMS bandpass response, is now applied to a hotter signal and overshoots its documented "comparable to the modal body" target. (5) Real-world relevance: Noise Mix default is 0.35 (controller.cpp:199) and presets set noiseLayerMix to 0.62-0.98 (CHANGELOG), so the layer is on at meaningful levels, not off-by-default. The described behaviour is present and is genuinely a miscalibration: the calibration constant assumes a filter response the path no longer uses. Severity reduced from a potential high to medium because it is a gain-staging/scaling error (no crash, NaN, or RT-safety violation), the magnitude varies per preset with cutoff/Q rather than being a fixed gross error, and downstream level is bounded by the per-voice softClip([-1,1]) so it does not run away. It is consistent with the reported noise-dominated/flat preset character but is one contributing factor rather than a proven sole cause.

VERIFIED IN CODE. The finding's three premises all hold:

1. noise_layer.h:210-218 — kStandaloneOutputGain = 3.0f, and its comment explicitly states the 3x compensates for "the bandpass SVF + ADSR chain [attenuating] white noise to ~0.1 RMS for typical Q values," with the goal of a "layer peak comparable to the modal body without exceeding the 0 dBFS per-voice peak contract (SC-008)." The constant is calibrated against a Bandpass response.

2. drum_voice.h:112 — DrumVoice's parallel layer is explicitly switched to Lowpass: noiseLayer_.setFilterMode(Krate::DSP::SVFMode::Lowpass), with a comment confirming the parallel layer no longer uses Bandpass.

3. The 3x is applied on top of that lowpass output in all three voice paths: drum_voice.h:434 (processSample), :745 (processBlockFast), :875 (processBlockSlow): noiseLayer_.processSample()/processBlock(...) * NoiseLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain, summed into combinedBody pre-UnnaturalZone/ToneShaper/softClip.

DSP correctness of the mis-calibration: svf.h confirms the standard Cytomic TPT SVF. Bandpass out.band = k*v1 is normalized to a constant 0 dB PEAK (line 456), so for flat white noise it only passes a narrow band → low RMS (the ~0.1 the constant assumes). Lowpass out.low = v2 is a 2nd-order LP with unity (0 dB) passband from DC to ~cutoff and only 12 dB/oct rolloff — it integrates far more white-noise power than the narrow normalized bandpass. So at a given mix the lowpass layer RMS exceeds the value the 3x was tuned against, and the 3x makes it hotter still. The discrepancy is cutoff-dependent: modest (~1.5x / a few dB) at the default low cutoff (~850 Hz at norm 0.5), but large (well over 6 dB) at bright presets (high cutoff) where the lowpass passband is huge relative to the narrow bandpass band. The constant's documented assumption is genuinely invalid for the path that uses it.

AUDIBILITY: Real but bounded. The noise layer default mix is 0.0 (off), so this only manifests on presets that engage noise (e.g. the diagnostic test uses 0.12, damping test uses 0.85). When engaged, the layer is louder than intended — biasing the per-voice mix toward broadband noise/hash, homogenizing timbre across presets and consuming headroom ahead of the per-voice softClip(shaped*env*level). That is consistent with the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom, though it is a single-layer level mis-trim rather than a path-breaking defect. Not critical (doesn't break signal flow, no NaN/RT-safety issue, off by default), and high-cutoff worst case is the only place it's strongly audible — hence medium. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact audible magnitude depends on per-preset noiseMix and cutoff values stored in binary .vstpreset files I could not decode, so I could not measure the in-context RMS delta directly.

finding.title: Pitch envelope only modulates the Membrane body; ignored for Plate/Shell/String/Bell/NoiseBody
finding.problem: updateBodyFundamental() only does work for BodyModelType::Membrane (lines 1192-1196); the else branch is an explicit no-op. In the block paths pitchEnvForMembrane = pitchEnvActive && currentBody==Membrane (line 486-487), so for every non-Membrane body the pitch-env Hz computed by toneShaper_.processPitchEnvelope() is either discarded or never advanced. The ToneShaper exposes Start/End/Time/Curve/Knee/Mid parameters that are fully wired through the processor (processor.cpp 90-112), but they reach the DSP only for one of six body models.
finding.why_wrong: The pitch envelope is documented as the control-plane modulator that feeds the body f0 (tone_shaper.h lines 22-24, 363-378). For 5 of 6 bodies, changing Pitch Env Start/End/Time/Curve produces NO audible change. A user dialing an 808-style downward sweep on a Plate or Bell body hears nothing move. Combined with the report that presets 'sound the same,' a whole family of pitch-motion parameters is silently inert on most bodies, removing a primary axis of timbral variation.
finding.sound_impact: Pitch-sweep parameters are dead for Plate/Shell/String/Bell/NoiseBody presets; those presets cannot get the characteristic pitch-drop motion that distinguishes e.g. an 808 boom from a static tone, so they collapse toward static, similar-sounding hits.

verdicts.reasoning: Confirmed by reading drum_voice.h directly. updateBodyFundamental() (lines 1190-1200) only calls updateMembraneFundamental() when bodyBank_.getCurrentType()==Membrane; the comment (1185-1199) explicitly states "Other body types: no-op in Phase 7" and that String/Plate/Bell/Shell/NoiseBody "do not track the sweep per-sample." Both production block paths gate the pitch envelope on pitchEnvForMembrane = isPitchEnvActive() && currentBody==Membrane (lines 484-487): the fast path (603-613) only calls toneShaper_.processPitchEnvelope() and updateMembraneFundamentalOnBank() inside the pitchEnvForMembrane branch, and the slow path (823-827, 859-860) does the same. So for the 5 non-Membrane bodies the envelope is never even advanced (processPitchEnvelope never ticked) and the bank f0 is never rescaled. The noteOn seed at lines 263-267 is also gated on currentBody==Membrane, so non-Membrane bodies don't even get the start-frequency seed via that call (the comment at 1187 claims configureForNoteOn applies an initial value, so at most a static start pitch survives, never the sweep). The per-sample processSample() path (397-401) routes through updateBodyFundamental() which is itself the Membrane-only no-op dispatcher, so it provides no relief. The ToneShaper Start/End/Time/Curve/Knee/Mid params are real, fully wired through the processor, but their pitch-sweep effect reaches only 1 of 6 body models. This is a genuine correctness defect: pitch-sweep motion is inert for Plate/Shell/String/Bell/NoiseBody, matching the "presets sound the same/static" symptom by removing a primary timbral-motion axis. Severity downgraded from any implied higher level to medium: it is a documented "future phase" limitation rather than a crash or RT-safety violation, partially mitigated by an initial static pitch, but the parameters genuinely do nothing audible on most bodies, so it is a real bug.

Confirmed by reading the actual code, and the defect is actually broader than the finding states.

PER-SAMPLE/PER-BLOCK PATH: updateBodyFundamental() (drum_voice.h:1190-1200) does work only for BodyModelType::Membrane; the else branch is an explicit documented no-op ("Other body types: no-op in Phase 7"). In processBlock (lines 484-487) the gate is pitchEnvForMembrane = pitchEnvActive && currentBody==Membrane. In processBlockFast (603-621) the pitch env is only advanced/applied inside `if (pitchEnvForMembrane)`; for non-Membrane bodies the env is neither processed nor pushed into the bank. The per-sample slow path (397-401) calls updateBodyFundamental() which is the same Membrane-only dispatcher. So per-sample/per-block pitch sweep reaches only the Membrane body.

NOTE-ON PATH (stronger than the finding claims): the finding says non-Membrane bodies at least "receive the initial envelope value at noteOn." I checked the mappers — they do NOT. configureForNoteOn passes initialPitchHz into each body's mapper, but PlateMapper, BellMapper, ShellMapper, NoiseBodyMapper, StringMapper (and even MembraneMapper) all declare the parameter as `float /*pitchHz*/` and ignore it (plate_mapper.h:34, bell_mapper.h:30, shell_mapper.h:39, noise_body_mapper.h:78, string_mapper.h:35). The seed-at-start block (drum_voice.h:263-266) that applies initialPitchHz via updateMembraneFundamental() is also gated on currentBody==Membrane. So for the 5 non-Membrane bodies, NOT EVEN the pitch-env start frequency is applied — the entire Start/End/Time/Curve/Knee/Mid family is fully inert.

WIRING IS REAL: processor.cpp:90-112 fully denormalizes and pushes all six pitch-env params into ToneShaper; tone_shaper.h:22-24,363-378 documents the env as the control-plane body-f0 modulator. So these are shipped, user-facing controls that reach the DSP for only 1 of 6 bodies.

AUDIBILITY: Pitch sweep is one of the most perceptually salient dimensions of a percussive hit (the 808 boom vs static-tone distinction). Its complete absence on Plate/Shell/String/Bell/NoiseBody is plainly audible, not marginal, and directly matches the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom: an entire motion-parameter family does nothing on 5/6 bodies, removing a primary axis of variation and collapsing those presets toward static hits. Severity high rather than critical because it is a dead-parameter/missing-modulation defect (no crash/NaN/corruption) and the Membrane path still functions; the code even frames it as a deferred "future phase" scope limitation, but from the user's perspective the controls are shipped and inert.

finding.title: Pitch envelope refresh is block-rate coarse and diverges between fast and slow paths
finding.problem: Three code paths advance/apply the pitch envelope at three different rates. process() (line 397-401) updates the body fundamental EVERY sample. processBlockFast (line 603-620) advances the envelope by 1 sample then consumes (chunk-1) more and applies updateModes ONCE per chunk, where chunk is up to kMaxBlockSize=2048 (line 469/581). processBlockSlow (line 823-827) applies updateModes exactly ONCE for the entire numSamples block with no chunking at all. The code comments repeatedly claim a '64-sample audio block ... ~14 update points across a 20 ms sweep' (lines 587, 811), but nothing subdivides into 64-sample sub-blocks; the only subdivision is the 2048-sample kMaxBlockSize.
finding.why_wrong: A 20 ms sweep at 44.1 kHz spans ~880 samples. With a typical 512-sample host block the slow path performs ~2 pitch updates across the entire sweep (one per block), and the fast path performs 1 update for any block <=2048 samples. That is gross stair-stepping of the body fundamental, not the ~14 points the comment asserts. The two block paths are supposed to be perceptually equivalent (per the task brief) but apply the sweep at materially different granularity, and both differ from the per-sample process() path. The 808 test (test_pitch_envelope_808.cpp line 119) exercises only the per-sample process() path, so the block-path coarseness/divergence is entirely untested.
finding.sound_impact: On the block paths the pitch glide becomes a coarse staircase (audible zipper/quantized pitch steps on fast sweeps), and the same preset can sound different depending on host buffer size / which path (feedback exciter vs not) is active. Fast attack-pitch transients lose their smooth glide, flattening the very motion that gives kicks/toms their character.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code in plugins/membrum/src/dsp/drum_voice.h.

CONFIRMED FACTUAL CLAIMS:
- process() lines 397-401: updates body fundamental EVERY sample (processPitchEnvelope() + updateBodyFundamental()).
- processBlockFast lines 603-621: when pitchEnvForMembrane, takes pitchHz from ONE processPitchEnvelope() call, then ticks (chunk-1) more envelope samples WITHOUT reissuing updateModes, and calls updateMembraneFundamentalOnBank ONCE per chunk. chunk = min(remaining, kMaxBlockSize) where kMaxBlockSize=2048 (lines 469, 581).
- processBlockSlow lines 823-827: applies processPitchEnvelope() + updateMembraneFundamentalOnBank EXACTLY ONCE for the whole block; the per-sample inner loop (lines 859-862) only ticks counters for i>0 with no further bank refresh. No chunking at all in the slow path.
- The '64-sample block / ~14 update points' phrasing appears only as comments at lines 497, 587, 811. grep for 64/sub-block confirms NO 64-sample subdivision exists anywhere; the only block constant is kMaxBlockSize=2048.
- Caller chain confirms no upstream sub-blocking: processor.cpp:723/750 calls voicePool_.processBlock(..., data.numSamples) with the raw host buffer; voice_pool.cpp:345 forwards numSamples directly to DrumVoice::processBlock. So a 512-sample host block => one chunk of 512 => exactly one pitch refresh per block.
- test_pitch_envelope_808.cpp lines 118-119 render via voice.process() per-sample, so the block paths' pitch granularity is indeed untested.

So the described behaviour (block-rate coarse refresh, misleading comments, fast/slow/process divergence, untested block paths) is genuinely present and is a real correctness/documentation defect, with the slow path being even coarser (1 update per full host block) than the comment implies.

WHY MEDIUM, NOT HIGH: The finding's audible-staircase severity is overstated because it ignores the modal bank's coefficient smoothing. In the slow path the bank calls sharedBank.prepareBlockSmoothing() (line 850) and the body uses processSampleNoSmooth — but updateMembraneFundamentalOnBank sets a TARGET that the per-block smoothing interpolates toward rather than a hard per-sample jump, and the fast SIMD path likewise smooths coefficients, so the fundamental ramps rather than hard-stepping. That blunts the 'zipper' claim. It is a real divergence and a real granularity problem, but bounded. It is also not a plausible driver of the user-reported 'presets sound the same/boring' symptom (that points to dynamics-flattening stages, not pitch-glide resolution). Real bug, moderate practical impact => medium.

VERIFIED by reading drum_voice.h. All factual claims hold:

1. process() (lines 397-401): updates the body fundamental EVERY sample via processPitchEnvelope() + updateBodyFundamental().

2. processBlockFast (lines 603-612): calls processPitchEnvelope()*tensionPitchMod once, calls updateMembraneFundamentalOnBank() exactly ONCE per chunk, then consumes (chunk-1) more envelope samples with (void)processPitchEnvelope() WITHOUT reapplying updateModes. chunk = min(remaining, kMaxBlockSize) and kMaxBlockSize=2048 (line 469/581). So 1 fundamental update per <=2048-sample chunk.

3. processBlockSlow (lines 823-827): applies updateMembraneFundamentalOnBank() exactly ONCE for the entire numSamples block, then ticks the envelope inside the per-sample inner loop (lines 859-860) with no reapplication and no chunking at all.

4. The comments at lines 587 and 811 assert "~14 update points across a 64-sample audio block" but NO 64-sample sub-blocking exists in any path; the only subdivision is the 2048-sample kMaxBlockSize. The comment is genuinely misleading.

5. I traced the call chain: processor.cpp:723/750 passes data.numSamples (full host block) to VoicePool::processBlock, which (voice_pool.cpp:345/448) passes numSamples straight to DrumVoice::processBlock with no sub-blocking. So real-world granularity = host buffer size for the fast path (capped at 2048) and the whole block for the slow path. With a 512-sample host block a 20ms/~880-sample sweep gets only ~2 fundamental steps; the slow path gets 1.

6. The 808 test (test_pitch_envelope_808.cpp line 119) drives only voice.process() per-sample, confirming the block paths' pitch-sweep behavior is untested.

So the divergence (three different rates) and the host-buffer dependence are real correctness defects, and the comment is wrong.

AUDIBILITY (the lens): The effect is real but narrow. It applies ONLY to BodyModelType::Membrane with an active pitch envelope (or active tension mod), and only during the short sweep. A 2-step staircase on a 20ms glide is at the edge of audibility (the modal resonator's continuous IIR phase smears the coefficient step somewhat, but the resonant frequency genuinely jumps). The genuine user-facing consequences are (a) the same membrane preset glides slightly differently depending on host buffer size, and (b) fast/slow paths (feedback-exciter vs not) glide at different granularity. These are correctness/consistency bugs but produce a subtle quality loss, not a gross artifact in most cases.

Regarding the reported "presets sound the same and boring" symptom: this finding does NOT contribute to it. Coarse pitch-glide granularity degrades motion WITHIN pitch-swept membrane presets; it does not homogenize timbre ACROSS presets, flatten dynamics, or collapse parameter ranges. It is unrelated to the sameness/dynamics-flattening cause the brief is hunting. Hence corrected_severity=low: real bug, audible only in a narrow case, not a driver of the headline symptom.

finding.title: Drive stage applies up to ~9x un-compensated makeup gain, compressing dynamics toward a uniform saturated level
finding.problem: setDriveAmount maps amount[0,1] to drive scaling 1.0..10.0 (line 202) feeding the ReciprocalSqrt shaper x/sqrt(x^2+1). The processSample blend is x + driveAmount*(shaped-x) (line 393). There is no output/makeup-gain compensation after the shaper. ReciprocalSqrt with internal drive g has unity-region slope g, so a quiet body sample (~0.05) at full drive returns ~10*0.05/sqrt(1.25) ~= 0.45 (a ~9x boost) while a unity transient is pinned to ~1.0. The downstream softClip(shaped*env*level) (drum_voice.h 769/782/885) then bounds the result.
finding.why_wrong: A drive control that simultaneously boosts low-level signal ~9x and clips peaks to +-1, with no makeup-gain normalization, is a hard compressor/limiter rather than a flavor saturator. The transfer becomes nearly independent of input amplitude as drive rises (everything is pushed into the saturation knee), so per-voice velocity dynamics and the body's natural decay envelope are flattened. This is the classic 'all presets converge to the same loud saturated tone' mechanism the symptom report points at: any preset with non-trivial drive loses dynamic contrast and inter-preset level/timbre differences are squashed into the same recip-sqrt knee.
finding.sound_impact: Directly contributes to presets sounding 'the same and boring': raising drive crushes velocity and decay dynamics and homogenizes timbre toward the recip-sqrt saturation curve; quiet tails get boosted into the same loud sustain so different bodies/decays end up at a similar perceived level and brightness.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against actual code. tone_shaper.h:198-203 maps drive amount[0,1] to internal drive g in [1.0,10.0] via drive_.setDrive(1.0f + driveAmount_*9.0f). The shaper is ReciprocalSqrt (set at lines 100-101). waveshaper.h:205-216 computes process(x) = recipSqrt(drive_*x + asymmetry_) with asymmetry 0, and sigmoid.h:188-194 defines recipSqrt(x)=x/sqrt(x*x+1). So the shaper output is g*x/sqrt((g*x)^2+1) with NO output/makeup-gain term. tone_shaper.h:393 blends x + driveAmount_*(shaped-x); at driveAmount=1 this is exactly the shaped value. I read processSample lines 383-447: the only subsequent stages are wavefolder (gated), DC blocker, and SVF filter — none applies a 1/g makeup or normalization that would undo the low-level boost.

Numerical check confirms the finding's claims: at full drive g=10, a quiet body sample x=0.05 -> 10*0.05/sqrt(0.25+1)=0.5/1.118=0.447 (~8.9x boost); a unity transient x=1.0 -> 10/sqrt(101)=0.995 (pinned to ~1.0). Unity-region slope equals g (up to 10x). The transfer function therefore boosts low-level signal heavily while pinning peaks to +-1, which compresses per-voice velocity and decay dynamics and pushes output toward a uniform saturated level — exactly the dynamics-flattening / 'presets sound the same' mechanism described. The downstream softClip(shaped*env*level) in drum_voice.h further bounds peaks.

The described code behavior is unambiguously present and the dynamics-compressing effect is real, so the finding is real. Severity reduced from the implied high to medium: (1) the effect only manifests at non-trivial drive amounts (driveAmount=0 is bypassed exactly, line 389), so presets with low/zero drive are unaffected; (2) amplitude-compressing transfer with no makeup is the inherent, expected behavior of essentially any saturation/drive control, not a logic error — the genuine gap is the absence of any makeup-gain option, and the unusually aggressive 1..10 drive range with no compensation. It contributes to the reported sameness but is one of several plausible contributors rather than a clear-cut correctness defect.

Verified against the actual code. tone_shaper.h:198-203: setDriveAmount maps amount[0,1] -> drive_.setDrive(1.0f + driveAmount_*9.0f), i.e. internal pre-gain up to 10x. drive_ is a Krate::DSP::Waveshaper with type ReciprocalSqrt (set at line 100). Waveshaper::process(x) = recipSqrt(drive_*x + asymmetry_) (waveshaper.h:205-215), and recipSqrt(x)=x/sqrt(x*x+1) (sigmoid.h:188-194). processSample (tone_shaper.h:387-394) does the dry/shaped blend x = x + driveAmount*(shaped - x) with NO output/makeup gain afterward. Downstream in drum_voice.h:769/782/885 the result is softClip(shaped * env * level). So the described topology is exactly as claimed: a steep small-signal slope (~g, up to 10) into a bounded shaper, with a final hard softClip and zero makeup normalization.

The arithmetic checks out. At full drive (g=10): quiet sample 0.05 -> 0.5/sqrt(1.25)=0.447 (~9x boost); peak 0.36 -> recipSqrt(3.6)=0.964. A body decay from 0.36 down to 0.05 has its dynamic ratio crushed from ~7.2:1 to ~2.2:1 -- a genuine, audible dynamics/decay compression. The 1/sqrt(N) bank normalization (drum_voice.h:200-257) confirms body peaks sit ~0.36 (Membrane) to ~1.0 (Bell), so much of the signal -- especially decay tails -- lives in the small-signal regime where the high-drive boost is steepest. bodyGainCompensation_ is fixed at 1.0 (line 251); no makeup gain exists. The mechanism (high drive => recip-sqrt compressor/limiter that flattens velocity and decay) is real and audible at high drive settings.

AUDIBILITY caveat that lowers severity from the claimed level: drive defaults to 0.0 and the stage is hard-bypassed when amount==0 (plugin_ids.h:67 'default 0'; controller.cpp:165/326 defaultValue 0.0; gate at tone_shaper.h:389). The dry/shaped blend also dilutes the effect at low/moderate amounts (smaller drive_ AND smaller blend weight), so the dramatic 9x compression only appears near driveAmount=1.0. Thus it is a real contributing cause of the 'presets sound the same' symptom ONLY for presets that explicitly dial drive high -- it does not affect default/no-drive presets and is not a universal flattener. Lack of makeup-gain is also a defensible voicing choice rather than an unambiguous defect. Net: real bug, audible at high drive, conditional contributor to the sameness symptom -> medium.

finding.title: Per-voice softClip saturates the entire voice to a clipped square wave even at low level/velocity, erasing dynamics and timbre across presets
finding.problem: The final stage is `out = softClip(shaped * env * level)` where softClip (dsp_utils.h:105) hard-limits to +/-1.0 (output saturates at input >= ~3.0). Empirically the pre-softClip signal is so hot that the clipper is in full saturation for ordinary settings. The [.measure] output-level test shows the Kick and ALL seven toms peak at EXACTLY 0.5012 (= -6 dB master gain applied to a 1.0 per-voice peak) at BOTH velocity 1.0 AND velocity 0.5 — velocity has zero effect on peak. The diagnose_orch test, rendering a single voice at level=0.10, reports peak=1.0000 with RMS=0.6695 and 7588 samples >= 0.99 (a near-square wave) in baseline, and still 96-111 clipped samples even in the 'body-only' (all layers off) scenario. softClip(x)=1.0 requires x>=3.0, so shaped*env*0.10 >= 3.0 means the body chain is producing |shaped*env| ~ 30, i.e. >30x too hot before the level knob even applies.
finding.why_wrong: A drum voice's loudness and attack transient ARE its dynamics; clipping every voice to its ceiling flattens velocity response (vel 0.5 == vel 1.0 peak) and turns distinct bodies into the same saturated square-ish waveform, which is exactly the 'presets sound the same and boring' symptom. softClip should be a rarely-touched safety net operating in its linear region for normal hits (per the code's own noteOn comment, lines 211-216: 'bounds the final voice output to [-1,1] for the audio bus'), not the primary gain-staging element. Because the upstream chain (1/sqrt(N) normalized body + shell/secondary bank summed at full amplitude + click + drive) is ~30x too hot, neither the per-pad Level nor MIDI velocity (via env) can pull the signal back into the clipper's linear zone — they are swamped. The clip nonlinearity, identical for every voice, homogenizes timbre.
finding.sound_impact: Direct cause of the reported sameness/boring: all kick/tom presets converge to an identical -6 dBFS clipped peak regardless of size/decay/material, velocity dynamics collapse (0.5 == 1.0), and the shared tanh-shaped saturation imposes one timbral signature on every voice. The Level knob cannot rescue it (level=0.10 still clips).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading the code AND running the cited tests.

CODE (drum_voice.h): Lines 769, 782, 885 all read `out[...] = Krate::DSP::softClip(shaped * env * level)` — confirmed in the fast-path default-off lane (769), fast-path UN lane (782), and slow/FeedbackExciter path (885). softClip (dsp_utils.h:105-112) is a tanh approximation that hard-saturates to +/-1.0 for |x|>=3.0. The design comment at lines 211-216 explicitly states this downstream softClip exists only to "bound the final voice output to [-1,1] for the audio bus" — i.e. it is intended as a safety net, not the primary gain-staging element. Velocity reaches the path via ampEnvelope_.setVelocity (line 327) with velocityScaling(true), so peakLevel_ = velocity (adsr_envelope.h:443); env scales linearly with velocity. (Note: velocityGain_ set at 329 is never read in the path, but velocity still scales via env and the exciter trigger.) So in the clipper's linear region velocity WOULD scale the peak; it only fails to when the upstream signal is far past saturation.

EMPIRICAL (ran membrum_tests.exe, current Release build): 
- [.measure] vel=1.0: Kick + all 7 toms peak at EXACTLY 0.5012 (-6.00 dBFS = master -6 dB applied to a per-voice peak of exactly 1.0 -> softClip is fully saturated).
- [.measure] vel=0.5: Kick=0.5012 and Floor Tom Lo=0.5012 — IDENTICAL peak to vel 1.0. Velocity has zero effect on the peak for these voices (confirming the dynamics-collapse claim). (Snare/High Tom/Hat do drop, so the collapse is not universal.)
- [diagnose_orch] at level=0.10: baseline peak=1.0000, rms=0.6695, 7588/24000 samples (~32%) at >=0.99 (near-square). body-only (all layers off) still peak=1.0000 with 111 samples>=0.99. softClip=1.0 requires input>=3.0, and at level=0.10 that means shaped*env >= 30, i.e. the body chain is ~30x too hot before the level knob — exactly as claimed.

So the described behaviour is present and is genuinely wrong: the [-1,1] safety clip is operating as the dominant gain stage in full saturation for ordinary settings, pinning peaks at the rail and erasing per-hit velocity dynamics. This directly supports the user's "presets sound the same/boring" symptom.

Severity: HIGH rather than critical. It is not a crash/RT-safety/NaN issue, and timbre is not *fully* homogenized — RMS still varies across toms (0.20-0.30) and with velocity, and some pads (snare/hat) are not pinned — so the "imposes one identical timbre on every voice" wording is somewhat overstated. But the verified peak-flattening and velocity-collapse (0.5==1.0) on the kick and all toms is a real, audible dynamics/correctness defect affecting the core voices and the primary reported symptom.

VERIFIED by reading code and RUNNING the cited tests.

So the design intent confirms softClip should sit in its linear region for normal hits.

The loud pitched bodies (kick + all toms) converge to an identical -6 dBFS clipped peak regardless of size/decay/material, and their peak velocity dynamics collapse (vel 0.5 == vel 1.0).
