But the core claim (per-voice softClip is running in full saturation as the de-facto gain stage, flattening peak dynamics and timbre on the dominant kick/tom voices) is correct and well-evidenced.

finding.title: Level knob and velocity cannot reduce voice output below clipping because upstream gain is ~30x too hot
finding.problem: `level_` enters only as the final multiplier `shaped * env * level` before softClip. diagnose_orch renders at level=0.10 and the voice still peaks at 1.0 (saturated). For the per-pad Level (default 0.8, range [0,1] linear) to have audible headroom control, shaped*env at level=1.0 should sit near unity; instead shaped*env reaches ~30 (since softClip saturates at input>=3 and level=0.10 still saturates). The secondary/shell bank is the dominant contributor: diagnose_orch shows baseline RMS 0.6695 collapsing to 0.196 when coupling/secondary is disabled (no-coupling, no-secondary, body-only all ~0.196). The shell bank output is added to bodyScratch (line 731) with only 1/sqrt(N) normalization and no headroom budget against the primary body.
finding.why_wrong: A linear [0,1] Level parameter must be able to set the voice somewhere below full scale; here it is post-multiplied into a signal already 30x over the clip threshold, so the realizable output range is compressed into the top of the softClip curve. Two parallel modal banks (primary + secondary) each normalized to ~unity peak and then summed will routinely exceed unity; the design lacks a mix/gain stage to keep their sum bounded before the safety clip.
finding.sound_impact: Turning Level down barely changes loudness until it is very low; presets that differ only in Level/velocity sound identical. Reinforces the sameness symptom because the headroom control that would differentiate hits is defeated.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading the code AND running the actual diagnostic binary.

CODE CONFIRMS the mechanism:
- `level` is the final multiplier applied AFTER all shaping: `softClip(shaped * env * level)` at drum_voice.h:769, 782, 446 (slow path 885). It is a linear normalized [0,1] value passed straight through from the VST boundary (voice_pool.cpp:584 `cfg.level = normalizedValue`, :724 `v.setLevel(cfg.level)`, default level_=0.8 at drum_voice.h:1313).
- softClip (dsp_utils.h:105-112) is a tanh approximation that hard-saturates to +/-1.0 at |input|>=3.0 and compresses heavily below that. So Level only has usable headroom if shaped*env at level=1.0 sits near unity.
- Body bank is normalized by 1/sqrt(N) with the legacy clip removed (drum_voice.h:248-249); the secondary/shell bank (24 modes) is independently normalized by 1/sqrt(ns) (:255-256) and then added to bodyScratch at UNITY (:731 `bodyScratch[i] += secOut[i]`) with no joint mix/headroom budget. Two parallel modal banks summed at unity routinely exceed the softClip threshold — confirmed.

DIAGNOSTIC OUTPUT (ran membrum_tests.exe [diagnose_orch], level=0.10, vel=1.0) matches the finding's numbers exactly:
- baseline: peak=1.0000 rms=0.6695, 7588/24000 samples >=0.99 (32% pervasively clipped)
- no-secondary / no-coupling: rms=0.1960, still peak=1.0000 with clipping
- body-only: rms=0.2005, peak=1.0000, 111 samples clipping
Because even body-only at level=0.10 saturates to peak 1.0, shaped*env reaches >=30 transiently — the "~30x too hot" magnitude is real. With the secondary bank on, the signal is pervasively (32%) pinned at the softClip ceiling, which is a genuine dynamics-flattening / timbre-homogenizing mechanism consistent with the user's "presets sound the same/boring" symptom. The Level control has essentially no usable range until very low values.

CORRECTNESS VERDICT: The described behaviour is present and is wrong. A linear [0,1] Level used as a post-saturation multiplier on a signal already far above the clip threshold cannot set the voice below full scale across most of its range, and the dual-bank sum lacks any pre-clip headroom/mix stage.

NUANCES (do not invalidate the finding): (1) The claim "velocity cannot reduce voice output below clipping" is overstated — the diagnostic runs at velocity=1.0 (max), and velocity does scale the exciter input (exciter_bank.h:50-54), so lower velocity does reduce the input. The robust part of the claim is the Level control, not velocity. (2) The headline attributes the problem chiefly to the secondary/shell bank, but body-only also clips at level=0.10, so the over-hot gain staging is fundamental to the per-voice path; the secondary bank turns a transient clip into pervasive clipping rather than being the sole cause. Severity adjusted to high (defeats a primary mix control and homogenizes output) rather than critical, since there is no RT-safety/NaN/crash violation — the softClip keeps output bounded in [-1,1].

Confirmed by reading drum_voice.h and running the cited diagnostic.

MECHANISM (code-verified):
- `level_` (default 0.8, stored as raw normalized linear gain at voice_pool.cpp:584, displayed as 20*log10(norm) dB) enters ONLY as the final post-multiplier in the post-chain: `out = softClip(shaped * env * level)` at lines 769, 782 (fast path) and 885 (slow path). There is no pre-scale or compensating attenuation upstream.
- softClip (dsp_utils.h:105-112) hard-saturates at input >= 3.0.
- Both modal banks are normalized only by 1/sqrt(N) (primary line 248, secondary line 256) and the secondary/shell output is added DIRECTLY into bodyScratch (line 731) with NO mix coefficient or headroom budget against the primary body. No voice-level mix/gain stage bounds the bank sum before the safety clip.

EMPIRICAL (ran the existing [diagnose_orch] test against the built membrum_tests.exe, level=0.10, velocity=1.0):
- baseline: peak=1.0000 rms=0.6695, 7588/24000 samples >= 0.99 (CLIPPING)
- no-secondary: rms=0.1960, only 96 samples clipping
- no-coupling: rms=0.1960, 96 samples
- body-only: rms=0.2005, 111 samples
These match the finding's quoted figures exactly. Since softClip saturates at input>=3 and level=0.10 still pins peak at 1.0 with 7588 clipped samples, shaped*env must reach ~30 — i.e. ~30x over the clip threshold. Removing the secondary/shell bank collapses RMS from 0.67 to ~0.20 and clipping from 7588 to ~96 samples, proving the shell bank is the dominant over-hot contributor exactly as claimed.

AUDIBILITY: A linear [0,1] Level control feeding a signal ~30x over the clip ceiling means turning Level down barely reduces peak loudness until it goes very low; the realizable range is compressed into the top of the softClip curve. Hits that differ only in Level/velocity collapse toward the same clipped waveform, which directly defeats the per-hit headroom/dynamics differentiation and reinforces the reported "presets sound the same/boring" symptom. This is clearly audible and material, so severity high (not the top 'critical' since it does not break/crash, but it materially flattens dynamics across the product). Note: velocity also has upstream entry points (exciter trigger amplitude line 278, amp env line 327), so the velocity sub-claim is weaker than the Level claim, but the central Level-headroom defect is fully confirmed.

finding.title: Fast vs slow path divergence: the secondary (shell) coupling bank is processed only in processBlockFast, silently dropped in processBlockSlow
finding.line: 691-734 (present in fast path; absent in processBlockSlow 813-890 and process() 388-447)
finding.problem: processBlockFast handles the Phase 8D head<->shell coupling (`if (effectiveCoupling_ > 0.0f)` at line 691: builds secondary excitation, runs secondaryBank_.processBlock, mixes secOut into bodyScratch). processBlockSlow (the FeedbackExciter path, selected by useSlowPath=feedbackExciter at line 500) contains NO secondaryBank_ / effectiveCoupling_ handling at all, and neither does the single-sample process(). Coupling/secondary setup at noteOn (lines 304-308) is independent of exciter type, so a pad configured with FeedbackExciter + secondaryEnabled + couplingStrength>0 will have its entire shell bank omitted from the audio.
finding.why_wrong: The two block paths are documented (lines 449-468) as 'supposed to be perceptually equivalent'; only FeedbackExciter semantics justify the slow path, not a different signal topology. Dropping a whole resonator bank on one path is a correctness divergence and means the Coupling Strength / Secondary parameters do not reach the DSP for FeedbackExciter voices.
finding.sound_impact: FeedbackExciter pads with shell coupling enabled lose the shell character entirely (thinner, different timbre) vs the same patch on a non-feedback exciter; a documented parameter (coupling/secondary) audibly fails to take effect on that path.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly in plugins/membrum/src/dsp/drum_voice.h.

Path selection (line 500): `const bool useSlowPath = feedbackExciter;` where feedbackExciter is `exciterBank_.getCurrentType() == ExciterType::Feedback` (line 490-491). So a FeedbackExciter voice always takes processBlockSlow.

Fast path (processBlockFast, lines 691-734): contains the entire Phase 8D shell coupling — gated by `if (effectiveCoupling_ > 0.0f)`, builds secExc = bodyScratch * effectiveCoupling_, runs secondaryBank_.processBlock, applies secondaryGainCompensation_, and mixes secOut into bodyScratch (passive additive).

Slow path (processBlockSlow, lines 813-890): I read the full body. There is NO reference to secondaryBank_ or effectiveCoupling_ anywhere in its loop — the inner per-sample loop goes exciter -> body.processSampleNoSmooth -> noise/click -> unnatural -> tone shaper -> env, with no shell stage. Likewise process() (lines 388-447) has no secondary handling.

A grep for `effectiveCoupling_|secondaryBank_` confirms the only PROCESSING use is line 691/698/699 (fast path). All other references are prepare()/setOutputGain (94, 254-257), reset (301, 363), and noteOn setup (303-307). Line 1292/1333 are member declarations.

Crucially, the secondary-bank setup at noteOn (lines 290-308) is independent of exciter type — it only checks `secondaryEnabled_ >= 0.5f && couplingStrength_ > 0.0f` and sets effectiveCoupling_ via stabilityClampedCoupling(). So a pad configured with Feedback exciter + secondaryEnabled + couplingStrength>0 configures the shell bank at noteOn, then routes to the slow path which never processes it. The shell character is silently dropped, and the Coupling Strength / Secondary Enabled parameters fail to reach the DSP for that exciter type. This is a genuine fast/slow topology divergence, contradicting the documented (lines 449-468) intent that the two paths differ only in FeedbackExciter per-sample feedback semantics.

Severity: medium rather than high. It is a real correctness divergence and a parameter-doesn't-take-effect bug, but it is conditional on a specific combination (Feedback exciter AND secondary enabled AND coupling > 0), which is not the default (secondary defaults disabled). It degrades/thins timbre and silences two parameters on that path rather than producing instability, NaN/Inf, or a crash. It is also plausibly a contributor to the 'presets sound the same' symptom only for the subset of presets using FeedbackExciter with shell coupling.

Verified by reading drum_voice.h directly. The Phase 8D shell-coupling block (`if (effectiveCoupling_ > 0.0f)`, lines 691-734) — which builds secondary excitation = bodyScratch*effectiveCoupling, runs secondaryBank_.processBlock, applies secondaryGainCompensation_, and mixes secOut into bodyScratch — exists ONLY in processBlockFast. processBlockSlow (813-890) has zero references to secondaryBank_ / effectiveCoupling_; its inner loop goes body.processSampleNoSmooth -> noise -> modeInject -> nonlinearCoupling -> toneShaper -> env, with no shell stage. The single-sample process() (388-447) likewise has no secondary handling. processBlock selects useSlowPath = feedbackExciter (line 500), so any voice whose active exciter is ExciterType::Feedback takes the slow path. noteOn (lines 301-308) configures the secondary bank purely on secondaryEnabled_>=0.5 && couplingStrength_>0, independent of exciter type, and effectiveCoupling_ is set there. These three are independent per-pad params (setExciterType / setSecondaryEnabled / setCouplingStrength, all populated from cfg in voice_pool.cpp:718/752-753 and persisted in state_codec). So a pad configured Feedback + secondaryEnabled + coupling>0 is fully reachable and will silently drop its entire shell bank, making the Coupling Strength / Secondary parameters no-ops on that path — a genuine signal-topology divergence between two paths documented (449-468) as perceptually equivalent. Audibility: in the affected configuration the shell bank is a whole resonator bank contributing real timbre, so its removal is clearly audible (thinner sound, two dead documented params). However, none of the factory default-kit templates use ExciterType::Feedback (default_kit.h uses Impulse/NoiseBurst/Mallet) and secondaryEnabled defaults to 0, so this does NOT drive the user-reported "presets all sound the same/boring" symptom — it only bites a deliberate Feedback+coupling user combo. Real, audible, but conditional and not the sameness culprit -> medium.

finding.title: bodyGainCompensation_ / secondaryGainCompensation_ are dead: hard-coded to 1.0, contradicting the documented sum*0.707 loudness restoration
finding.problem: In noteOn both compensation factors are unconditionally set to 1.0f (lines 251-252). The member doc (lines 1356-1361) states they are 'Set in noteOn() from sum(amplitudes) * 0.707 (target = old saturated peak)', and the apply sites (676-681, 701-706) are guarded by `!= 1.0f`, so they are always skipped. The Phase 11 post-bank loudness compensation described throughout the noteOn comments never runs; body output is purely 1/sqrt(N) with no per-preset/per-body loudness normalization.
finding.why_wrong: Either the comments are stale or the feature was dropped. With compensation inert, bodies with very different amplitude sums get only the count-based 1/sqrt(N) norm, so relative loudness between body models is not what the design intended. Combined with finding #1, the absence of any calibrated gain staging is why the chain runs straight into the safety clip.
finding.sound_impact: No active flattening, but the intended per-body loudness shaping is absent; contributes to the lack of gain-staging that lets the softClip dominate. Misleading for anyone tuning the chain.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly in plugins/membrum/src/dsp/drum_voice.h. Lines 251-252: noteOn() unconditionally sets bodyGainCompensation_ = 1.0f and secondaryGainCompensation_ = 1.0f, with no body-model-dependent or sum(amplitudes)*0.707 assignment anywhere. Apply sites (676-681 body, 701-706 shell) are both guarded by `if (... != 1.0f)`; since the members are always exactly 1.0f (and 1.0f is exactly representable, so the guard never spuriously passes), the multiply loops never run. Member doc (1356-1361) claims they are "Set in noteOn() from sum(amplitudes) * 0.707 (target = old saturated peak)", which contradicts the code. A repo-wide grep shows these members are referenced only in drum_voice.h (plus the audit doc itself) and are never assigned anything other than 1.0f; the slow path doesn't touch them. So the described behaviour is exactly as the finding states: the Phase 11 post-bank loudness compensation is dead/inert code, body output is purely 1/sqrt(N) with no per-body loudness restoration, and the comments are stale relative to the implementation.

On the CORRECTNESS lens: the described code behaviour is genuinely present. However it is not a functional malfunction — the code is internally consistent (1.0f set, != 1.0f guard correctly skips), there is no NaN/Inf, no RT-safety issue, no wrong arithmetic being applied. It is a documentation-vs-implementation mismatch plus a dropped/inert tuning feature. The audible consequence (no intended per-body loudness shaping, only count-based normalization) is real but is a design-intent gap, consistent with the finding's own "No active flattening" note. Hence low severity: misleading stale docs and an absent calibration hook, not an active bug.

VERIFIED the code at drum_voice.h. Lines 251-252: bodyGainCompensation_ and secondaryGainCompensation_ are unconditionally set to 1.0f in noteOn(). A grep for both members across plugins/membrum/src shows the ONLY writes are these two 1.0f assignments plus the =1.0f default initializers (lines 1360-1361); no setter or path ever assigns the documented sum(amplitudes)*0.707 value. The apply sites (676-681, 701-706) are guarded by `!= 1.0f`, so with the values pinned to 1.0f the compensation loops are dead and never execute. The member doc (1356-1361) does state they should be set from sum(amplitudes)*0.707, which contradicts the hard-coded 1.0f. So the finding's factual core is correct: the factors are dead and the documentation is stale.

HOWEVER, the surrounding noteOn comment (lines 233-249) is decisive for interpreting this. It explicitly documents the CURRENT, INTENTIONAL design: the non-feedback path uses 1/sqrt(N) energy-equalisation normalisation which is deliberately "INDEPENDENT of per-mode amplitudes, so parameters that boost individual modes (decaySkew, modeScatter, future per-mode weights) preserve their relative effect rather than being self-cancelled by the normalisation." An amplitude-sum-based compensation (the sum(amplitudes)*0.707 the stale member doc describes) would re-introduce exactly the self-cancellation the design chose to avoid. So this is not a dropped/forgotten feature regression — it is a superseded approach whose member comment was never updated. The dead branch is a stale-doc + dead-code cleanliness issue, not a DSP correctness bug.

AUDIBILITY: zero audible impact. The active path (1/sqrt(N), set at lines 248/255-256) is the designed behavior and runs unconditionally. The inert factor would only ever multiply by 1.0 (skipped), so removing/fixing it changes nothing the user hears. The finding itself concedes "No active flattening." It does NOT contribute to the presets-sound-the-same symptom: count-based normalization preserves per-mode parameter differences (the opposite of homogenizing), and per-body loudness differences (Membrane ~0.36 vs Bell ~1.0 peak per the comment) actually remain intact rather than being normalized away. The finding is real as a code-hygiene/documentation observation (dead branches + contradictory comment), hence not "not-a-bug," but its audible severity is low and its causal framing (lost gain-staging that lets softClip dominate / contributes to sameness) is overstated.

finding.title: velocityGain_ is dead code with a misleading comment claiming velocity is applied at the output
finding.problem: velocityGain_ is assigned `clamp(velocity,0,1)` at line 329 and reset at 378 but is never read in any process path (process/processBlockFast/processBlockSlow all use `shaped * env * level_`, no velocityGain_). The member comment (1315-1317) says 'velocity scaling applied at the output (previously handled by the amp envelope peakLevel_)', but velocity is in fact STILL applied by the amp envelope (setVelocityScaling(true) at line 128; peakLevel_=velocity).
finding.why_wrong: The comment describes a refactor that did not happen; velocity reaches output only via the ADSR's peakLevel, not via velocityGain_. Dead member + wrong comment invites a future maintainer to double-apply velocity or to assume the env is velocity-independent.
finding.sound_impact: No direct audible effect today (velocity still gets through via env), but the stale member is a latent double-scaling hazard if someone wires it in.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly against the code. `velocityGain_` appears at exactly three sites in plugins/membrum/src/dsp/drum_voice.h: declaration/comment at 1317 (default 1.0f), write at 329 (`velocityGain_ = std::clamp(velocity,0,1)` in noteOn) and write at 378 (reset to 0 in resetForKitSwitch). A repo-wide grep across plugins/membrum/src finds NO read of the member anywhere. All three output sites multiply only `shaped * env * level(_)`: process() at 446, processBlockFast at 769/782, processBlockSlow at 885 — none reference velocityGain_. So the member is genuinely dead code.

The comment at 1315-1317 says velocity scaling is "applied at the output (previously handled by the amp envelope's peakLevel_)", implying the env no longer applies velocity. That is false: ctor line 128 calls `ampEnvelope_.setVelocityScaling(true)`, noteOn line 327 calls `setVelocity(velocity)`, and the ADSR (dsp/include/krate/dsp/primitives/adsr_envelope.h line 443) sets `peakLevel_ = velocityScalingEnabled_ ? velocity_ : 1.0f`, with peakLevel_ scaling the entire envelope output (lines 280/285/295/298/303-312). So velocity still reaches the output exclusively through the amp envelope's peakLevel — exactly the path the comment claims was superseded. The described refactor did not happen.

No current audible effect (velocity still gets through via the env), so this is not an audible bug and not a dynamics-flattening cause for the "presets sound the same" symptom. It is a latent double-scaling hazard plus a misleading comment — correctly a low-severity correctness/maintainability finding, not the originally implied higher tier.

Confirmed by reading drum_voice.h. velocityGain_ is written at line 329 (noteOn: `velocityGain_ = std::clamp(velocity, 0.0f, 1.0f)`), reset at line 378 (resetForKitSwitch: `velocityGain_ = 0.0f`), and declared at line 1317 (`float velocityGain_ = 1.0f`). A whole-file and whole-src Grep shows exactly these three occurrences and NO read site. All three output paths use `softClip(shaped * env * level/level_)` with no velocityGain_ factor: process() line 446, processBlockFast lines 769/782, processBlockSlow line 885. So velocityGain_ is genuinely dead.

The comment at 1315-1317 ("velocity scaling applied at the output (previously handled by the amp envelope's peakLevel_)") is misleading: velocity is in fact STILL applied via the amp envelope — prepare() calls setVelocityScaling(true) (line 128) and noteOn calls ampEnvelope_.setVelocity(velocity) (line 327). So velocity reaches the output through the ADSR's velocity scaling, not through velocityGain_. The described refactor (move velocity from env peakLevel to an output multiply) did not happen.

AUDIBILITY lens: velocity still gets through correctly via the amp envelope, so there is no audible effect today and this does NOT contribute to the presets-sound-the-same symptom. The bug is purely a latent maintainability/double-scaling hazard (a future maintainer could wire velocityGain_ into the output multiply and double-apply velocity). Real bug, but zero current audible impact -> corrected_severity = low.

finding.title: Fast same-pad retrigger/re-steal hard-cuts the voice when the shadow slot is already fading (click, not click-free)
finding.problem: There is exactly ONE shadow (fast-release) slot per voice index. beginFastRelease() is idempotent: if releasingMeta_[slot].state == FastReleasing it returns immediately without snapshotting (lines 933-934). On a fast retrigger or re-steal that targets the SAME slot while its shadow is still fading, the Steal-event handler calls beginFastRelease(slot) (no-op because shadow is busy), sets meta_[slot].state=Free, and then the following NoteOn event calls applyPadConfigToSlot(slot)+VP_VOICES[slot].noteOn() directly on the still-ringing main voice. Because the swap never happened, the currently-sounding main voice is overwritten with a fresh attack with NO crossfade.
finding.why_wrong: The whole two-array design (voice_pool.h top comment, FR-124/FR-126/FR-127) exists to guarantee click-free steals/chokes by fading the outgoing voice through the shadow. When the shadow is occupied the outgoing voice is hard-truncated, producing a sample-to-sample discontinuity. For percussion this is exactly the case that occurs on fast rolls on a single pad and on dense choke-group patterns (hi-hat chokes), i.e. precisely when click-freeness matters most. The single-fade-per-slot reservation documented in the header is acknowledged but the overwrite path silently degrades to a hard cut instead of, e.g., extending the fade or zeroing the main voice.
finding.sound_impact: Audible clicks/pops on fast same-pad retriggers and rapid choke sequences. The existing tests (test_fast_release_double_steal.cpp, test_choke_click_free.cpp) only cover the idle-shadow case and the test comment at lines 129-134 explicitly avoids asserting a click bound when the same slot is re-hit, so the defect is untested. Not a sameness contributor, but a stability/click correctness bug.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. The defect chain is present exactly as described:

1. Single shadow per slot: voice_pool.h declares `releasingVoices_[16]` (one shadow per voice index); the header comment (lines 30-35) documents the deliberate "per-slot single-fade reservation" and that `beginFastRelease` rejects re-entry on a slot already FastReleasing.

2. Idempotent no-op (voice_pool.cpp:933-934): `beginFastRelease` returns immediately, WITHOUT performing the `std::swap(VP_RVOICES[slot], VP_VOICES[slot])` (line 963), when `releasingMeta_[slot].state == FastReleasing`. So the still-ringing main voice is NOT moved into the shadow.

3. Steal handler (lines 200-214): calls `beginFastRelease(ev.voiceIndex)` then unconditionally sets `meta_[slot].state = Free`. The following NoteOn event handler (lines 216-222) calls `applyPadConfigToSlot(slot)` + `VP_VOICES[slot].noteOn(clampedVel)` directly on that slot. I read `applyPadConfigToSlot` (lines 709-749) and confirmed it only sets parameters; `DrumVoice::noteOn` (drum_voice.h:135+) restarts the amp-envelope attack and re-triggers the exciter in place. Neither performs any crossfade. So when the swap was skipped, the currently-sounding main voice is overwritten by a fresh attack with no fade -> sample-to-sample discontinuity (click).

4. The same hazard exists on the choke path: `chokeGroup` mute calls `beginFastRelease(slot)` (line 1088) for active voices and again at lines 1106-1110 for already-FastReleasing slots (explicitly relying on idempotency), so a same-slot re-choke degrades to the same no-fade overwrite via a subsequent note-on.

5. Untested: test_fast_release_double_steal.cpp lines 129-134 explicitly state they do NOT assert a click bound on the full output for the re-steal case, only verifying the already-releasing shadow slot's monotonic gain. So the overwrite-with-no-crossfade path on the main voice is uncovered.

The claimed sound impact (audible click on fast same-pad retrigger / dense choke) is correct: the window is the ~5 ms shadow fade, but the Quietest stealing policy naturally re-targets the just-restarted slot (currentLevel ~ 0 at re-noteOn, see selectQuietestActiveSlot lines 906-922), which is precisely the dense-roll / hat-choke scenario.

Severity: medium rather than high. It is a real correctness/click bug that defeats the click-free guarantee the two-array design exists for, but it is not a crash, not NaN/Inf, not a real-time-safety violation, and not a contributor to the preset-sameness symptom. It only manifests in the bounded same-slot-within-5ms re-target case and produces a click, not a hang or instability.

Verified against the actual code. beginFastRelease() (voice_pool.cpp:925-1001) is idempotent: lines 933-934 return early when releasingMeta_[slot].state == FastReleasing, WITHOUT performing the std::swap (line 963) that moves the ringing main voice into the shadow and WITHOUT calling fastReleaseDamp() (line 978). The Steal handler (lines 200-214) then unconditionally sets meta_[slot].state = Free, and the immediately-following NoteOn event (lines 216-247) calls applyPadConfigToSlot(slot) + VP_VOICES[slot].noteOn() on the still-ringing main voice. So when a Steal+NoteOn pair targets the SAME slot while its shadow is already fading, the crossfade is skipped and the main voice is overwritten with a fresh attack — a hard cut, exactly as the finding states.

The mechanism that produces a same-slot Steal+NoteOn is confirmed in voice_allocator.h: noteOn() (line 228) calls findVoicePlayingNote() and, if the note is still playing, dispatches to retriggerNote() (line 820) which pushes Steal(firstVoice) then NoteOn(firstVoice) on the SAME index. The allocator tracks only Idle/Active; it has no knowledge of the VoicePool's separate shadow (releasingMeta_) state, so it will happily re-target a slot whose shadow is still FastReleasing. This is the fast same-pad retrigger case. The test (test_fast_release_double_steal.cpp:127-134) explicitly declines to assert a click bound on this exact path, so the defect is untested. All structural claims are accurate.

AUDIBILITY: The collision requires retriggering the SAME pad within the fast-release window (~5 ms; fastReleaseK_ from 0.005*sampleRate, line 1012), i.e. a >~200 Hz same-pad retrigger rate. Normal drum rolls (20-100 Hz) do not hit this; it is reachable only with programmed bursts, stacked flam notes on one tick, or duplicate MIDI events. When triggered it truncates a full-amplitude ringing modal body (lines 971-977 confirm the body sustains at unity under Option-D), so the discontinuity is a genuinely audible click/pop. So: real bug, real audible click, but a narrow edge-case timing window and not the common case. Crucially it does NOT contribute to the user's "presets sound the same and boring" symptom (the finding itself says it is not a sameness contributor). No crash, no NaN, no allocation. Given the narrow trigger window and that it does not touch the reported symptom, corrected_severity=low.

finding.title: Per-voice -60 dBFS auto-release threshold is measured on post-level output, so low-level pads can be retired prematurely
finding.problem: The pool retires a voice (calls VP_VOICES[slot].noteOff(), which fires ModalResonatorBank::damp()) when the per-block peak of the voice's FINAL output (softClip(shaped*env*level_)) drops below kSilenceThreshold = 1e-3 (-60 dBFS). Because the measurement includes the per-pad level_ multiplier, a pad whose level knob is set very low has its peak scaled down before the comparison.
finding.why_wrong: Voice lifetime/retirement should track the body's intrinsic decay, not the user gain stage. A pad with low level (or a soft velocity hit) can cross -60 dBFS almost immediately and be force-damped, truncating its natural tail, whereas the same pad at high level rings out fully. The threshold therefore couples perceived sustain to the level/velocity setting rather than to the decay/material parameters.
finding.sound_impact: Quiet pads or soft hits lose their tail and get a uniform accelerated damp; the decay no longer scales with the decay/material knobs at low levels. A mild dynamics-flattening / parameter-coupling effect that can make soft hits across presets behave similarly (cut short), contributing slightly to the 'boring/same' impression for low-level material.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED the mechanism is present. voice_pool.cpp:345-366 (mono) and 448-484 (multi-bus): the pool renders the voice into `scratch`, computes per-block `peak = max|scratch[i]|`, and when `peak < kSilenceThreshold (1e-3, -60 dBFS)` while Active, calls VP_VOICES[slot].noteOff(). The measured `scratch[i]` is the voice's FINAL output: drum_voice.h:446/769/782/885 all write `softClip(shaped * env * level_)`. `level_` is the raw normalized [0,1] value used directly as a LINEAR gain (voice_pool.cpp:584 `cfg.level = normalizedValue`, no denorm; controller.cpp:2378 formats it as 20*log10(level) dB), and velocity scaling also feeds the amp env. So the -60 dBFS comparison is indeed taken on the post-level/post-velocity output, and noteOff() calls bodyBank_.damp(0.993) (drum_voice.h:343) which accelerates the modal decay. Therefore the instant at which the body tail is force-damped IS coupled to the level knob and velocity, exactly as the finding states. The amp envelope sustain is 1.0 (drum_voice.h:126), confirming the voice does not self-decay via the envelope during a held note, so for that case retirement genuinely depends on output crossing the threshold.

HOWEVER the finding overstates the impact and mischaracterizes the design, so severity is LOW not as implied:
1. The DOMINANT, intended retirement path is level-INDEPENDENT: drum_voice.h:527 retires when `bankSilent && envFinished`, where bankSilent comes from ModalResonatorBank::flushSilentModes() (modal_resonator_bank.h:378-384) testing per-mode ENERGY against its own 1e-12 threshold on the bank's INTERNAL state — i.e. before the level_ multiplier. This path tracks the body's intrinsic decay (decay/material knobs) precisely as the finding argues it should.
2. The pool's peak<1e-3 path is explicitly documented (voice_pool.cpp:320-338 and drum_voice.h:532-538) as a SAFETY NET for the opposite failure mode — voices whose layered output SETTLES ABOVE 1e-3 (sustained noise/click/filter ringing) and would otherwise ring forever (the infinite-ring regression). It is not the normal tail-decay path.
3. Whatever tail gets truncated is, by construction, already below -60 dBFS IN THE FINAL OUTPUT, so it is sub-audible. The level coupling only shifts the damp-trigger within the sub-(-60 dBFS) region of the output.

Net: the code behaviour described is genuinely present and is a minor correctness wart (a lifetime trigger arguably belongs on intrinsic body state, not the post-gain output), but it is a weak/low-severity contributor and not a meaningful cause of the 'presets sound the same/boring' symptom, since audible decay is governed by the level-independent modal-flush path.

VERIFIED THE MECHANISM — the code matches the finding's description. voice_pool.cpp:362-365 (mono) and 480-483 (multibus) compute the per-block peak of the voice's FINAL output and fire VP_VOICES[slot].noteOff() when peak < kSilenceThreshold = 1e-3 (-60 dBFS). The measured signal is drum_voice.h:446 (and 769/782/885 in the block paths): softClip(shaped * env * level_). voice_pool.cpp:584 sets cfg.level = normalizedValue directly (raw [0,1] LINEAR gain, no dB mapping), and that feeds level_ via setLevel (line 724 -> drum_voice.h:932/1313). So a pad with a low level knob has its absolute peak scaled down ~20·log10(level) dB before the fixed -60 dBFS comparison, retiring the voice earlier in the body's *intrinsic* decay. noteOff() then calls bodyBank.damp(kNoteOffDampScale=0.997) and ampEnvelope_.gate(false), accelerating decay. Voice lifetime is therefore coupled to the user level stage, not purely to decay/material — the finding is technically correct.

AUDIBILITY (the lens) is weak, hence low severity, not higher:
1) The retirement fires at an ABSOLUTE floor of -60 dBFS. The portion of the natural tail that gets cut for a low-level pad lies entirely BELOW -60 dBFS absolute (e.g. for level=0.1 the lost tail spans roughly -60 to -80 dBFS absolute) — at or under the audible floor in any realistic mix.
2) The accelerator is damp(0.997): a one-time radius multiply of ~-0.026 dB on the pole, not a hard truncation; combined with the amp-env release it produces a gradual fade, not an audible chop.
3) Factory presets default to level=0.8 (default_kit.h, ~-2 dB), where the coupling shifts the retirement point by only ~2 dB — negligible. The effect is only meaningful for pads deliberately set very low (0.1-0.2), which is atypical.
4) The 'soft velocity' half of the claim is partly wrong: velocity does NOT enter level_ (velocityGain_ at drum_voice.h:1317 is stored but never multiplied into the output expression). Velocity enters via ampEnvelope_.setVelocity (line 327), i.e. through env, so the dominant coupling is the static level knob, not velocity.

Net: a genuine but minor correctness coupling between the user gain stage and voice retirement. Lost material is sub -60 dBFS absolute and faded, defaults are unaffected, so it contributes negligibly to the 'boring/same' symptom. Corrected severity: low.

finding.title: Entire voice/pool path is mono, summed identically to L and R with no per-pad pan or decorrelation
finding.problem: DrumVoice is mono (no outR/stereo/width fields). The pool writes scratch into both outL and outR equally (outL[i]+=scratch[i]; outR[i]+=scratch[i]) for every voice and every aux bus. scratchR_ is allocated but never used. PadConfig has no pan field and the pool applies no pan law or stereo decorrelation.
finding.why_wrong: A polyphonic drum instrument with 32 pads collapses to dead-center mono regardless of which pads are struck. There is no spatial differentiation between kick/snare/hat/toms, and no pan parameter reaches the DSP because none exists. Real kits and competing instruments place pads across the stereo field.
finding.sound_impact: All presets share an identical, centered, mono image; nothing in the mix differentiates pads spatially. This directly reinforces the user-reported 'presets mostly sound the same / boring': two presets with different pad layouts still produce the same flat centered stereo picture.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly against the code.

1) DrumVoice is mono: drum_voice.h:471 `void processBlock(float* out, int numSamples)` — single mono output buffer; the two block paths (processBlockFast:562, processBlockSlow:813) also take one `out`. No outR/stereo/width fields.

2) Pool sums identically to L and R: at all three cited locations the scratch is added to both channels with no divergence — voice_pool.cpp:354-355 (`outL[i] += scratch[i]; outR[i] += scratch[i];`), 455-456 (multi-bus overload), 401-402 (fast-release shadow voices). Aux buses do the same (474-475: `auxL[bus][i] += scratch[i]; auxR[bus][i] += scratch[i];`).

3) scratchR_ allocated but never used: declared voice_pool.h:334, allocated voice_pool.cpp:73; a content search for `scratchR_` in voice_pool.cpp returns exactly 1 occurrence (the allocation). It is never read or written during processing. voice_pool.h:329-331 comment confirms intent: "Mono, because DrumVoice is mono. The right-channel scratch is reserved for a future stereo DrumVoice."

4) PadConfig has no pan field: read the entire struct (pad_config.h:166-296) and the full PadParamOffset enum (35-161). There is chokeGroup and outputBus but no pan/width/spread field, and no corresponding pad-param offset.

5) No spatialization anywhere downstream: grep across processor/ and voice_pool/ found no pan/decorrelation/width application. The master coupling stage takes a mono sum `(outL+outR)*0.5` (processor.cpp:787) and adds the same coupling and master gain to both channels (792-793, 811-812). L and R never diverge — output is mono duplicated to both channels.

So the described behaviour is exactly present: the entire voice/pool/master path is mono, every pad lands dead-center, no pan law or decorrelation exists, and scratchR_ is dead. The claim that this defeats spatial differentiation and plausibly reinforces the "presets sound the same" symptom is sound.

Severity adjusted from the implied higher rating to medium: this is a genuine and meaningful timbral/spatial limitation for a 32-pad kit, and reasonably contributes to the sameness symptom — but it is a documented missing-feature/design choice (mono by design, stereo deferred), not a logic bug producing incorrect output, a crash, or an RT-safety violation. The code does what it intends; it just intends too little for a polyphonic drum instrument.

Verified every mechanical claim against the actual code.

1. DrumVoice is mono: drum_voice.h:471 `void processBlock(float* out, int numSamples)` — single output buffer, dispatches to processBlockFast/Slow which also take one `float* out`. No outR/width/stereo fields.

2. Pool writes both channels identically: voice_pool.cpp:354-355, 401-402 (mono overload), 455-456, 506 (multibus overload): `outL[i] += scratch[i]; outR[i] += scratch[i];`. Aux buses too at 474-475: `auxL[bus][i] += scratch[i]; auxR[bus][i] += scratch[i];`. So output is hard dual-mono (L==R bit-identical).

3. scratchR_ allocated but unused: declared/allocated at voice_pool.cpp:73, and grep finds zero other references in the file. Confirmed dead.

4. PadConfig has no pan field: read full struct (pad_config.h:166-296). Fields cover material/size/decay/tone/unnatural/macros/noise/click/coupling/etc., plus `outputBus` (line 210) and `chokeGroup` for routing — but no pan/width. Grep for pan/Pan/width/stereo/decorrel across src/ returns nothing relevant (the line-89 "decorrelation" comment refers to per-voice PRNG seed state, not stereo).

5. Downstream stages also mono: master coupling (processor.cpp:787-794) sums to mono, processes, then adds the SAME `coupling` scalar to outL and outR; master gain (809-813) scales both equally. No pan/width anywhere from voice -> pool -> master.

AUDIBILITY: The instrument emits a hard-centered, perfectly correlated (mono) image with zero per-pad spatial differentiation across all 32 pads. This is genuinely audible and is a real limitation versus any real kit or competing drum instrument, which spread pads across the field. So the finding is real, not a no-op.

However, regarding the SPECIFIC user symptom ("presets mostly sound the same and boring"): a mono image is a constant shared identically by every preset. It does not flatten dynamics, homogenize timbre, or collapse any parameter range — the timbre set upstream passes through the mono path intact. Therefore it cannot explain why two presets with different timbral params sound similar; it only means they share the same centered spatial picture. It is a weak/secondary contributor to the timbral-sameness complaint (the dynamics-flattening stages — per-voice softClip, master energy limiter, 1/sqrt(N) normalize+gain-comp — are the stronger suspects), though it does plausibly contribute to a general "lifeless/flat/no width" impression.

Severity: real and audible, but it is a missing-feature/design gap (no stereo field exists) rather than a stage that corrupts or cancels the signal, and only a secondary driver of the reported symptom. Medium.

finding.title: Per-voice softClip saturates on loud modal bodies, crushing velocity dynamics
finding.problem: Every voice ends with `softClip(shaped * env * level_)`. For modal bodies (kick/toms, Membrane 48 modes) the pre-clip argument routinely reaches 2-3, which the tanh-style softClip pins near +/-1.0. The measurement test (test_output_level_measure.cpp) shows kick and ALL toms peak at exactly 0.5012 (= -6 dB master * 1.0 voice peak) at BOTH velocity 1.0 and velocity 0.5 -- the peak is identical, i.e. velocity no longer changes the peak for these pads.
finding.why_wrong: Velocity is supposed to scale output via ampEnvelope peakLevel_ = velocity (adsr_envelope.h:443). Because the signal is driven well past the softClip knee, the env's velocity scaling is swallowed by the clipper: softClip(2.0)=0.984 and softClip(1.0*2.0)~=softClip(0.5*... ) both land near unity. A drum synth must preserve velocity dynamics; here loud pads collapse to a fixed ceiling. Verified numerically: vel 1.0 and vel 0.5 both yield peak 0.5012 for Kick/Floor Tom.
finding.sound_impact: Loud pads (kick, all 6 toms) all hit the same -6 dBFS ceiling regardless of velocity or of which mode-balance the parameters produce, so they sound equally loud and equally saturated -- a direct cause of presets sounding the same/boring. Soft hits and hard hits on a tom are nearly indistinguishable in level.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL by reading the code AND running the measurement test.

Code at drum_voice.h:446, 769, 782 confirms all three voice output paths (processBlockSlow per-sample, processBlockFast default-off lane, processBlockFast UN lane) end with `Krate::DSP::softClip(shaped * env * level_/level)`. softClip (dsp_utils.h:105-112) is a tanh-like polynomial that hard-pins to +/-1.0 for |x|>3 and strongly compresses near the knee (softClip(2.0)=0.984, softClip(1.0)=0.778). Velocity reaches this argument through `env`: ampEnvelope_.setVelocityScaling(true) (line 128) + setVelocity(velocity) (line 327), and updatePeakLevel sets peakLevel_=velocity (adsr_envelope.h:443). (Note: a separate `velocityGain_` member is set at line 329 but is dead — never read anywhere — with a stale comment claiming velocity moved off the env; functionally velocity still scales via env, so the finding's mechanism holds.)

I ran the diagnostic test membrum_tests.exe "[.measure]". Real measured peaks confirm the claim exactly:
- Kick: vel 1.0 -> 0.5012, vel 0.5 -> 0.5012 (IDENTICAL peak)
- Floor Tom Lo: vel 1.0 -> 0.5012, vel 0.5 -> 0.5012 (IDENTICAL peak)
- All toms at vel 1.0 cluster at exactly 0.5012 (-6.00 dBFS)
0.5012 = the default master gain (state_codec.h:78 / processor.h:136: masterGainNorm default 0.5 = -6 dB = 0.5012 linear), meaning the pre-master voice output is pinned to ~1.0 by softClip. So these modal bodies ARE being driven well past the softClip knee and the peak is velocity-independent. RMS still differs (kick 0.2164 vs 0.1859), so some sub-peak dynamic survives, but the peak is fully crushed.

The finding is correct that velocity dynamics on the peak collapse for loud modal bodies (kick + floor toms), directly supporting the user-reported "presets sound the same/boring" symptom. The code's own comment (line 241) claims Membrane peaks ~0.36 post-1/sqrt(N), but the FULL chain (exciter + click layer mixed at half into excitation and full into output + noise + toneShaper drive) clearly pushes the softClip argument past unity in practice, as the measured 0.5012 proves.

Minor inaccuracies that do NOT change the verdict: it is not literally ALL toms at both velocities — High Tom drops 0.5012 -> 0.4580 at vel 0.5, so it is not perfectly saturated. But kick, floor tom, and the vel-1.0 tom cluster confirm widespread saturation.

Severity high (not critical): no crash/NaN, output stays bounded in [-1,1] and audio is produced; but it materially defeats per-velocity peak dynamics — a core drum-synth feature — and homogenizes loud pads, a real contributor to the sameness symptom.

VERIFIED BY READING CODE + RUNNING THE MEASUREMENT TEST.

Literal code (drum_voice.h:446, 769, 782, and also 885 in the slow path): every voice output is `Krate::DSP::softClip(shaped * env * level_)`. softClip (dsp_utils.h:105) is a tanh approximation `x*(27+x²)/(27+9x²)` hard-clamped to [-1,1], saturating fully for |x|>3. softClip(2.0)=0.984, so an argument near/above 2-3 pins near unity. CONFIRMED.

Ground truth: ran membrum_tests.exe "[.measure]". At velocity 1.0, Kick(36) and ALL six toms (41,43,45,47,48,50) peak at exactly 0.5012 (-6.00 dBFS = master gain × clipped 1.0). At velocity 0.5, Kick=0.5012 and Floor Tom Lo=0.5012 — IDENTICAL peak to vel 1.0. The per-voice softClip is genuinely pinning these loud modal bodies at 1.0 regardless of velocity, so the instantaneous PEAK no longer encodes velocity. CONFIRMED for kick/floor toms.

HOWEVER the finding is OVERSTATED in two ways:
1. "ALL toms" is false. At vel 0.5, High Tom(50) dropped 0.5012→0.4580, i.e. higher-pitched toms partially recover dynamics — only the lowest, loudest pads fully pin.
2. "velocity dynamics collapse / soft and hard hits nearly indistinguishable in level" is too strong. Velocity scales BOTH the exciter amplitude (drum_voice.h:278 -> impulse_exciter.h:39 trigger(velocity)) AND the amp env peakLevel (line 327; adsr peakLevel_=velocity). The RMS clearly still tracks velocity (Kick RMS 0.2164→0.1859; Floor Tom Lo 0.2982→0.2561). So sustain/loudness DO change; only the transient PEAK is flattened to a fixed ceiling.

Audibility: the transient peak is the dominant perceptual cue for drum velocity and timbral "punch", so pinning every loud pad's attack to the same -6 dBFS ceiling really does homogenize the loud pads and flatten velocity-driven dynamics — a genuine contributor to the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom. But it affects only the loudest pads, RMS dynamics survive, and it is one of several contributors rather than the dominant or sole cause. Hence the bug is real and audible but its impact is narrower than claimed: corrected_severity=medium rather than high.

finding.title: Per-pad Level knob is applied INSIDE the softClip, so it cannot attenuate an over-driven voice
finding.problem: level_ multiplies the signal before the clipper: softClip(shaped * env * level_). When the modal body + Phase-8D secondary/coupling drive the argument far above the knee, reducing level_ just moves the operating point along the clipper's flat region instead of reducing output. The diagnostic test_orchestral_distortion.cpp sets level=0.10 (a deep cut) yet the baseline voice still peaks at 1.0 with RMS 0.67 and 7588 of 24000 samples pinned >=0.99 (CLIPPING).
finding.why_wrong: A level/volume control must be a clean post-saturation gain (or the saturation must be calibrated so nominal signals sit below the knee). Placing the user's volume cut before a hard-driven nonlinearity makes it a near no-op -- exactly the user-reported 'distortion that doesn't respond to preset-level cuts'. The named diagnostic test was written to reproduce this very complaint.
finding.sound_impact: Turning a pad's Level down does not make it quieter or cleaner once the voice is in saturation; the timbre stays distorted and the level barely moves. Removes a primary per-preset differentiator and homogenizes the kit.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly in the code. drum_voice.h:446 (per-sample path) computes `return Krate::DSP::softClip(shaped * env * level_);` and the block path at 769/782 computes `out[offset + i] = Krate::DSP::softClip(shaped * env * level);`. In all three, the per-pad Level knob is the argument to softClip, i.e. applied PRE-saturation, exactly as the finding claims. `level_` is the per-pad Level (setLevel() at line 932, default 0.8, sourced from kPadLevel) and it is used nowhere else as a post-clip gain (grep shows uses only at 152/446/483/769/782/1168/1253 — all the param-snapshot copies plus the two clip expressions). The voice's softClipped output flows to the pool where only pan/master gain are applied afterward, so there is no compensating post-clip level stage.

softClip (dsp_utils.h:105) is a tanh-like saturator: returns exactly ±1.0 for |arg|>=3 and is compressive in the knee. So when shaped*env is driven well above the knee, multiplying by a reduced level_ slides the operating point along the flat/near-flat region instead of cleanly attenuating output. Worked example: if shaped*env≈30, then level_=1.0 -> arg=30 -> 1.0, and level_=0.10 -> arg=3.0 -> softClip(3.0)=3*(27+9)/(27+81)=108/108=1.0 — a 10x level cut yields zero change in output. This is consistent with the diagnostic test_orchestral_distortion.cpp, which sets level=0.10 yet checks for peak ~1.0 and heavy clipping (samples>=0.99). A correct volume control would be softClip(shaped*env) * level_ (post-saturation), or the saturation should be calibrated so nominal signals sit below the knee.

The behaviour is genuinely present and genuinely wrong as a gain-staging/UX defect: turning a pad's Level down does not make a saturated voice quieter or cleaner, removing a key per-preset differentiator and contributing to the 'presets sound the same' symptom. I downgrade severity from the implied high to medium: it is a real correctness/perceptual bug but only manifests when a voice is actually driven into saturation (not universally), and it does not crash, corrupt state, propagate NaN/Inf, or violate real-time safety.

VERIFIED by reading code and running the named diagnostic.

STRUCTURAL CLAIM (confirmed exact): drum_voice.h:446 (slow per-sample), :769 and :782 (fast block paths) all compute `Krate::DSP::softClip(shaped * env * level)` / `level_`. The per-pad Level multiplies the signal INSIDE the saturator. softClip (dsp/include/krate/dsp/core/dsp_utils.h:105) is a tanh approximation that hard-clamps to +/-1 for |x|>3 and is monotonic-saturating below that. level_ defaults 0.8 (line 1313), set via setLevel (line 932) from the per-pad Level knob. Velocity also enters inside the clip via env (ampEnvelope_.setVelocity -> env), reinforcing that the user's volume cut sits pre-nonlinearity.

AUDIBILITY (confirmed by running the cited test, not trusting the report): membrum_tests.exe "[diagnose_orch]" prints, at level=0.10:
  baseline      peak=1.0000 rms=0.6695 samples>=0.99: 7588 CLIPPING
  no-secondary  peak=1.0000 rms=0.1960 samples>=0.99: 96
  no-coupling   peak=1.0000 rms=0.1960 samples>=0.99: 96
These match the finding's numbers exactly. Since shaped*env*0.10 still exceeds the +/-3 knee at peaks, shaped*env is ~30+ at peaks; at any higher level value the voice is even more saturated, so output peak stays pinned at 1.0 and RMS barely changes across the level range. Requesting an 18 dB+ cut (0.8 -> 0.1) yields essentially no attenuation -> the Level knob is a near no-op once the voice is in saturation. The dominant overdrive source is the Phase-8D secondary shell bank, which is summed into the body pre-post-chain at line 731 (bodyScratch[i] += secOut[i]); disabling secondary/coupling drops RMS from 0.67 to 0.196.

CONCLUSION: A volume/level control placed before a hard-driven nonlinearity cannot attenuate or clean up an over-driven voice. This directly produces the user-reported 'distortion that does not respond to preset-level cuts' and homogenizes the kit by flattening a primary per-preset differentiator. Real, audible, mechanism correctly identified. Severity high rather than critical: no crash/NaN/silence, but it defeats a key control and is a strong contributor to the sameness symptom.

finding.title: bodyGainCompensation_ / secondaryGainCompensation_ are dead: 1/sqrt(N) normalization runs with no compensation
finding.problem: The comment at lines 673-675 says the code compensates for the bank's 1/sum normalization to restore historical loudness, computed from sum(amplitudes)*0.707. But bodyGainCompensation_ and secondaryGainCompensation_ are only ever assigned 1.0 (lines 251-252; grep confirms no other writes). The `if (bodyGainCompensation_ != 1.0f)` blocks (676, 701) are therefore unreachable dead code.
finding.why_wrong: Either the compensation was intended (then it is silently missing and the 1/sqrt(N) output gain stands alone and unscaled), or it was abandoned (then the branch and member should be removed). As-is the documented loudness-restoration does not happen. 1/sqrt(48)=0.144 for Membrane is applied with no per-body calibration, so absolute body level is governed only by sum(rawBesselAmplitudes)*0.144, which still over-drives the downstream softClip (see findings above) -- the 'compensation' that would have let it be balanced instead of clipped is inert.
finding.sound_impact: Body loudness across bodies/mode-counts is uncalibrated, contributing to the over-drive into the per-voice clipper and to inconsistent loudness vs the (clipped) tom/kick path. Misleads future maintainers into thinking gain is staged when it is not.

verdicts.reasoning:

The 1/sqrt(N) output gain (lines 247-248, 255-256) stands alone with no per-body calibration, exactly as the finding states.

Two nuances that lower the severity below the finding's framing: (1) The separate, newer noteOn comment at lines 240-246 describes the 1/sqrt(N)+softClip design as the deliberate, intended post-Phase-11 behaviour — i.e. the abandoned-compensation branch (673-675/1356-1359) appears to be stale leftovers rather than a silently-missing feature. So the "as-is loudness restoration doesn't happen" is accurate but the system is functioning as the later comment intends. (2) The audible "sound impact" is indirect: nothing is broken at runtime; the gain behaviour is fully governed by the working 1/sqrt(N) path. The over-drive-into-softClip claim is a plausible downstream consequence but is not caused by this dead code per se — removing the dead code would change nothing audibly. Hence the bug is real but its practical effect is confined to dead code + misleading docs, warranting low severity.

The STRUCTURAL facts in the finding are correct: bodyGainCompensation_/secondaryGainCompensation_ are declared = 1.0f (1360-1361), the only assignment anywhere is the unconditional `= 1.0f` at 251-252 (grep shows no other writes), so the `if (... != 1.0f)` guards at 676 and 701 are always false and the multiply loops (678-680, 703-705) are unreachable dead code. Reading the Phase 11 design comments (200-249) shows this is the DELIBERATE intended behavior: drop the modal-bank soft-clip, use 1/sqrt(N) as the SOLE output normalization (set at 248), and rely on the downstream per-voice softClip(shaped*env*level) (446, 769, 782, 885) as the only bus-bounding stage. The 1.0f compensation is consistent with that design — the compensation path was intentionally neutralized, not accidentally lost. The plugin's audible output is exactly 1/sqrt(N) + downstream softClip whether or not the dead branches exist; deleting them would change nothing audible. So this does NOT alter body loudness, does NOT add to over-drive (the code applies no extra gain), and does NOT contribute to the "presets sound the same" symptom — that lives in the genuinely active stages (standalone 1/sqrt(N), downstream softClip, master limiter). finding.title: bodyGainCompensation_ is hard-coded to 1.0, so the 1/sqrt(N) modal-bank normalize attenuates the body ~17 dB with no make-up gain
finding.problem: noteOn() sets the bank output gain to 1.0f/sqrt(N) for every non-feedback voice (line 248) and then sets bodyGainCompensation_ = 1.0f (line 251). The documented design (member comment lines 1356-1360) is that bodyGainCompensation_ should be sum(amplitudes)*0.707 to restore loudness after the 1/sum normalization, but that assignment is never made anywhere (the only writes are the literal 1.0f at 251 and the member initializer at 1360). At line 676 the compensation multiply is therefore skipped (`if (bodyGainCompensation_ != 1.0f)` is false). For the default Membrane bodies N = getNumModes() = 48 (membrane_mapper.h kMembraneModeCount, reset in modal_resonator_bank.h:654), so the modal body is scaled by 1/sqrt(48) = 0.144 with zero make-up.
finding.why_wrong: The 1/sqrt(N) factor is an energy-equalization norm that is only loudness-neutral if paired with the intended make-up gain. Field practice (Faust physmodels.lib `:> /(nModes)`, STK Modal) normalizes the SUMMED bank so the summed peak is unity-ish; here the bank is divided by sqrt(N) AND the promised compensation is dropped, leaving the body ~17 dB below its calibrated level. getNumModes() also returns the full partial count (48), not the post-cull active count, so strikes that excite few modes are attenuated even harder.
finding.sound_impact: The modal body -- the ONLY stage that carries Material, Size, Strike Position, air-loading and per-mode damping timbre -- is pushed ~17 dB under the parallel noise (x3) and click (x2) layers (next finding). Because the loud, broadband noise/click transient is what the ear hears, presets with very different body parameters converge to the same noisy-thwack character. This is the primary mechanical cause of 'different presets, same boring sound'.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified by reading the actual code in plugins/membrum/src/dsp/drum_voice.h and dsp/include/krate/dsp/processors/modal_resonator_bank.h.

FACTUAL CLAIMS — all confirmed:
- Line 248: for every non-feedback voice, bank.setOutputGain(1.0f / sqrt(getNumModes())). Confirmed.
- Line 251: bodyGainCompensation_ = 1.0f. A grep over plugins/membrum/src shows the ONLY writes to bodyGainCompensation_ are this literal 1.0f (line 251) and the member initializer (line 1360). The documented sum(amplitudes)*0.707 assignment is NEVER made anywhere. Confirmed.
- Line 676: `if (bodyGainCompensation_ != 1.0f)` — always false, so the make-up-gain multiply (lines 678-681) is dead code on every non-feedback voice. Confirmed.
- Member comment 1356-1360 explicitly documents the intended behaviour: "Set in noteOn() from sum(amplitudes)*0.707 (target = old saturated peak)." That code path is never armed. The inline comment 673-676 likewise claims the multiply "compensate[s] for the bank's 1/sum normalisation so the post-bank perceived loudness matches the pre-Phase-11 saturated peak" — also dead.
- N for Membrane: membrane_mapper.h:76 kMembraneModeCount = 48; numPartials = 48 (line 163); modal_resonator_bank.h:654 numModes_ = numPartials; getNumModes() returns numModes_. So 1/sqrt(48) = 0.144 ≈ -16.8 dB. Confirmed.
- The bank exposes getInputGainSum() = sum(|gain_k|) (modal_resonator_bank.h:165, accumulated at 722), so the documented compensation WAS implementable from available data but was dropped.

SIGNAL-PATH IMPACT — confirmed: lines 766/776 sum combined = bodyScratch + noiseScratch + clickScratch. noiseScratch is multiplied by NoiseLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain = 3.0 (noise_layer.h:218) and clickScratch by ClickLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain = 2.0 (click_layer.h:153), both routed at full level, while the body sits at 0.144 with no make-up. The body is the only stage carrying Material/Size/Strike-Position/damping timbre, so it is buried ~17–26 dB under the broadband noise/click layers — a strong mechanical cause of 'different presets, same boring thwack'.

CAVEAT (why not critical): there is an internal contradiction in the comments. The Phase 11 / spec 145 comment block at 207-246 argues that 1/sqrt(N) alone is the intended energy-equalisation calibration (claiming Membrane peak ~0.36) with downstream softClip bounding output — i.e., one school of thought says NO make-up is wanted. But the compensation member + its multiply + the member doc-comment all describe an armed make-up path that was never wired. So either the comment at 1356-1360 / 673-676 is stale, or the assignment was forgotten. Either way it is a real defect: dead, self-contradicting code whose documented intent (restore historical loudness) is not delivered, with an audible loudness consequence. No test asserts the body level (grep of plugins/membrum/tests finds no reference to bodyGainCompensation/inputGainSum), so the unfinished path went uncaught. High rather than critical because the exact dB shortfall vs. an intended target is design-dependent (the contradicting comment leaves the intended body level ambiguous), but the homogenisation effect on timbre is clearly present.

Verified against the actual code. drum_voice.h:251 sets bodyGainCompensation_ = 1.0f (the ONLY non-initializer write; grep confirms writes only at 251 and the 1360 member initializer). At 676-681 the make-up multiply is gated `if (bodyGainCompensation_ != 1.0f)`, so it is never applied. The member comment (1356-1360) documents the INTENDED value `sum(amplitudes)*0.707` to restore historical loudness, which is never assigned. At 247-248 the non-feedback path sets the bank output gain to 1/sqrt(getNumModes()). Default Membrane body = 48 modes (membrane_mapper.h:76 kMembraneModeCount=48; r.numPartials=48 at line 163; getNumModes() returns numModes_ at modal_resonator_bank.h:434), so the bank is scaled by 1/sqrt(48)=0.144 (~ -17 dB) with zero make-up. All mechanical facts in the finding check out.

NUANCE that lowers severity below the finding's claim: the Phase 11 comments at 233-249 describe 1/sqrt(N) as a DELIBERATE amplitude-independent energy norm and explicitly accept a low body peak ("Membrane ~0.36"), relying on the downstream softClip(shaped*env*level) to bound output. So bodyGainCompensation_=1.0 reads as an intentional design with a STALE member comment, not a literally "dropped" assignment — the finding's "documented design never wired" framing overstates intent. However, the AUDIBILITY consequence is real: the parallel noise layer is mixed at kStandaloneOutputGain=3.0 and the click at 2.0 (drum_voice.h:745, 662; constants in noise_layer.h:218, click_layer.h:153), and the noise constant's own comment says it was "calibrated against the modal body's peak." With the body left at ~0.36 peak and no make-up while the layers carry full standalone gain, presets that engage the noise/click layers will have the broadband transient dominate the timbre-carrying modal body, flattening differences in Material/Size/StrikePos toward a uniform "noisy thwack" — consistent with the reported sameness symptom.

Limits on impact: NoiseLayer mix defaults to 0.0 (noise_layer.h:29,63) and the layer outputs mix_*env*noise, so the homogenization only bites on presets that actually dial in noise/click; body-only presets still carry full per-body timbre (just quieter, recovered downstream by amp env*level and master gain). The getNumModes()==48 (uncull­ed) detail is real but secondary. Net: a genuine relative gain-staging imbalance that plausibly contributes to the sameness, but it is a contributing factor among several, not conclusively the single "primary mechanical cause." Hence corrected_severity=medium, confidence medium.

finding.title: Parallel noise (x3) and click (x2) layers are summed at full strength while the body is attenuated, so the broadband layers dominate every preset
finding.problem: noiseSample = noiseLayer.processSample() * NoiseLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain (3.0, noise_layer.h:218) and clickSample is mixed at ClickLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain (2.0, click_layer.h:153) -- click is added TWICE (half into excitation at line 667/425, full into output at line 766/435). These layers are summed into combinedBody (line 435 / 766) with NO 1/sqrt(N)-type attenuation, while the body has been scaled by ~0.144 (previous finding). Default kit mixes are high: noiseLayerMix 0.5-0.95 and clickLayerMix 0.4-0.75 across Kick/Snare/Tom/Hat/Cymbal (default_kit.h:64-207).
finding.why_wrong: After the body finding, the layer-vs-body balance is roughly (mix*3) and (mix*2) for un-attenuated layers vs ~0.144*modeSum for the body. With snare noiseLayerMix=0.9 -> peak ~2.7 from noise alone vs a body fraction. The layers are filtered-white-noise bursts plus a raised-cosine click; their timbre barely changes with the body knobs. The intended calibration comment ('comparable to the modal body') assumed the body was at its compensated level, which it is not.
finding.sound_impact: Across the default kit nearly every pad is dominated by a filtered-noise burst + click transient. Kick, snare, toms and percs all lead with the same broadband attack and noise wash, masking the modal pitch/material differences -- the audible reason presets 'mostly sound the same and boring'. Turning Material/Size/airLoading/damping makes little perceptual difference because the stage they control is buried.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified all cited code. drum_voice.h:433-435 (slow path): noiseSample = noiseLayer_.processSample() * NoiseLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain, then combinedBody = body + noiseSample + clickSample. noise_layer.h:218 confirms kStandaloneOutputGain = 3.0f; click_layer.h:153 confirms ClickLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain = 2.0f. Both layers apply mix_ internally (noise_layer.h:147 returns mix_*env*f; click_layer.h:132 returns mix_*velocity*env*f), so the caller-side peak scales as mix*3 and mix*2*velocity. Fast path (lines 743-748, 760-770) reproduces the same: noiseScratch *= 3.0, then combined = bodyScratch[i] + noiseScratch[i] + clickScratch[i] with no per-layer attenuation.

Click-added-twice is confirmed: half-amplitude into excitation (exc = excMain + clickSample*0.5f at line 425; excScratch[i] += clickScratch[i]*0.5f at line 667) AND full-amplitude into the audible sum (line 435 combinedBody, line 766 combined).

Body attenuation confirmed: lines 247-248 set the modal bank output gain to 1.0f/std::sqrt(N) (Phase 11, spec 145), and bodyGainCompensation_ stays at 1.0f (line 251) so it does NOT undo the normalization. The in-code comment (line 241) states the resulting Membrane body peak is ~0.36. So per-voice balance is ~mix*3 (noise) and ~mix*2 (click) for body-independent broadband layers vs a ~0.36-peak modal body.

Default kit mixes confirmed high across all 6 pads (default_kit.h:64/69, 97/102, 124/129, 154/159, 178/183, 200/205): noiseLayerMix 0.50-0.95, clickLayerMix 0.40-0.75. The noise/click layers are bandpass-filtered white noise + raised-cosine click, independent of the body params (Material/Size/airLoading/damping), which only affect the attenuated modal stage. The downstream softClip(shaped*env*level) and toneShaper apply equally to all summed components, so they do not change the relative balance — the layer-vs-body dominance is real.

Corroborating dev comments: default_kit.h:60-63 explicitly notes "the noise + click layers were drowning the modal body's air-loading/damping character" and reduced kick mixes (85->50%, 75->40%) — but those Phase 8C reductions were calibrated against the pre-Phase-11 (un-attenuated) body, and the 1/sqrt(N) attenuation is a later change. The noise calibration comment (noise_layer.h:213-216) likewise assumes the layer should be "comparable to the modal body" at the old level. This is a genuine calibration/correctness defect that homogenizes timbre and masks body-parameter changes — a direct mechanism for the user-reported 'presets sound the same and boring' symptom. Downgraded from critical to high: it strongly flattens timbre but does not crash/produce NaN/break real-time safety, and the body is still present (not fully inaudible), so it is a serious calibration bug rather than a catastrophic one.

Verified all cited code. STRUCTURAL CLAIMS CONFIRMED: (1) drum_voice.h:433-435 slow path: noiseSample = noiseLayer_.processSample() * NoiseLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain, then combinedBody = body + noiseSample + clickSample — layers summed with NO 1/sqrt(N) attenuation. (2) Lines 422-425: clickSample = click*2.0; exc = excMain + clickSample*0.5 (half into excitation) AND clickSample added full at line 435 — click is indeed counted twice (half drives the modal bank, full goes direct to output). (3) Fast path 743-748 scales noise by 3.0; default-off lane 760-770 sums bodyScratch+noiseScratch+clickScratch directly. Constants confirmed: NoiseLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain=3.0 (noise_layer.h:218), ClickLayer::kStandaloneOutputGain=2.0 (click_layer.h:153). Body attenuation confirmed: setOutputGain(1/sqrt(N)) at drum_voice.h:248 with bodyGainCompensation_ only ever = 1.0f (no compensation on the fast non-feedback path); Membrane kMembraneModeCount=48 -> 1/sqrt(48)=0.144 exactly matches the prior finding; Plate=16 -> 0.25. Body peak documented as ~0.36 for Membrane (comment line 241). Default kit mixes confirmed (noise 0.50-0.95, click 0.40-0.75) and Kick/Snare/Tom use Membrane (modal), Perc uses Plate, while Hat/Cymbal use NoiseBody (body is itself broadband noise, reinforcing sameness).

ONE QUANTITATIVE ERROR in the finding: it claims "snare noiseLayerMix=0.9 -> peak ~2.7 from noise alone." That is ~10x too high. The 3.0 gain is calibrated against an already-attenuated noise source (~0.1 RMS per noise_layer.h:214 comment), so actual noise output is ~0.9*0.1*3.0 ≈ 0.27 RMS, not 2.7. The finding treats the 3x as a multiplier on a unit signal.

NET ASSESSMENT: Even with corrected magnitude, the balance is unfavorable — noise ~0.27 RMS vs a modal body whose PEAK is only ~0.36 (RMS far lower), summed un-attenuated, plus the click double-counted into the output. So the broadband layers are at minimum co-equal with and plausibly louder (RMS) than the modal body across the default kit. These layers (filtered white noise + raised-cosine click) barely change timbre when Material/Size/airLoading/damping are turned, because those knobs only steer the modal body that is being buried. This is an audible, real contributor to the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom. Confidence medium (not high) because the precise loudness balance is estimated from RMS/peak relationships and runtime noise statistics rather than measured from a render. Severity high rather than critical: it flattens timbre but does not break audio; magnitude was overstated and the click double-count is partly intentional commuted-synthesis design.

finding.title: velocityGain_ is computed and reset but never read -- dead per-voice state
finding.problem: velocityGain_ = std::clamp(velocity,0,1) is assigned in noteOn (line 329) and zeroed in resetForKitSwitch (line 378) but is never used in process(), processBlockFast, or processBlockSlow. The member comment (1315-1317) claims it replaced the amp envelope's peakLevel_ velocity scaling, but the amp envelope still does velocity scaling itself (ampEnvelope_.setVelocityScaling(true) at prepare line 128, setVelocity at 327; adsr_envelope.h:443 peakLevel_=velocity_).
finding.why_wrong: Velocity does reach the output via the ADSR's peakLevel_, so this is not a functional bug, but the dead variable and its misleading comment indicate the velocity-to-output path was refactored and left half-wired. If a future reader 're-enables' velocityGain_ they would double-apply velocity. It is a latent correctness/maintenance hazard, not a current audible defect.
finding.sound_impact: No direct audible effect today (velocity still works through the envelope). Flagged because it muddies the velocity gain-staging that the dynamics complaint touches and could cause a double-scaling regression if 'fixed' naively.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified directly against the code. drum_voice.h:1317 declares `float velocityGain_ = 1.0f;`, line 329 assigns `velocityGain_ = std::clamp(velocity,0,1)` in noteOn, line 378 zeroes it in resetForKitSwitch. A Grep across the entire plugins/membrum/src tree returns ONLY those three lines — declaration plus two writes, zero reads. It is never referenced in process() (line 446 output is `softClip(shaped * env * level_)`), processBlockFast, or processBlockSlow. So the member is genuinely dead per-voice state.

Velocity still reaches the output through the ADSR envelope, exactly as the finding claims: prepare() calls `ampEnvelope_.setVelocityScaling(true)` (line 128), noteOn calls `ampEnvelope_.setVelocity(velocity)` (line 327), and adsr_envelope.h:443 sets `peakLevel_ = velocityScalingEnabled_ ? velocity_ : 1.0f`. peakLevel_ scales the envelope output (e.g. lines 280/285/295/312), and that `env` multiplies the signal at drum_voice.h:446. Velocity is therefore functionally applied — this is not a current audible defect.

The member comment at 1315-1317 ("velocity scaling applied at the output (previously handled by the amp envelope's peakLevel_)") is inaccurate: the amp envelope STILL applies velocity via peakLevel_, confirming the velocity-to-output path was refactored and left half-wired. Re-enabling velocityGain_ naively would double-apply velocity.

Conclusion: the described behaviour is present and the code claim is accurate. It is a real dead-code / misleading-comment maintenance hazard, not a functional bug and unrelated to the presets-sound-the-same symptom. Corrected severity: low (latent hazard, no audible effect today). The prior agent's characterization is accurate.

Confirmed by reading drum_voice.h and adsr_envelope.h. velocityGain_ is declared at line 1317 (default 1.0f), assigned at line 329 in noteOn (std::clamp(velocity,0,1)), and zeroed at line 378 in resetForKitSwitch. A grep across the entire plugins/membrum/src tree finds it referenced ONLY in drum_voice.h, and within that file the only three occurrences are the declaration and the two writes — it is never read in process(), processBlockFast, or processBlockSlow. So it is genuinely dead per-voice state.

The misleading comment claim is also verified: lines 1315-1317 state velocity scaling was moved "to the output (previously handled by the amp envelope's peakLevel_)", but the amp envelope still performs that scaling itself — setVelocityScaling(true) at prepare line 128, setVelocity(velocity) at noteOn line 327, and adsr_envelope.h:443 sets peakLevel_ = velocityScalingEnabled_ ? velocity_ : 1.0f, which scales every envelope output value (lines 280/285/295/298/303-304/312). Thus velocity dynamics DO reach the output through the ADSR; nothing is broken functionally.

AUDIBILITY: zero audible effect today. Velocity-to-output works correctly via the ADSR peakLevel_ path. The dead variable does not flatten dynamics, clamp range, or homogenize timbre, so it does NOT contribute to the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom. It is purely a maintenance/latent-regression hazard (a naive re-enable would double-apply velocity). Real as described, but not an audible bug — severity low.

research.claim_as_coded: membrane_modes.h:30-37 — kMembraneRatios[48] = {1.0000, 1.5933, 2.1357, 2.2954, 2.6528, 2.9173, 3.1554, 3.5001, ...} computed as j_mn/j_01 (j_01=2.4048). kMembraneBesselZeros[48] (lines 108-115) = {2.4048, 3.8317, 5.1356, 5.5201, 6.3802, 7.0156, 7.5883, ...} (A&S Table 9.5). kMembraneBesselOrder (40-47) and kMembraneBesselRadial (53-60) tag each mode (m,n). Strike weighting (membrane_mapper.h:101-108): amplitude[k] = |J_m(j_mn * r_over_a)| with r_over_a = strikePos*0.9 (strike at fixed angle, cos(mθ) factor dropped). Air-loading (membrane_modes.h:98-105, applied at membrane_mapper.h:90-99): f_k = f0*ratio*(1 + airLoading*(targetScale-1)); kAirLoadingTargetScale shifts only the (m,1) modes for m=2..5 by {1.1192, 1.2012, 1.2321, 1.2668}, anchoring (0,1) and (1,1) at pure Bessel and leaving all n>=2 and m>=6 modes at unity.
research.correct_form: Ideal-membrane Bessel-zero ratios (j_mn/j_01): (0,1)=1.000, (1,1)=1.5933, (2,1)=2.1356, (0,2)=2.2954, (3,1)=2.6531, (1,2)=2.9173, (4,1)=3.156, (2,2)=3.501, (0,3)=3.598, (5,1)=3.652. The coded values match A&S/Fletcher&Rossing to 4 decimals (Wikipedia rounds (1,1) to 1.591; code's 1.5933 is the more precise 3.83171/2.40483). The Rossing air-loaded timpani "preferred mode" series for diametric modes (1,1):(2,1):(3,1):(4,1):(5,1) = 1 : 1.5 : 2.0 : 2.44 : 2.9 (relative to (1,1)). Verified the code lands exactly on this: (2,1) 2.1357*1.1192=2.390=1.5*1.5933; (3,1) 2.6528*1.2012=3.187=2.0*1.5933; (4,1) 3.1554*1.2321=3.888=2.44*1.5933; (5,1) 3.6475*1.2668=4.621=2.90*1.5933. Strike weighting |J_m(j_mn*r/a)| is the correct radial mode-shape evaluation; physically a strike also has a cos(mθ) angular factor, which the code omits (reasonable simplification, slight over-excitation of m>=1 modes near the rim).

sources.title: Vibration of a circular membrane — Wikipedia (Bessel zeros j_mn and ratio-to-fundamental table)
sources.says: Tabulates alpha_mn (Bessel zeros): (0,1)=2.40483, (1,1)=3.83171, (2,1)=5.13562, (0,2)=5.52008, (1,2)=7.01559; frequency ratio = alpha_mn/alpha_01 gives 1.000, 1.591, 2.135, 2.294, 2.917 — matching the coded ratios.

sources.title: Euphonics 3.6 Tuned drums / 3.6.1 Vibration modes of a circular drum (Jim Woodhouse)
sources.says: Natural frequencies of an ideal membrane are governed by J_m(ka)=0 (Bessel zeros). For a real timpani, air loading plus the kettle make the preferred diametric modes (m,1) fall into a near-harmonic series with a missing fundamental; reference (single-nodal-diameter) mode is assigned 2, others near 3 and 4 (i.e. 1.5:2:2.5 relative to (1,1)).

sources.title: Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments (kettledrum diametric-mode ratios)
sources.says: After air loading the preferred (diametric) modes (1,1),(2,1),(3,1),(4,1),(5,1) of the timpani are nearly in the ratios 1 : 1.5 : 2 : 2.44 : 2.9, giving the drum a definite pitch; principal modes approximate 2:3:4 harmonic relation.

sources.title: The Well-Tempered Timpani — Air Loading (Rossing et al. cross-reference)
sources.says: Air loading substantially lowers the lower-frequency modes (small effect on higher modes); only the diametric (m,1) modes form the harmonic relationship, consistent with anchoring (1,1) and shifting (2,1)..(5,1) upward in ratio space.

research.implication: Not a source of the "boring/same" symptom.
