The modal frequency model is scientifically correct: pure Bessel ratios match A&S to 4 decimals, and the airLoading parameter morphs the (m,1) modes precisely onto Rossing's measured timpani series (1:1.5:2:2.44:2.9). Strike-position weighting via |J_m(j_mn*r/a)| is the textbook radial mode-shape and will produce genuine timbral variation across strikePos (rim hits suppress low-m modes, center hits favor axisymmetric modes). Two minor, non-bug nuances: (1) the cos(mθ) angular factor is dropped, so m>=1 modes are slightly over-weighted vs a true off-center point strike, but the radial dependence (the audibly dominant effect) is correct; (2) air loading only reshapes 4 of the 48 modes and leaves all n>=2 and m>=6 at pure Bessel, which is faithful to the limit of Rossing's published data, not an error. If presets sound homogeneous, the cause lies downstream (softClip on shaped*env*level, the 1/sqrt(N) normalize + gain compensation, the master energy limiter, or parameters clamped/cancelled before reaching the bank) — NOT in these mode ratios.

research.claim_as_coded: plate_modes.h hardcodes simply-supported (SSSS) square-plate eigenvalues as the mode-ratio table: kPlateRatios[k] = (m^2+n^2)/2 for the (m,n) pairs in kPlateIndices, i.e. 1.000, 2.500, 4.000, 5.000, 6.500, 8.500, 9.000, 10.000, 12.500, 13.000, 14.500, 16.000, 18.500, 17.000, 20.500, 20.000, ... up to 65.000 (48 modes). The header comment explicitly derives these from "simply-supported square plate ... f_{m,n} = (pi/2)*sqrt(D/(rho*h))*(m^2+n^2)/a^2, normalized ratio = (m^2+n^2)/2" (Leissa NASA SP-160). plate_mapper.h applies them as f[k] = f0*kPlateRatios[k] with f0 = 800*0.1^size, and per-mode strike amplitude A_{m,n} = sin(m*pi*x0)*sin(n*pi*y0) (the SSSS mode shape) with a 0.05 amplitude floor. This same table is reused for cymbal/gong-like "metallic" Plate/Noise bodies (brightness = 0.5+0.5*material; "plates are metallic").
research.code_ref: plugins/membrum/src/dsp/bodies/plate_modes.h:42-111 (ratio table + (m,n) grid), :131-144 (SSSS amplitude); plugins/membrum/src/dsp/bodies/plate_mapper.h:39-53 (f0 and frequency/amplitude mapping)
research.correct_form: For a real cymbal/gong (a thin plate with FREE edges, often non-flat), the modal frequencies do NOT follow the simply-supported sum (m^2+n^2)/2. Rossing's holographic/near-field measurements of cymbals (and Chladni's classical law for flat plates) fit a power law in (m+2n), not a sum of squares: classical flat-plate Chladni's law f[m,n] = C*(m+2n)^2 (m = nodal diameters, n = nodal circles), and the modified form measured on real cymbals f[m,n] = C_n*(m+2n)^P_n with empirically fitted exponents averaging k1 ~= 1.73 and k2 ~= 1.60 (i.e. clearly sub-quadratic and irregular), Kirchhoff showed (m+2n)^2 is only the high-frequency asymptote. Free-edge boundary conditions also introduce rigid-body modes, a lower/denser low-frequency mode set, and increasing modal density with frequency, none of which the SSSS table reproduces. The SSSS ratios the code uses describe a plate hinged/pinned on all four edges (a constrained boundary), which a struck cymbal/gong is not; using them gives a quasi-regular ladder with exact degeneracies and literal repeats (e.g. 12.500 and 25.000 appearing twice, dense integer/half-integer spacing) that is markedly MORE ordered than a real inharmonic cymbal spectrum. A physically faithful Plate body should derive ratios from free-plate eigenvalues (e.g. Leissa free-free tables / Waller's measured free square-plate modes) or a (m+2n)^P Chladni fit, optionally with the dynamic/nonlinear pitch-glide and energy-cascade behavior Fletcher & Rossing describe for cymbals/gongs.

sources.title: Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments (Springer, 2nd ed.), Ch. on plates/cymbals/gongs
sources.says: Cymbals and gongs are inharmonic; cymbals are among the few instruments whose vibrational modes are NOT in simple harmonic ratios; gongs/cymbals exhibit dynamic nonlinearity (pitch glide, energy cascade to high frequencies, transition to chaos) at amplitudes above plate thickness. Free-edge thin-plate behavior, not simply-supported.

sources.title: Rossing & Peterson / Rossing et al., 'Modes of vibration and decay times in cymbals' and 'Vibrational modes of cymbals and Chladni's law' (J. Acoust. Soc. Am.)
sources.says: Measured cymbal modal frequencies fit a modified Chladni's law f[m,n] = C_n*(m+2n)^P_n; principal low-frequency modes fit f = c*m^k with k surprisingly consistent across cymbals, average k1 ~= 1.73, k2 ~= 1.60. ~300 modes measured in a 16-in cymbal by holography/near-field scanning. This is a power law in (m+2n), not (m^2+n^2).

sources.title: Chladni's Law / Kirchhoff asymptotics for flat plates
sources.says: Chladni's law for a circular/flat plate: frequency proportional to (m+2n)^2 (m nodal diameters, n nodal circles); Kirchhoff showed this is only an asymptotic high-frequency relation. Confirms free-plate ratios are governed by (m+2n), distinct from the simply-supported (m^2+n^2) sum.

sources.title: Waller, 'Vibrations of free square plates: Part I, Normal vibrating modes' (Proc. Phys. Soc. 51, 1939)
sources.says: Seminal measurement of FREE square-plate Chladni modes; free-edge square plates have an irregular eigenvalue ladder distinct from the simply-supported (m^2+n^2)/2 sequence, and is the appropriate reference for a struck plate body.

sources.title: Fletcher, 'The nonlinear physics of musical instruments' (Rep. Prog. Phys. 62, 1999)
sources.says: Gongs and cymbals with shallow curvature show pitch glide and energy cascade toward high frequencies dependent on dynamic nonlinearity and chaotic vibration; their resonator modes are not in simple ratios. A linear fixed-ratio modal bank omits the defining nonlinear cymbal/gong behavior.

research.implication: Audible consequence: the Plate/cymbal body is built on a quasi-regular, partly-degenerate ratio ladder (integers and half-integers, with exact repeats like 12.5 and 25.0 and many tied (m,n)/(n,m) pairs) that is far more "ordered" than a real cymbal/gong's irregular inharmonic spectrum. That regularity makes the body read as a generic struck-metal/marimba-ish tone with a pseudo-pitched character rather than a shimmering, dense, chaotic cymbal/gong. Because the ratio table is FIXED, the only timbral knobs are f0 (Size), an overall brightness from Material, a global modeStretch [0.5,2.0]->[0,1], decay and a uniform modeScatter — none of which change the fundamental ratio TOPOLOGY, so different presets land on the same skeletal spectrum scaled/tilted slightly. Combined with the model's lack of the cymbal/gong dynamic nonlinearity (pitch glide / high-frequency cascade) and a 0.05 amplitude floor that forces every mode on regardless of strike position, this is a plausible contributor to the "presets mostly sound the same and boring" symptom for metallic/plate-class kits: the inharmonic fingerprint that distinguishes one cymbal/gong from another is simply not in the parameter space. Caveat: it is a contributor, not the sole cause — downstream homogenizers (per-voice softClip, master energy limiter, 1/sqrt(N) normalize + gain comp) and the membrane bodies share blame; this finding specifically explains why the metallic bodies lack distinct character, not the whole kit.

research.claim_as_coded:
Three claims in the shell/bell code:

(1) SHELL RATIOS (shell_modes.h:36-43, kShellRatios[32]): first 6 = {1.000, 2.757, 5.404, 8.933, 13.344, 18.637}, claimed to be the roots of cos(bL)cosh(bL)=1 for a free-free Euler-Bernoulli beam; modes 7-32 use the asymptotic ratio_k ~= ((2k+1)pi/2 / 4.73)^2. Strike-position amplitude (shell_modes.h:48-57) uses A_k = sin(k*pi*x0/L).

(2) BELL RATIOS (bell_modes.h:44-48, kBellRatios[16]) = {0.250, 0.500, 0.600, 0.750, 1.000, 1.500, 2.000, 2.600, 3.200, 4.000, 5.333, 6.400, 7.333, 8.667, 10.000, 12.000}, normalized so nominal=1.000; first 5 claimed to be hum/prime/tierce/quint/nominal; entries 5..15 are a 'Hibberts big-bell Chladni' extension. Bell amplitude (bell_modes.h:56-65) uses A_k = |cos((k+1)*pi*r/R)|.

(3) SECONDARY/SHELL FUNDAMENTAL (drum_voice.h:1071-1076): f0 = head_f0 * (1 - 0.75*secondarySize), so default secondarySize=0.5 gives shell f0 = 0.625*head_f0, commented as "Chromaphone's 'shell f0 ~0.6 f_head' rule of thumb."
research.correct_form:
(1) SHELL RATIOS — CONFIRMED. I numerically solved cos(bL)cosh(bL)=1: roots bL = 4.7300, 7.8532, 10.9956, 14.1372, 17.2788, 20.4204; frequencies prop to (bL)^2 give ratios 1.000, 2.757, 5.404, 8.933, 13.344, 18.638. The code's 18.637 is just rounding of 18.638; all other values are exact. The asymptotic (2k+1)pi/2 continuation is the textbook high-mode formula. So the shell ratios ARE the free-free Euler-Bernoulli eigenvalue ratios as claimed. (Minor science nit, not the asked question: the strike-amplitude sin(k*pi*x/L) is the simply-supported/pinned mode shape, NOT the true free-free mode shape, which is a sin+sinh-cos-cosh combination; this only affects relative mode excitation, not the ratios.)

(2) BELL RATIOS — first 7 CONFIRMED. Canonical tuned-bell series relative to PRIME=1.0 is hum 0.5, prime 1.0, tierce 1.2, quint 1.5, nominal 2.0, twelfth 3.0, double-octave 4.0 (Strike tone / Rossing). The code normalizes to NOMINAL=1.0, i.e. divides by 2: 0.25, 0.50, 0.60, 0.75, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00 — which is EXACTLY the code's first 7 entries. Tierce=0.60 correctly encodes the minor-third (1.2/2) bell character. So bell ratios match published partials. Entries 8-15 (2.6, 3.2, 4.0, 5.333, ...) are a plausible but not-canonically-named upper-partial extension; the code honestly documents these as approximate.

(3) 'shell f0 ~= 0.6 * f_head' — UNSUPPORTED as a named rule. No textbook, paper, or the Chromaphone manual states a 0.6 head/shell ratio. The Chromaphone 3 manual actually emphasizes the opposite case (coupled resonators are often tuned to the SAME fundamental to amplify it, e.g. vibraphone tube tuned to its bar). 0.6 is a defensible arbitrary design choice but the 'Chromaphone rule of thumb' attribution is a folk claim, not literature. It is harmless physically but the comment overstates its provenance.

sources.title: Euphonics (J. Woodhouse, Cambridge) — 3.2.1 Bending beams and free-free modes
sources.says: Free-free Euler-Bernoulli beam eigenvalues satisfy cos(kL)cosh(kL)=1 with asymptotic kL ~= (n+1/2)pi; frequencies prop to (kL)^2, giving the inharmonic ratio series for free bars (glockenspiel/xylophone class).

sources.title: Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments, ch.2 (Transverse vibrations of a free-free bar) — cited in code
sources.says: Free-free bar overtone ratios are 1 : 2.756 : 5.404 : 8.933 : ... (relative to fundamental); these are the standard published free-bar mode ratios the code reproduces.

sources.title: Strike tone — Wikipedia (table sourced from Rossing/bell acoustics)
sources.says: Tuned church-bell partials relative to prime=1: hum 0.5, prime 1.0, tierce 1.2, quint 1.5, nominal 2.0, twelfth 3.0, double-octave 4.0; nominal:twelfth:double-octave ~ 2:3:4 form the strike note.

sources.title: The Sound of Bells (Hibberts) — Identifying Bell Partials / Five Main Partials
sources.says: The five main partials are hum, prime, tierce, quint, nominal; True-Harmonic tuning gives hum 0.25, prime 0.50, tierce 0.60, quint 0.75, nominal 1.00 (normalized to nominal), matching the code's first five entries; this is the same B. Hibberts source the code cites.

sources.title: AAS Chromaphone 3 user manual — Coupling
sources.says: Describes bidirectional resonator coupling and notes resonators are often tuned to the SAME fundamental to amplify it (vibraphone tube=bar); states NO 0.6 head/shell frequency ratio rule of thumb — the code's '0.6 f_head' attribution is not in the manual.

research.implication: Largely benign for the 'boring/same' symptom — the ratios themselves are correct, so they are NOT a homogenization source. Audible notes: (a) The shell strike-amplitude uses the simply-supported sin(k*pi*x) shape instead of the true free-free mode shape, so the shell partials' relative levels are slightly mis-weighted vs a real free bar — a subtle timbral inaccuracy, not a sameness driver. (b) More relevant to the user symptom: the secondarySize->f0 mapping is hard-clamped to f0 in [0.25, 1.0]*head_f0 (drum_voice.h:1075, factor 0.75), and the secondary/shell bank's damping b1 is forced high (>=25 s^-1, >=2.5x body b1, drum_voice.h:1141) specifically so the shell decays fast and cannot ring. Combined, the shell contributes only a brief attack coloration whose pitch range is narrow; this CAN flatten the perceptual difference between presets that vary secondarySize/secondaryMaterial, because the shell's contribution is short-lived and pitch-restricted regardless of settings. The 0.6 default is fine; the aggressive damping floor + narrow f0 range is the more plausible contributor to presets sounding similar in their shell/coupling character. Recommend treating the shell-damping floor and secondarySize range (not the ratios) as the dynamics-flattening suspects.

research.claim_as_coded: Modal decay is `decayRate_k = b1 + b3*f_w^2` (s^-1), with f_w the per-mode warped frequency in **Hz**, converted to a per-sample radius `R_k = exp(-decayRate_k / sampleRate)` and applied as a Gordon-Smith coupled-form decay `s_new = R*(s+eps*c)+inj`. modal_resonator_bank.h:703-704 (compute), :286-287 (updateDampingLaw), :77 (DampingLaw doc). Coefficient ranges actually reachable: b1 floored at 1/5 s^-1 (line 661); legacy b1=1/decayTime with decayTime clamped [0.01,5] s (line 101-103). Per-mode-damping override path in membrane_mapper.h:63,67 maps normalized [0,1] -> b1 = 0.2 + n*49.8 -> [0.2, 50] s^-1 and b3 = n*1.0e-3 -> [0, 1e-3] s. Legacy brightness path: b3 = (1-brightness)*kMaxB3 with kMaxB3 = 4.0e-5 (line 93,103). Secondary shell bank uses b1 in ~[40,80] s^-1, b3 = mat*1.0e-5 (drum_voice.h:1141-1143). f^2 uses Hz (no Hz->rad/s conversion anywhere), confirmed at lines 286/703.
research.correct_form: The form R_k(decay rate) = b1 + b3*f^2 is exactly the Chaigne & Askenfelt (1994) string loss model. Their string PDE damping terms are 2*b1*(dy/dt) - 2*b3*(d^3 y / dt dx^2); the mixed time-space derivative gives a wavenumber-squared (hence frequency-squared) contribution, so the per-mode decay rate is sigma_k = b1 + b3*omega_k^2 (or equivalently b1 + b3'*f_k^2 in Hz). b1 = global/uniform loss (s^-1), b3 = frequency-squared loss controlling high-frequency roll-off. So the coded formula and its physical interpretation (b1 flat, b3*f^2 high-frequency) are correct and per-mode damping (higher modes decay faster) is modelled correctly via the +b3*f^2 term. Two correctness nuances, neither a bug: (1) The CANONICAL Chaigne form is a second-order POLYNOMIAL in frequency for plates/membranes: sigma(f) = c1 + c2*f + c3*f^2 (Chaigne & Lambourg 2001 "Time-domain simulation of damped impacted plates"), where the LINEAR term models thermoelastic + radiation losses and dominates at low/mid frequency, while the f^2 term is viscoelastic. The code omits the linear (c2*f) term entirely, so it is the string-grade approximation (b1 + b3*f^2), not the full plate/membrane law. (2) The b3 ceiling here (1e-3 s, Hz convention) is ~6.3e6x Chaigne's fitted piano-string value (1.58e-10 s, Hz convention) and ~25x the legacy kMaxB3=4e-5; it is a free perceptual "material brightness" control, not a fitted physical constant -- legitimate for a synth but not literature-derived. t60 implied by the coded ranges is physically plausible for drums: t60 = ln(1000)/decayRate = 6.908/decayRate, so b1 in [0.2,50] gives low-mode t60 of [0.14 s, 35 s] (the comment's "typical body t60 ~= 0.3 s" = b1 ~= 23 s^-1, realistic), and at b3=1e-3 a 1 kHz mode adds 1000 s^-1 -> t60 ~= 7 ms (strong high-frequency damping), correctly faster than low modes.

sources.title: Numerical simulations of piano strings. I. A physical model for a struck string using finite difference methods (Chaigne & Askenfelt, JASA 95(2), 1994)
sources.says: String PDE includes frequency-dependent damping terms 2*b1*(dy/dt) - 2*b3*(d^3 y/dt dx^2); the mixed space-time derivative yields a wavenumber/frequency-squared loss, i.e. per-mode decay rate sigma = b1 + b3*omega^2 -- the origin of the R = b1 + b3*f^2 modal damping form.

sources.title: Time-domain simulation of damped impacted plates. I. Theory and experiments (Chaigne & Lambourg, JASA 109(4), 2001)
sources.says: Plate/membrane damping combines three mechanisms -- thermoelastic, viscoelastic, and radiation -- producing a loss factor that is a polynomial in frequency (constant + linear + quadratic terms), not purely b1 + b3*f^2; the linear term (radiation/thermoelastic) is significant for plates and membranes.

sources.title: Exploring Modal Synthesis (Nathan Ho)
sources.says: Documents the R_k = b1 + b3*f_k^2 modal-synthesis decay model attributed to Chaigne & Askenfelt 1993/1994, with b1 (s^-1) = global decay and b3 (s) = high-frequency damping; cites piano middle-C values b1 = 0.5 s^-1, b3 = 1.58e-10 s (Hz-adjusted), and notes the side-effect that low modes ring far longer than high modes.

sources.title: Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments / Rossing 'Acoustics of Drums'
sources.says: Real drum membranes show strongly mode-dependent damping -- e.g. snare (0,1) mode is heavily damped (decays in a fraction of a second) while higher antisymmetric modes ring longer; per-mode decay differences of an order of magnitude are physical, consistent with the coded t60 spread.

research.implication: The damping LAW is correct and is NOT a primary cause of the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom -- the form is right, per-mode high-frequency damping works, and the parameter ranges span a wide, audible, physically plausible t60 sweep (low-mode 0.14-35 s; b3 gives a ~9 dB high-band drop at the ceiling, per the mapper's own measured contract). Two secondary, non-fatal observations relevant to sameness: (a) Omitting the Chaigne-Lambourg LINEAR (c2*f) term means mid-frequency modes are damped slightly less than a true plate/membrane -- a minor timbral inaccuracy, not a homogenizer. (b) The much larger sameness risk lies DOWNSTREAM of this stage: this damping modulation only stays audible if the per-voice softClip and the bank's output soft-clip/normalize do not pin the modal sum at the ceiling for the whole ring-out (the code comments at modal_resonator_bank.h:110-148 and drum_voice.h:186-193 explicitly acknowledge that the legacy 0.707 clip "silently masks per-mode damping modulation" and that Membrum opts into a transparent path to avoid it). So the damping law itself faithfully carries material/decay differences; whether those differences survive to the output depends on the output-stage/clip settings, which is where a sameness investigation should focus rather than here.

research.claim_as_coded: Non-feedback voices set the body modal bank's output gain to a flat scalar 1/sqrt(N) where N = bank.getNumModes(), and disable the bank's output soft-clip (threshold 0). The secondary shell bank is identical (1/sqrt(ns)). This scalar is applied in ModalResonatorBank::applyOutputStage as `modeSum *= outputGain_` AFTER the N modes are summed — i.e. a single gain on the summed output, INDEPENDENT of the per-mode amplitudes. The in-source comment (drum_voice.h:201-249) justifies this as 'the energy-equalisation norm ... matches the steady-state RMS sum of uncorrelated mode contributions' and cites 'Faust physmodels.lib `:> /(nModes)`' as field-standard. CRITICAL DEAD CODE: the documented amplitude-aware compensation `bodyGainCompensation_` (doc-commented at line 1357-1359 to equal `sum(amplitudes)*0.707`, i.e. the old saturated peak target) and `secondaryGainCompensation_` are HARD-SET to 1.0f in noteOn (lines 251-252) and never assigned anything else anywhere in the source. So the post-bank multiply at lines 676-680 / 701-703 is unreachable, and the bank's own `getInputGainSum()` (= sum of per-mode amplitudes, the canonical `1/sum(amplitudes)` unit-peak ceiling) is never used. Net effect: every body, regardless of its amplitude profile (Membrane sum~0.36, Bell sum~1.0, etc.), is scaled only by 1/sqrt(N) with no amplitude-aware correction.
research.code_ref: plugins/membrum/src/dsp/drum_voice.h:247-257 (1/sqrt(N) set), :251-252 (compensation hard-set to 1.0), :676-703 (dead compensation branch), :1357-1361 (doc comment vs reality); dsp/include/krate/dsp/processors/modal_resonator_bank.h:135 setOutputGain, :163-166 getInputGainSum, :750-755 applyOutputStage flat multiply
research.correct_form: 1/sqrt(N) is the correct energy-equalisation (RMS) norm ONLY for N MUTUALLY UNCORRELATED contributions in steady state: RMS of a sum of decorrelated signals grows as sqrt(sum of variances) ~ sqrt(N), so dividing by sqrt(N) holds long-term loudness roughly constant as mode count changes. That is the regime the code's comment describes, and it is internally consistent for keeping perceived steady-state level flat vs N. BUT a struck membrane/plate/bell does NOT start uncorrelated: at the strike instant all modes are excited essentially IN PHASE, so the transient peak is the COHERENT sum ~ Sum(|a_i|) (~N for comparable amplitudes), not sqrt-sum. Smith/CCRMA (Modal Expansion / PASP) gives the canonical form H(z) = Sum_i G_i * (1+b_i z^-1)/(1 + a_i1 z^-1 + a_i2 z^-2) with NO 1/N or 1/sqrt(N) factor — each G_i is fit individually to the measured peak height; the excitation point sets the per-mode weights and hence the timbre/peak. Faust physmodels.lib (modeInterpRes/modeFilterBank) normalizes the parallel sum by `/(nModes)` = 1/N, NOT 1/sqrt(N); so the code's cited authority actually prescribes a DIFFERENT (stronger, peak-oriented) divisor. The physically faithful choice for a struck body is amplitude/peak-aware: divide by Sum(|a_i|) (== the bank's already-implemented getInputGainSum(), giving unit transient peak) or by N (Faust), NOT a flat 1/sqrt(N) that ignores the per-body amplitude sum. The intended `bodyGainCompensation_ = sum(amplitudes)*0.707` would have partially restored amplitude-awareness but is dead code.

sources.title: Modal Expansion / Modal Synthesis (Physical Audio Signal Processing, J.O. Smith, CCRMA Stanford)
sources.says: Modal synthesis = parallel sum of 2nd-order sections H(z)=Sum_i G_i*(1+b_i z^-1)/(1+a_i1 z^-1+a_i2 z^-2); each section gain G_i is fit individually (least-squares / matched to measured peak height). There is NO normalization by number of modes — gains are per-mode and physically meaningful.

sources.title: faustlibraries/physmodels.lib (GRAME, modeInterpRes/modeFilterBank)
sources.says: The parallel mode bank is normalized as `par(i,...,modeFilter(...)) :> /(nModes)` — i.e. divide the summed output by nModes (1/N), NOT 1/sqrt(N). The code's comment misattributes 1/sqrt(N) to Faust; Faust actually uses 1/N.

sources.title: The Physics of Musical Instruments, 2nd ed. (Fletcher & Rossing, Springer 1998), Ch. on struck plates/membranes/bars
sources.says: A struck percussion body excites its normal modes simultaneously at the strike instant; the partials are launched together (initially phase-aligned) and only decorrelate over time as each mode decays at its own rate. The strike produces a sharp coherent attack transient whose amplitude reflects the in-phase superposition of excited modes, with relative mode weights set by the strike position via the mode shapes.

sources.title: Coherent vs incoherent (random-phase) summation of N sinusoids — standard result
sources.says: Coherent in-phase sum of N equal-amplitude sinusoids has peak ~N (grows linearly); incoherent/random-phase sum has RMS ~sqrt(N) (grows as square root). 1/sqrt(N) equalizes the incoherent-RMS case; 1/N (or 1/sum|a_i|) is required to bound the coherent in-phase transient peak.

research.implication: Audible consequences, and yes this plausibly contributes to the 'presets sound the same / boring' symptom: (1) Loudness flattening across mode count: 1/sqrt(N) holds steady-state RMS roughly constant as N varies, so presets that differ mainly in mode count produce near-identical perceived level — a deliberate sameness. (2) Transient/attack homogenization: because the strike is a coherent in-phase event with peak ~Sum|a_i| ~ N but the divisor is only sqrt(N), bodies with many modes get a relatively HOTTER, fuller transient than sqrt(N) accounts for; downstream this hits the per-voice softClip(shaped*env*level) (hard [-1,1] bound) and the master energy limiter, which clip/compress the attack of louder/denser presets harder than sparser ones — equalizing their attack envelopes and crushing the very transient cues that distinguish drum timbres. (3) Lost amplitude-aware differentiation: the dead bodyGainCompensation_ (= sum(amplitudes)*0.707) and unused getInputGainSum() mean the per-body amplitude profile (Membrane 0.36 vs Bell 1.0) is NOT reflected in output level — so two presets with very different modal amplitude distributions are normalized to similar levels, removing a major source of timbral/loudness contrast between presets. Net: the flat 1/sqrt(N) plus dead compensation collapses per-preset loudness and attack differences and, in combination with the downstream softClip + master limiter, is a credible mechanism for presets converging toward a generic, similarly-loud, similarly-blunted sound. Fix direction: either restore the amplitude-aware compensation (use getInputGainSum() for a 1/sum|a_i| unit-peak ceiling) or adopt Faust's 1/N, and verify the attack peak isn't being silently clipped/limited downstream.

research.claim_as_coded: Head<->shell coupling lives entirely in drum_voice.h (coupling_matrix.h is a separate inter-pad sympathetic-resonance system, kMaxCoefficient=0.05, unrelated). At noteOn, when secondaryEnabled_>=0.5 AND couplingStrength_>0, effectiveCoupling_ = clamp(couplingStrength_,0,1)*0.25 (hard 0.25 ceiling, stabilityClampedCoupling(), drum_voice.h:1153-1155). In processBlockFast (drum_voice.h:691-734): secExc[i]=bodyScratch[i]*effectiveCoupling_ feeds the secondary (shell) modal bank, shell output is gain-compensated, then ADDED to bodyScratch (bodyScratch[i]+=secOut[i]). It is explicitly FEEDFORWARD ONLY (body->shell input; shell output only mixed to audible out, never returned to the body's modal excitation). The comment justifies dropping the former closed loop with the heuristic: peak loop gain = coupling^2 * 0.5 * Q_body * Q_shell, which with Q_body~30, Q_shell~6, max coupling 0.25 gave ~5 >> 1, causing self-oscillation ('continuous beep'). configureSecondaryBank() additionally forces shell b1 = max(25, 2.5*bodyB1)+(1-mat)*15 so shell t60 <= ~40% of body t60 (Q=omega/(2*b1)). Coupling is OPT-IN / default OFF (default_kit.h:76, pad_config.h:271 couplingStrength=0, secondaryEnabled=0). The slow path (processBlockSlow, FeedbackExciter, drum_voice.h ~853-887) processes NO secondary bank at all -- head<->shell coupling is entirely absent there, so the two block paths are NOT perceptually equivalent when coupling is enabled.
research.correct_form: The passivity argument's GOAL is correct (a coupled modal system must be passive / net energy-dissipative to be stable; Bank & Karjalainen 2010, Bilbao 2009), but the implementation's CONCLUSION -- 'therefore drop the return path and make coupling feedforward-only' -- is not what the literature prescribes and is not what real drum head<->shell (or string<->body) coupling is. Real coupling is BIDIRECTIONAL: Fletcher & Rossing model the two lowest modes of a two-headed drum with a coupled two-mass system, and general coupled-oscillator theory shows coupling lifts degeneracy -> normal-mode frequency SPLITTING and slow inter-resonator energy exchange (beating). Those two signatures (mode splitting and beating) are intrinsic to two-way coupling and CANNOT be produced by feedforward injection, which merely superimposes an independently-ringing shell on top of an unperturbed body. The correct stable form is a PASSIVE reciprocal coupling: a symmetric (reciprocal) coupling operator with the coupling-energy term bounded so the coupled state matrix remains dissipative (eigenvalues strictly inside the unit circle). Concretely, the proper stability criterion is passivity/positive-realness of the coupled admittance (a coupling that only exchanges, never injects, energy), per Bank & Karjalainen. The code's own quoted heuristic 'coupling^2*0.5*Q_body*Q_shell<1' is a hand-derived loop-gain estimate, not a cited passivity result; it is a reasonable conservative bound for the feedforward case but is not the literature's stability condition for a bidirectional model. A correctly-built passive bidirectional coupling at coupling<=0.25 with the chosen Q's would be stable WITHOUT deleting the return path -- the 'continuous beep' was a symptom of an ACTIVE (non-passive, energy-injecting) coupling, not proof that bidirectional coupling is inherently unstable.

sources.title: Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments (2nd ed., Springer)
sources.says: The low-frequency vibrations of two-headed drums couple and are correctly described by a coupled two-mass (bidirectional) model; the coupled modes split in frequency. Real drum head/shell/air coupling is two-way, not one-directional.

sources.title: Bank & Karjalainen, 'Passive Admittance Matrix Modeling for Guitar Synthesis', DAFx 2010
sources.says: For coupled string-body synthesis the fitted coupling/admittance model must be PASSIVE (energy-dissipative); otherwise the coupled instrument model is unstable. The stability requirement is passivity of the coupling operator, not removal of the feedback path.

sources.title: Bilbao, Numerical Sound Synthesis (Wiley, 2009)
sources.says: Coupled modal/finite-difference instrument models are kept stable by enforcing passivity (a discrete energy balance with non-increasing total energy); a properly passive bidirectional coupling is provably stable.

sources.title: Two Coupled Oscillators (Springer Nature Link chapter) and standard coupled-oscillator theory
sources.says: Coupling between two resonators lifts degeneracy producing symmetric/antisymmetric normal modes with split frequencies, and weak coupling with one resonator initially excited yields slow periodic two-way energy exchange (beating). These are signatures of bidirectional coupling.

research.implication: Audibly, feedforward-only coupling loses the two most characteristic products of real head<->shell interaction: (1) normal-mode frequency SPLITTING (the slightly detuned doublets that give a real drum its lively, complex pitch) and (2) BEATING / slow energy exchange between head and shell that animates the decay. With feedforward, the shell is just an independently-decaying mode set summed on top of an unperturbed body, so changing secondarySize/secondaryMaterial/couplingStrength only adds a static ringing layer rather than re-shaping the body's own modes -- a much smaller, more generic timbral move. This DIRECTLY supports the 'presets sound the same and boring' symptom IN PRINCIPLE, but with an important caveat that limits real-world blame: coupling is OPT-IN and default OFF in the factory kit (pad_config.h couplingStrength=0, secondaryEnabled=0; default_kit.h:76), so for any preset that leaves it off the stage contributes nothing at all -- it can neither homogenize nor enliven. Where it IS enabled, it cannot flatten dynamics (it is additive/passive), but it fails to deliver the distinctive, parameter-responsive character a bidirectional model would, so it under-differentiates presets rather than actively homogenizing them. Secondary concrete bug: the slow/FeedbackExciter path processes no secondary bank, so enabling coupling changes timbre on the fast path but not the slow path -- the two paths are not perceptually equivalent, which can make a 'coupled' preset sound inconsistent depending on exciter choice.

research.claim_as_coded: drum_voice.h applies an energy-driven pitch glide. At noteOn: tensionAmtEffective_ = clamp(tensionModAmt_,0,1) * 0.15f * vClamp * vClamp where vClamp = clamp(velocity,0,1), so the effective depth is proportional to velocity^2 (line 314-316). energyEnv_ is a one-pole follower of the squared final voice output sample s*s with tau = 20 ms: energyAlpha_ = exp(-1/(0.02*sr)) (line 97), env = alpha*env + (1-alpha)*s*s (line 797). The pitch multiplier is tensionPitchMod = 1.0f + tensionAmtEffective_ * energyEnv_ (line 599-601), applied as a multiplicative scale on the membrane fundamental (pitchHz = naturalFundamentalHz_ * tensionPitchMod, line 619; or multiplied onto the pitch-envelope output, line 606). Because energyEnv_ >= 0, the multiplier is always >= 1, so pitch jumps UP at the strike (when the energy follower peaks) and relaxes back toward 1.0 (nominal) as body energy decays. The 0.15 ceiling caps the maximum multiplier near 1.15 (~2.4 semitones) when amt=1 and energyEnv_~1, with realistic peaks lower because the 20 ms follower lags the transient. NOTE: this whole block lives only in processBlockFast; processBlockSlow (FeedbackExciter path, line 813-890) contains NO tension code at all -- no tensionPitchMod, no energyEnv_ update -- so the two block paths are NOT perceptually equivalent for tension modulation.
research.correct_form: Qualitatively correct and well-grounded. The literature (Avanzini, Marogna & Bank, JASA 131(1):897-906, 2012) establishes that the short-time-average (quasistatic) tension variation responsible for pitch glides is approximately PROPORTIONAL TO THE SYSTEM ENERGY -- exactly the relation the code uses (deltaT_avg ~ k*E, with energyEnv_ as the energy estimate). Direction is correct: large-amplitude vibration raises mean tension -> higher pitch at the strike, then exponential relaxation to nominal as energy decays (CCRMA/Smith, Physical Audio Signal Processing, 'Tension Modulation'; same JASA result). Energy ~ velocity^2 is correct kinetic-energy scaling, so depth ~ velocity^2 is physically sound. TWO nuances/oversimplifications: (1) Frequency depends on tension as f ~ sqrt(T) (membrane/string), so the true pitch ratio is sqrt(1 + k*E), i.e. Delta f/f ~ (1/2)(Delta T/T). The code uses a first-order LINEAR map 1 + amt*energyEnv instead of sqrt(1 + ...). For small shifts these agree (sqrt(1+x) ~ 1 + x/2), so within the <=2 semitone budget the linearization is a fine small-signal approximation; it would slightly over-sharpen only at large energy. The absolute 0.15 ceiling is an arbitrary perceptual cap, not a calibrated physical constant -- real tom/timpani glides are typically a few hundred cents at most, so ~2 semitones is a reasonable, conservative upper bound, but it is tuned-by-ear, not derived from membrane T0/thickness. (2) The energy follower is driven by the final post-softClip voice output (shaped*env*level) rather than the membrane's modal/vibrational energy, and it is used one block late; this couples the glide to amp envelope and drive/clip rather than to true membrane energy -- a reasonable proxy but not the paper's modal-energy estimate. (3) Real bug, not just science: tension modulation is entirely missing from processBlockSlow, so FeedbackExciter voices get no glide while all other exciters do.

sources.title: Efficient synthesis of tension modulation in strings and membranes based on energy estimation (Avanzini, Marogna, Bank)
sources.says: JASA 131(1):897-906, 2012. The short-time average of the tension variation, which is responsible for pitch glides, is approximately proportional to the system energy; this proportionality is the basis of an efficient pitch-glide synthesis for strings and tom-tom-like membranes. Confirms tension ~ energy as coded.

sources.title: Energy Based Synthesis of Tension Modulation in Membranes (Marogna, Avanzini, Bank, DAFx)
sources.says: Above a certain amplitude membrane vibration is nonlinear due to surface-tension variation, producing audible pitch glides characteristic of tom-toms; the quasistatic tension is modeled as linearly related to membrane energy because energy is cheap to compute.

sources.title: Tension Modulation - Physical Audio Signal Processing (J.O. Smith, CCRMA)
sources.says: Higher-amplitude vibrations stretch the string to a longer average length, raising average tension -> faster wave propagation -> higher fundamental frequency, which then relaxes to nominal as the vibration decays. Confirms pitch-UP-then-relax direction and the f-increases-with-tension mechanism.

sources.title: The Physics of Musical Instruments (Fletcher & Rossing), drums/membranes & nonlinear behavior
sources.says: Membrane fundamental rises with tension (f ~ sqrt(T/sigma)); pitch glide / high-frequency energy increase observed at high excitation amplitude for bass drum and tom-toms due to nonlinear tension increase. Grounds the f ~ sqrt(T) dependence that makes the code's linear-in-energy map a small-signal approximation.

research.implication: For the tension feature in isolation: the model is physically faithful in mechanism, direction, and velocity^2 energy scaling, so it is NOT a primary cause of the generic/boring preset complaint -- when enabled it adds the correct velocity-dependent UP-then-relax glide that makes hard hits sound sharper and livelier. HOWEVER it can contribute to the sameness in three indirect ways: (a) it is gated behind tensionAmtEffective_ > 1e-6, which requires both a nonzero per-pad tensionModAmt_ AND nonzero velocity; if presets leave tensionModAmt_ at its 0.0 default the glide never fires, removing one of the few velocity-expressive, per-preset-distinctive behaviors -- so presets played at fixed velocity lose an obvious source of variation. (b) The 0.15 hard ceiling plus the linearization means even at max knob and max velocity the shift saturates near ~2 semitones with little spread between presets, flattening one expressive axis. (c) The slow-path omission means any preset that selects FeedbackExciter sounds different (no glide) from the same preset on other exciters, and more importantly gets zero benefit from this expressive feature -- but this is a fast/slow consistency bug, not a sameness cause. Net: enable and expose tensionModAmt_ in presets to recover expressivity; the science itself is sound and the linear/sqrt and energy-proxy simplifications are audibly minor within the 2-semitone budget.

research.claim_as_coded: Membrane mapper codes amplitude[k]=abs(J_m(j_mn*strikePos*0.9)) (membrane_mapper.h:101-108): the radial part of the circular-membrane mode shape, with r/a spanning [0,0.9] = correct center-to-edge node suppression. Plate codes A=sin(m*pi*x0)*sin(n*pi*y0) but clamps x0 to [0.35,0.65], y0 to [0.28,0.58] and floors amps at 0.05 (plate_modes.h:131-144). Bell uses abs(cos((k+1)*pi*r)) (ad-hoc, not a bell mode shape). String forwards pickPosition directly.
research.correct_form: Modal amplitude is proportional to mode shape at the strike point. Circular membrane: J_m(lambda_mn*r/a)*cos(m*theta); radial weight J_m(j_mn*r/a) matches the membrane mapper, so membrane is physically correct. Square plate A_mn=sin(pi*m*x)*sin(pi*n*y) is correct as a formula; defect is the narrow +-0.15 strike-coordinate mapping plus 0.05 floor, which prevents the comb from sweeping nodes/antinodes. Bell cosine is not derivable from bell physics.
research.implication: Membrane (default body) correctly reproduces center-vs-edge timbre, so Strike Position works there and is not a sameness cause. Plate is an offender: the +-0.15 clamp and 0.05 floor make Strike Position barely change the excited modes, so plate/noise-plate presets differing only in strikePos sound nearly identical and lack rim-vs-centre contrast, contributing to the boring/same symptom. Fix: widen plate x0/y0 toward [0.05,0.95] and remove the 0.05 floor; membrane needs no change.

sources.title: Vibration of a circular membrane (Wikipedia)
sources.says: Mode shape J_m(lambda_mn*r)*(cos/sin m theta); for m>=1 J_m(0)=0 so a centre strike cannot excite nonzero-angular-order modes.

sources.title: The evolution of drum modes with strike intensity, JASA 150(1):202
sources.says: Modal amplitude depends on modal surface amplitude at strike position; rectangular weight A=sin(pi m x)sin(pi n y).

sources.title: The Physics of Musical Instruments, Fletcher and Rossing
sources.says: Membrane modes J_m(k r)cos(m theta); plate modes products of sines; struck-mode amplitude set by mode shape at contact point; bell partials are 2D shell modes.

sources.title: Modal Synthesis, Bilbao (CCRMA)
sources.says: Point-excitation contribution to each mode is the projection of input onto that modal shape at the strike location.

research.claim_as_coded: Membrum's exciters/*.h are thin wrappers; the physics lives in two core processors. (A) IMPACT (mallet+impulse), dsp/include/krate/dsp/processors/impact_exciter.h: the excitation is a parametric raised-sine pulse, NOT a physical contact model. Pulse = amplitude*pow(sin(pi*t^(1-skew)), gamma), with gamma=1+3*effectiveHardness (line 124), skew=0.3*hardness (line 127), amplitude=pow(velocity,0.6) (line 130). Contact duration T=kTMin(0.5ms)+(kTMax(15ms)-kTMin)*pow(mass,0.4), then T*=pow(max(1-velocity,0.01),0.2) (lines 133-140) -- comment labels this 'Hertzian scaling' but it is a direct lerp, not derived from any force-compression law. Spectral content is imposed by an SVF lowpass: cutoff=500*pow(24,hardness)*exp2(brightness)*exp2(velocity*1.5) (lines 166-176), not emergent from contact dynamics. There is no f=k*x^alpha contact force, no penetration-depth/mass ODE, no collision integration. mallet_exciter.h / impulse_exciter.h only remap hardness/brightness lerps into this same backend. (B) FRICTION, dsp/include/krate/dsp/processors/bow_exciter.h: reflectionCoeff = clamp(1/x^4, 0.01, 0.98) with x=|deltaV*slope+offset|+0.75 and slope=clamp(5-4*pressure,1,10) (lines 313-319). force=deltaV*reflectionCoeff (line 322). This is exactly the STK/Smith bow table.
research.correct_form: FRICTION exciter is literature-faithful (CONFIRMED): clamp((|deltaV*slope+offset|+0.75)^-4, ...,1) is verbatim STK BowTable, where STK does samples=(|in+offset|*slope)+0.75; samples=pow(samples,-4); if(>1)=1. Membrum's 1/x^4 == pow(x,-4), and slope=5-4*pressure matches STK's documented (5-4p) bow-force map. This memoryless friction table descends from Smith (1986) / McIntyre-Schumacher-Woodhouse and is the canonical DWG bow model (acknowledged simplification vs thermal/hysteretic friction, but standard). IMPACT exciter is NOT a physical contact model (OVERSIMPLIFIED): a true mallet-membrane model (Avanzini & Rocchesso; Hunt-Crossley) uses f(x)=k*x^alpha + lambda*x^alpha*xdot with alpha~1.5 (Hertz/hard) up to ~2-3.3 (stiffening felt), where contact time, restitution and spectrum EMERGE from integrating the mallet point-mass against the modal resonator. Membrum instead imposes a curve-fit raised-sine and an SVF. IMPORTANTLY the qualitative mappings are still physically CORRECT in sign: higher velocity -> shorter contact (line 139 exponent 0.2, lit: tau decreases with v) and higher centroid (line 172, lit: centroid rises with v); heavier mass -> longer contact (line 135, lit: confirmed). So it is a defensible perceptual emulation, not contradicted -- but it cannot reproduce velocity-dependent contact stiffening, nonlinear mallet 'pitch glide' on hard hits, or true coupling between strike force and the body, because the exciter never reads back resonator state (process() ignores feedbackVelocity for impact).

sources.title: F. Avanzini & D. Rocchesso, 'Physical modeling of impacts: theory and experiments on contact time and spectral centroid' (SMC 2004)
sources.says: Contact force is a power law of compression f=k*x^alpha (Hertz alpha~1.5 for hard, lower/with-hysteresis for felt); contact time DECREASES with impact velocity and spectral centroid INCREASES with velocity; heavier mallet mass gives LONGER contact. These are emergent from the nonlinear collision, not imposed.

sources.title: Avanzini & Rocchesso, 'Modeling collision sounds: Non-linear contact force' (DAFx-01)
sources.says: Foundational nonlinear stick/mallet-membrane contact model: colliding objects are modal resonators interacting through a nonlinear impact force = elastic power-law term plus dissipative term proportional to compression velocity; the mallet is a point mass with inertia.

sources.title: Hunt & Crossley contact force model (review: 'Celebrating 50 years...', Mech. Mach. Theory 2025)
sources.says: Extends Hertz with a nonlinear velocity-dependent damper; force exponent n is not fixed at 1.5 and ranges ~1-2 (higher for stiffening contacts like felt). Originally developed for hammer impacts.

sources.title: stk::BowTable Class Reference (CCRMA / Synthesis ToolKit)
sources.says: Exact bow table code: samples=(samples+offset)*slope; samples=fabs(samples)+0.75; samples=pow(samples,-4.0); if(samples>1.0)samples=1.0; -- identical (modulo the 0.01..0.98 clamp) to Membrum's 1/x^4 reflection coefficient.

sources.title: J.O. Smith, 'Bowed Strings', Physical Audio Signal Processing (CCRMA)
sources.says: Bow-string interaction modeled as a memoryless reflection-coefficient table; slope corresponds to bow force, offset biases friction symmetry; explicitly a simplification of true stick-slip (neglects bow-hair dynamics, time-varying viscosity, thermodynamic friction).

sources.title: J. Woodhouse, 'Bowed string simulation using a thermal friction model'
sources.says: The velocity-dependent friction-curve (Friedlander/McIntyre-Woodhouse) bow model has an ambiguity/hysteresis issue; a thermal friction model is more physically accurate and yields more reliable Helmholtz motion -- i.e. the memoryless 1/x^4 table is a known simplification.

research.implication: Audible consequence: the friction model is fine and characterful. The impact model loses two physical behaviors that normally make struck-instrument presets feel distinct: (1) velocity-dependent contact stiffening (the 'attack gets sharper AND pitch/centroid glides up on a hard hit') is only approximated by an SVF cutoff sweep, so material 'feel' across velocity is blander than a true contact model; (2) the impact never couples back to the body (process() ignores feedbackVelocity), so strike timbre does not change with where/what it hits. This is a MINOR contributor to the 'presets sound the same/boring' symptom -- the exciter contrast is real but gets flattened. A more direct contributor inside this file is the energy cap (lines 298-303): energyThreshold_ is computed ONCE in prepare() from fixed default params (line 80, hardness 0.3 / mass 0.3), so loud or hard-tuned strikes are normalized back toward the same energy, compressing the velocity->loudness and velocity->brightness range that should separate presets. But the dominant homogenizers are downstream (per-voice softClip on shaped*env*level, master energy limiter, 1/sqrt(N) normalize+gain comp), which are outside this exciter-correctness topic and should be audited there.

research.claim_as_coded: Every DrumVoice output path ends with softClip(shaped*env*level_) (drum_voice.h:446, :769, :782, :885). softClip (dsp_utils.h:105-112) is a rational tanh approx: for |x|<3, y=x*(27+x*x)/(27+9*x*x), saturating to +/-1 for |x|>=3, then clamped. Slope at origin=1 but it compresses below the knee: x=1.0->0.778 (-2.2 dB), x=2.0->0.939 (-6.6 dB). Inputs: level_ in [0,1] (raw normalized param, default 0.8, not a calibrated trim), env<=velocity, and shaped = post-ToneShaper signal AFTER drive+wavefolder, routinely >1. So it is an always-on fixed-curve waveshaper at the hottest point of the chain. Comments (drum_voice.h:209-216,234-246) cite Faust :>/(nModes), STK Modal, Bilbao NSS to justify removing the modal bank's per-mode clip (correct) but lean on this voice softClip as the bound -- yet those sources do not apply a tanh at the bank/voice output.
research.correct_form: The cited field-standard practice does NOT put a tanh on the modal output. STK Modal::tick() applies only linear masterGain_ plus a directGain_ dry/wet mix. Faust physmodels.lib sums modes (:>) and normalizes by mode count /(nModes) with per-mode modeGain only -- no tanh/limiter. Smith's CCRMA framework presents soft clipping (cubic x-x^3/3) as a DELIBERATE distortion with an INPUT GAIN to set distortion degree, not a transparent safety limiter. Bilbao NSS treats modal/FDTD banks as linear and BIBO-safe (|pole|<1), reserving nonlinearity for modeled physics. Correct designs: (a) keep the per-voice output linear and bound the SUM downstream (true-peak/brickwall limiter or the existing master energy limiter); or (b) make any saturation an OPTIONAL, gain-staged drive control (input-gain driven, per Smith), not a hidden always-on curve. A per-voice clip tied to level/velocity is acceptable only if the operating point stays near-linear (|x| well under ~0.5); here shaped is post-wavefolder/drive and routinely >1, so the curve is engaged in normal use.
research.implication: Plausible contributor to the presets-sound-the-same/boring symptom. softClip has unity slope at 0 but compresses progressively, and shaped is taken AFTER drive+wavefolder, so it is regularly driven into the 0.7-2.0 region where the curve gives -2 to -7 dB of reduction, acting as a fixed-curve per-voice compressor/waveshaper. Two flattening effects: (1) Dynamics crush -- loud transients are rounded by the same fixed knee regardless of preset, homogenizing attack contrast and velocity feel; tests/unit/diagnose/test_orchestral_distortion.cpp and the note at membrum_processor_tests.cpp:485 observe this rounding. (2) Timbral homogenization -- a static tanh adds the same odd-harmonic signature to every driven voice, so presets with different modal content converge to a common saturated tone. The threshold is tied to level_/velocity (a level control), not a headroom trim, so cutting preset level does not escape the curve shape -- it only rescales where on the curve you sit, matching the report that distortion does not respond to preset-level cuts. Fix: keep the per-voice output linear and bound the summed bus with a true-peak/brickwall limiter (or the master energy limiter); expose any saturation as an explicit input-gain-driven drive control. Verdict nuanced (not contradicted) because modal params, normalization, and the master limiter also affect sameness, but the always-on tanh at the voice output is contrary to STK/Faust/Bilbao practice and is a real dynamics/timbre flattener.

sources.title: thestk/stk Modal.h (Synthesis ToolKit, Cook/Scavone, CCRMA)
sources.says: Modal output is masterGain_*onepole(...) summed over filters_[i]->tick(temp) with directGain_ dry/wet mix; no tanh/soft-clip/saturation on the bank output.

sources.title: Faust physmodels.lib (Grame/CCRMA, Michon and Smith)
sources.says: modeFilter*gain and modeInterpRes sum modes with :> then normalize /(nModes); no tanh/saturation/limiter on the modal bank output.

sources.title: Julius O. Smith III, Soft Clipping (Nonlinear Methods, CCRMA)
sources.says: Soft clipping (cubic x-x^3/3) is a deliberate distortion; an input gain sets the desired degree of distortion -- an effect, not a transparent output limiter.

sources.title: Stefan Bilbao, Numerical Sound Synthesis (Wiley, 2009)
sources.says: Linear modal/FDTD synthesis is inherently bounded/stable (|pole|<1); nonlinearities model real physics, not a blanket output saturator -- a linear modal bank needs no output tanh for stability.

sources.title: The Faust Physical Modeling Library (Michon and Smith, ICMC 2017)
sources.says: Modal percussion models output the normalized modal sum directly; saturation, when present, is an explicit separate nonlinear element.

research.claim_as_coded: membrane_modes.h defines kAirLoadingTargetScale (per-mode multipliers at airLoading=1) and membrane_mapper.h applies f_k = f0 * kMembraneRatios[k] * (1 + airLoading*(targetScale[k]-1)). At airLoading=1 the five (m,1) modes land on the Rossing series RELATIVE TO (1,1): index1 (1,1)=1.5933*1.0; index2 (2,1)=2.1357*1.1192=2.390; index4 (3,1)=2.6528*1.2012=3.186; index6 (4,1)=3.1554*1.2321=3.888; index9 (5,1)=3.6475*1.2668=4.621. Dividing by the (1,1) value 1.5933 gives exactly 1.000 : 1.500 : 2.000 : 2.440 : 2.900 — the claimed series. The (0,1) anchor at index 0 and (1,1) at index 1 are held at pure Bessel (scale 1.0); all n>=2 modes and (m>=6,1) modes are held at unity (no shift). The code comment states the mechanism as: air-mass loading depresses (m>=1,1) modes and raises the axisymmetric (0,n) modes.
research.correct_form: The near-harmonic series for the air-loaded timpani (m,1) modes IS 1 : 1.50 : 2.00 : 2.44 : 2.90 for (1,1):(2,1):(3,1):(4,1):(5,1) relative to (1,1) — exactly Rossing's published values. The code reproduces this exactly. Mechanism nuance (not an error, a minor attribution refinement): air-MASS loading lowers the diametric (m>=1,1) modes; the RAISING of the axisymmetric (0,n) concentric modes is driven by the STIFFNESS (and viscothermal) behavior of the enclosed kettle air, not by mass loading per se. The code comment lumps both effects under 'air loading,' which is correct as a net description but slightly conflates the two distinct physical sub-mechanisms. Also a documented simplification: real air loading lowers ALL diametric modes (including n>=2), whereas the table only shifts the first five (m,1) modes and holds everything else at unity — the comment honestly discloses this.

sources.title: Euphonics (J. Woodhouse), 3.6 Tuned drums
sources.says: Air mass loading increases the effective mass per unit area of the membrane, lowering natural frequencies; the added-mass effect differs per mode and therefore changes the frequency ratios. Confirms the mass-loading-lowers-frequencies mechanism for diametric modes.

sources.title: The Well-Tempered Timpani — Air Loading / Significant Studies (summarizing Rossing et al. 1976,1977,1982,1998,2000)
sources.says: Air loading produces a considerable decrease in the frequency of the lower (diametric) modes and a small effect on higher modes; the proper combination yields near-harmonic preferred modes ~1:1.5:2:2.5:3, attributed to Rossing.

sources.title: Rossing et al., timpani acoustics summary (mode-ratio table)
sources.says: Modes (1,1),(2,1),(3,1),(4,1),(5,1) are respectively in frequency ratios 1, 1.5, 2, 2.44 (almost 2.5) and 2.9 (almost 3) relative to (1,1) — exactly the series coded.

sources.title: Effects of air loading on timpani membrane vibrations (JASA 76(5):1336)
sources.says: Air mass (inside+outside bowl) lowers the diametric preferred modes, while the stiffness/viscothermal properties of the enclosed kettle air RAISE the concentric (0,n) modes — the differential mechanism the code comment describes.

research.implication: No audible bug. The air-loading model is scientifically correct and faithfully reproduces Rossing's published 1:1.50:2.00:2.44:2.90 series, so it does NOT contribute to the 'presets sound the same/boring' symptom — on the contrary, sweeping airLoading 0->1 is one of the few stages that should produce a clearly audible timbral change (pure-membrane gong-like inharmonic -> tuned near-harmonic timpani pitch). Two caveats for whoever chases the sameness symptom: (1) the shift only engages when the airLoading parameter actually reaches the DSP and varies across presets — verify the param is denormalized and wired, otherwise the model's expressive range is unused; (2) because only 5 of 48 modes are shifted and the (0,1)/(1,1) anchors are fixed to preserve the Size->f0 mapping, the overall pitch/level is intentionally stable across airLoading, so the change is timbral, not a gross loudness/pitch move — that is correct behavior, not a flattening defect.

research.claim_as_coded: computeModeCoefficients() applies stiff-string inharmonicity as f_w = f_k * sqrt(1 + B*k*k) with B = stretch*stretch*0.001f, where k is the 0-based loop/array index over frequencies[] (k = 0,1,2,...). frequencies[k] already carries the modal frequency (the n*f0 term) from the per-body ratio tables. So for the fundamental (k=0) the stretch factor is sqrt(1+0)=1, and each successive partial uses the index k rather than the physical partial number. scatter adds a separate sinusoidal dither C=scatter*0.10 (not part of this claim).
research.code_ref: dsp/include/krate/dsp/processors/modal_resonator_bank.h:670 (B = stretch*stretch*0.001f) and :687 (f_w = f_k * sqrt(1.0f + B * k * k)); loop opens at :673 with k starting at 0
research.correct_form: Standard stiff-string / piano inharmonicity (Fletcher 1964; Young 1952; Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments): f_n = n*f0*sqrt(1 + B*n^2) with the partial number n starting at 1 for the fundamental. The per-partial stretch multiplier is therefore sqrt(1 + B*n^2) applied to the harmonic frequency n*f0. Two issues vs the code: (1) FUNCTIONAL FORM sqrt(1+B*n^2) is CORRECT, and B scaling as the square of a stiffness/stretch control is physically defensible (B is proportional to bending stiffness E*I/(T*L^2), and mapping a perceptual knob quadratically to B is a legitimate UX choice). (2) INDEXING is off by one: the code uses the 0-based array index k as the partial number, so it computes sqrt(1+B*k^2) instead of sqrt(1+B*(k+1)^2). Literature: fundamental (n=1) should be stretched by sqrt(1+B); code leaves k=0 unstretched. Partial 2 should use n=2 (sqrt(1+4B)) but code uses k=1 (sqrt(1+B)); partial 10 should use sqrt(1+100B) but code uses sqrt(1+81B). The dispersion is systematically under-applied and skewed toward the lower partials. (Note the bow-weight code at :608 correctly uses (k+1) as the partial number, confirming k is 0-based and the stretch law's bare k is the anomaly.) Separately, the magnitude ceiling B_max=0.001 is far below real piano treble values (B up to ~0.4) and even below typical bass strings (~0.0002-0.001), so even at stretch=1 the audible stretch is very subtle by design.

sources.title: Russell (Penn State Acoustics), Inharmonicity due to Stiffness for Guitar/Piano Strings
sources.says: Gives f_n = n*f_o*sqrt(1 + B*n^2) for a stiff string with pinned ends and states explicitly that n starts at 1 for the fundamental, so the fundamental itself is stretched by sqrt(1+B).

sources.title: Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments (stiff-string inharmonicity)
sources.says: Stiff string spectrum f_n proportional to n*f_1*sqrt(1 + B*n^2); B (inharmonicity coefficient) is proportional to bending stiffness E*I/(T*L^2), i.e. scales with stiffness, supporting a stiffness-squared control mapping being reasonable.

sources.title: Young, Inharmonicity of Plain Wire Piano Strings (1952)
sources.says: Establishes the inharmonicity relation and that B = (pi^3 E d^4)/(64 T L^2); typical piano B ranges ~0.0002 (bass) to ~0.4 (treble), well above the code's B_max of 0.001.

sources.title: The wave equation for stiff strings and piano tuning (arXiv:1603.05516)
sources.says: Derives the stiff-string quadratic-dispersion correction giving the spectrum f_n proportional to n*f_1*sqrt(1+B*n^2), corroborating the sqrt(1+B*n^2) functional form with n the partial index.

research.implication: Functionally the sqrt(1+B*n^2) form and the quadratic B-from-stretch mapping are correct/defensible, so this is not a gross physics error. But two effects make the Stretch knob audibly weak and contribute to the "presets sound the same/boring" symptom: (1) the 0-based index off-by-one under-stretches every partial (partial m gets the dispersion of partial m-1) and leaves the fundamental unstretched, flattening and mistuning the inharmonic dispersion that gives bars/bells/stiff drums their characteristic timbral identity; (2) B is hard-capped at 0.001, an order(s) of magnitude below real piano/bar values, so even at stretch=1 the spectral spread is tiny. Combined, the Stretch parameter moves partials only fractionally and incorrectly, so presets that differ only in Stretch (and to a lesser extent the related mode layout) converge toward the same near-harmonic spectrum -- consistent with timbres that fail to separate. Fixing the index to (k+1) and widening B_max toward physically realistic values would restore audible, correctly-shaped inharmonicity. Verdict nuanced: form correct, index wrong, range conservative.

research.claim_as_coded: computeShellAmplitude(modeIdx, strikePos) returns sin((modeIdx+1)*pi*strikePos), i.e. the per-mode strike-coupling weight is sin(k*pi*x0/L) for k=1..32, with x0/L = strikePos in [0,1]. In shell_mapper.h the result is abs()'d and floored to 0.05 (r.amplitudes[k]); in drum_voice.h the secondary shell bank uses the same function hard-coded at strikePos=0.3 with a 0.03 floor. The frequency ratios kShellRatios (1.000, 2.757, 5.404, 8.933, 13.344, 18.637, ... asymptotic (2k+1)pi/2) ARE the correct free-free Euler-Bernoulli beam ratios — I verified them numerically from beta_n*L roots {4.730, 7.853, 10.996, 14.137} as (beta_n L/beta_1 L)^2 = 1.000, 2.757, 5.404, 8.933, 13.344. So the model's frequencies are genuinely free-free, but the strike-position mode shape is the simply-supported/pinned beam shape sin(k*pi*x/L).
research.correct_form: The true free-free Euler-Bernoulli mode shape is phi_n(x) = cosh(beta_n x) + cos(beta_n x) - sigma_n*(sinh(beta_n x) + sin(beta_n x)), with sigma_n = (cosh(beta_n L) - cos(beta_n L)) / (sinh(beta_n L) - sin(beta_n L)) and beta_n L the roots of cos(beta L)cosh(beta L) = 1 ({4.730, 7.853, 10.996, ...}). sin(k*pi*x/L) is NOT an acceptable approximation for the strike-coupling of a free-free bar. I computed both normalized shapes: at the free ends (x=0 and x=L) the true free-free shape is at its MAXIMUM antinode (|phi| = 1.0) for every mode, whereas sin(k*pi*x/L) is identically 0 (a node) there — a maximal, sign-and-magnitude error exactly at the most musically common striking region (bar ends/edges). Interior node/antinode locations are also wrong: e.g. mode 1 at center is -0.61 (free-free) vs +1.0 (sin); mode 3 at center is +0.71 vs -1.0. A correct, cheap alternative that captures the dominant free-free feature is to use the real eigenfunctions (a small per-mode lookup of beta_n L and sigma_n, evaluated with the cosh/cos/sinh/sin combination), or at minimum a |cos(k*pi*x/L)|-family shape that has antinodes (not nodes) at the ends.

sources.title: Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments (2nd ed., 1998), Ch.2 Sec. 2.14-2.20 Transverse Vibrations of a Free-Free Bar
sources.says: Free-free bar eigenfunctions are linear combinations of sin, cos, sinh, cosh; frequencies satisfy cos(beta L)cosh(beta L)=1 giving inharmonic ratios 1, 2.756, 5.404, 8.933, ...; the free ends are antinodes of displacement (maximum motion), unlike a simply-supported bar whose ends are nodes.

sources.title: Rossing & Fletcher, Principles of Vibration and Sound (2nd ed., 2004), Sec. 2.19 Free Bars
sources.says: Mode shapes of the free-free bar have antinodes at both free ends and the inharmonic partial series 1:2.76:5.40:8.93; node positions move inward from the ends and differ entirely from the pinned-bar sin(n pi x/L) shapes.

sources.title: Numerical verification (this audit): beta_n L roots {4.730,7.853,10.996,14.137} give frequency ratios {1.000,2.757,5.404,8.933,13.344} and shape evaluation phi_n(x) vs sin(n pi x/L)
sources.says: Confirms the code's kShellRatios are the correct free-free ratios, and shows sin(k pi x/L) gives 0 at the ends where the real free-free shape peaks at 1.0 (max possible coupling error), with wrong interior node/antinode signs for modes 1 and 3.

research.implication: Audible and plausibly a contributor to the boring/same-sounding symptom, though secondary to gain-staging/limiter issues. Two concrete consequences: (1) The strike-position map is wrong everywhere, but it INVERTS at the bar ends: a real free-free bar struck near an edge (strikePos near 0 or 1) excites all modes maximally (bright, full-spectrum attack), whereas sin(k*pi*x) drives every mode toward 0 — so with the abs()+0.05 floor in shell_mapper.h (and 0.03 in the secondary shell), at extreme strikePos the entire mode set collapses to a flat near-uniform floor, producing the SAME dull spectrum regardless of other parameters. That is a direct dynamics/timbre-homogenizing mechanism tied to the reported symptom. (2) The drum_voice.h secondary shell hard-codes strikePos=0.3, so the head<->shell coupling timbre never responds to the strike-position parameter at all and is identical across presets — another sameness source. The code comments already acknowledge this is a deliberate character approximation, so it is documented, not a hidden bug; but the chosen shape is the qualitatively wrong one (nodes vs antinodes at the ends) and does measurably flatten the parameter range it is supposed to expose.

research.claim_as_coded: computeBellAmplitude(modeIdx, strikePos) returns std::abs(std::cos(k * kPi * r)) where k = (modeIdx+1) and r = strikePos (treated as r/R with R=1, range [0,1]). bell_modes.h:62-64. This is a single 1-D radial standing wave: mode index -> (k+1) radial half-waves of a cosine. BellMapper::map then floors the result at max(a, 0.05) (bell_mapper.h:45-47) and feeds it as the per-mode amplitude into ModalResonatorBank over the 16 Chladni partial RATIOS {0.250,0.500,0.600,0.750,1.000,1.500,2.000,2.600,3.200,4.000,5.333,6.400,7.333,8.667,10.000,12.000}. The header itself (bell_modes.h:50-55) admits it is a deliberate approximation 'without a full Chladni eigenfunction library.'
research.correct_form: Real church-bell (axial-shell) modes are 2-D, not a 1-D radial standing wave. In the Rossing-Perrin classification each partial is indexed by a pair (m,n): m = number of nodal meridians (vertical nodal lines over the crown; the bell has 2m of them, i.e. m diameters) and n = number of nodal circles (horizontal). The five tuned partials are NOT successive radial half-waves of one cosine -- e.g. hum = (2,0)/group-0, prime = (2,1#), tierce = (3,1), quint = (3,1#)/(4,1), nominal = (4,1). The frequency ordering used by the code (the kBellRatios table) is therefore decoupled from any single radial index k+1, so binding amplitude to (k+1) half-waves of cos has no physical correspondence to the mode it multiplies. A defensible physical amplitude needs the meridional dependence cos(m*theta) AND the through-thickness/meridional mode shape phi_n along the bell profile, evaluated at the strike location. The near-lip vs near-crown tonal difference is governed by where on the MERIDIAN (axially, crown->soundbow->lip) the strike lands relative to each mode's nodal circles -- which the 1-D r/R cosine does crudely capture in spirit (higher-n modes have more nodal circles, so high partials get re-weighted as the strike moves axially), but with the wrong index mapping and wrong nodal structure. Two further physical issues: (1) a real bell is struck by the clapper at the soundbow, a FIXED near-rim location (r ~ R), so sweeping r over the full [0,1] is unphysical as a 'strike position'; (2) the max(a,0.05) floor and feeding into a modal bank further blunt whatever weighting remains. Conclusion: the simple radial cosine is a heuristic that produces SOME spectral variation with the strike scalar, but it is not the physically correct (m,n) Chladni eigenfunction and does not faithfully reproduce the documented near-lip/near-crown behaviour.

sources.title: Rossing, T.D. (2000) Science of Percussion Instruments, Ch.5 'Bells'
sources.says: Bell partials are classified by nodal meridians (m) and nodal circles (n); the five tuned partials (hum, prime, tierce, quint, nominal) correspond to specific (m,n) 2-D mode shapes, not successive radial half-waves of a single standing wave.

sources.title: Fletcher & Rossing, The Physics of Musical Instruments, Ch.21 'Bells'
sources.says: Church-bell modes are axial-shell vibrations indexed by circumferential (meridian) and meridional/axial (circle) node counts; mode shapes are 2-D and the frequency ordering of partials does not follow a single monotonic radial index.

sources.title: Perrin, Charnley & DePont, 'Normal modes of the modern English church bell', J. Sound Vib. (1983)
sources.says: FE + experimental modal analysis establishing the (m,n) two-index group classification of bell normal modes and their nodal-meridian/nodal-circle structure.

sources.title: Perrin & Charnley, 'Chladni's law and the modern English church bell', J. Sound Vib. (1985)
sources.says: Bell partial frequencies fit a modified 2-D Chladni law f ~ C(m + b n)^p in the two indices (m,n); a single 1-D radial index cannot reproduce the partial set.

sources.title: Hibberts, 'Partial Frequencies and Chladni's Law in Church Bells', Open Journal of Acoustics (2014)
sources.says: Provides the big-bell Chladni-law parameters (the source of the code's ratio table) explicitly as a function of the two indices m and n, confirming partials are two-index, not single-radial-index.

research.implication: Audible consequence: because the amplitude weighting is bound to a physically wrong index (k+1 radial half-waves of cos vs the true (m,n) nodal structure), moving the Strike Position knob redistributes partial energy in a way that does not correspond to real near-lip vs near-crown timbre change. Combined with the max(a,0.05) floor (every mode always gets at least 5% amplitude) and the downstream 1/sqrt(N) normalize + per-voice softClip + master energy limiter, the strike-position control has weak and non-characteristic timbral authority. This is a plausible CONTRIBUTOR to the 'presets sound the same and boring' symptom for bell-body presets specifically: the parameter that should most strongly differentiate bell timbres (where it's struck, which excites different (m,n) groups) is flattened both by the unphysical model and by the 0.05 amplitude floor, so different strike-position preset values converge toward a similar all-partials-present spectrum. It is not the dominant cause across all bodies, but it does homogenize the bell body's response to strike position.

research.claim_as_coded: mode_inject.h injects 8 integer harmonics (k=1..8) with per-partial amplitude = 1.0f / (harmonicIndex * harmonicIndex), i.e. 1/k^2 (a -12 dB/octave rolloff). The source comment (lines 46-48) describes this as "Amplitudes are 1/k^2 (close to natural harmonic falloff)". relativeFrequency = harmonicIndex (pure integer ratios 1..8), inharmonicDeviation = 0.
research.correct_form: 1/k^2 is the TRIANGLE-wave spectrum (-12 dB/octave), not the generic "natural harmonic falloff." The canonical natural broadband / bowed-string (slip-stick, sawtooth-like) spectrum is 1/k (-6 dB/octave). A plucked string's smooth spectral ENVELOPE does follow 1/k^2 (Fletcher & Rossing: mode amplitude proportional to (1/n^2)*sin(n*pi*p/L)), but with comb notches from the pluck point, not the flat falloff coded here; a struck/piano string (idealized hammer impulse on string velocity) rolls off even steeper (toward 1/k^3). So 1/k^2 is accurate only for triangle waves and the plucked-string envelope, and is WRONG for the sawtooth/bowed/"bright natural tone" class most people mean by "natural harmonic falloff." A more defensible default for a broadly "natural" injected harmonic series would be 1/k (-6 dB/oct), or a tunable rolloff exponent.

sources.title: The Physics of Musical Instruments (Fletcher & Rossing, 2nd ed.)
sources.says: String mode amplitudes for an ideal plucked string follow a 1/n^2 envelope modulated by sin(n*pi*p/L) (pluck-point comb); bowed strings produce a sawtooth (Helmholtz) motion with 1/n harmonic amplitudes; struck (piano) strings have a distinct, generally steeper high-harmonic rolloff.

sources.title: ThinkDSP, Ch.2 Harmonics (Allen Downey)
sources.says: Square and sawtooth harmonics drop off in proportion to 1/f (-6 dB/oct); triangle-wave harmonics drop off like 1/f^2 (-12 dB/oct).

sources.title: Triangle wave - Wikipedia
sources.says: Triangle wave nth-harmonic amplitude is 1/n^2 (odd harmonics only); higher harmonics roll off as the inverse square of harmonic number = -12 dB/octave.

sources.title: Sawtooth wave - Wikipedia / Zoe Blade notebook
sources.says: Sawtooth harmonic amplitudes decay as 1/n (fundamental 1, 2nd 1/2, 3rd 1/3, ...) = -6 dB/octave; used to model bowed-string slip-stick motion.

sources.title: WolfSound: Basic Waveforms in Synthesis
sources.says: Corroborates that sawtooth/square = 1/n (-6 dB/oct) and triangle = 1/n^2 (-12 dB/oct).

research.implication: Audible and directly relevant to the "boring/same-sounding" symptom. With 1/k^2 the 8th injected partial is 36 dB below the fundamental (1/64); with the more typical natural 1/k it would be only 18 dB down (1/8). The injected series is therefore dominated by the fundamental and 2nd harmonic, contributing almost no bright/upper-harmonic character. Because the upper partials are so quiet, sweeping the ModeInject amount knob mostly adds a dull, dark low-harmonic tone rather than distinctive timbre, so different unnatural-zone settings converge toward a similar muffled result. The mislabel ("close to natural harmonic falloff") also risks the value being copied/trusted elsewhere as if it were the generic natural default when it is specifically the triangle/plucked-envelope case.

research.claim_as_coded: fm_impulse_exciter.h implements a 2-operator Chowning FM with carrier ratio fixed at 1.0 and modulator ratio defaulting to 1.4 (modulatorRatioNorm_=(1.4-1.0)/3.0, mapped norm[0,1]->[1.0,4.0] so default reproduces 1:1.4). Base freq = 400*pow(6.25,velocity) (400->2500 Hz). Modulation index = lerp(0.5,3.0,velocity). Two SEPARATE exponential decay envelopes: amp tau=80 ms (kAmpTauSec=0.080), modIndex tau=30 ms (kModIndexTauSec=0.030), each coeff = exp(-6.9078/(tau*sr)) reaching ~1e-3 at tau. modIndexEnv therefore decays FASTER than ampEnv. modulator output (radians) phase-modulates carrier; voice goes idle when ampEnv<0.001.", "code_ref">plugins/membrum/src/dsp/exciters/fm_impulse_exciter.h:48-51,77-98,123-132
research.correct_form: RATIO: CONFIRMED. Chowning (1973) 'Synthesis of Complex Audio Spectra by Means of FM' uses for its bell example carrier=200 Hz, modulator=280 Hz, i.e. c:m = 1:1.4 exactly — an inharmonic (non-integer) ratio is indeed the canonical choice for bell/struck-metal spectra, and the code's default 1.4 is faithful. INDEX-ENVELOPE CLAIM: mis-attributed. In Chowning's actual bell patch the modulation-index envelope and the amplitude envelope are the SAME exponential shape (index directly proportional to amplitude; both decay together, index from ~10 to 0 over the note). Chowning did NOT specify a faster-decaying index envelope. A faster index decay (30 ms vs 80 ms here) is a *defensible acoustic refinement* — struck metal's higher inharmonic partials do die off faster than the fundamental, and many bell/percussive FM patches use a faster index envelope for exactly this 'bright-attack, simpler-tail' evolution — but it is a post-Chowning practice, not the canonical 1973 bell. Also note the code's index range (0.5-3.0) is far below Chowning's bell index of ~10, so the spectrum is much less inharmonically rich than a classic FM bell regardless of envelope.

sources.title: Chowning, J. (1973), The Synthesis of Complex Audio Spectra by Means of Frequency Modulation (JAES 21(7)) — bell example parameters
sources.says: Bell timbre uses carrier 200 Hz / modulator 280 Hz (c:m = 1:1.4, inharmonic) with modulation index ~10 and an exponentially decaying envelope; the index envelope is proportional to / the same shape as the amplitude envelope (both decay together), not a separate faster-decaying index.

sources.title: FM Synthesis (Øland & Dannenberg, CMU Introduction to Computer Music readings)
sources.says: Inharmonic/percussive FM bell timbres are produced by non-integer c:m ratios with exponentially decaying envelopes so the spectrum evolves from complex to simple over the note; high modulation index gives the rich inharmonic bell spectrum.

sources.title: WolfSound — FM Synthesis Explained For Audio Programmers
sources.says: A constant c:m ratio preserves partial structure; irrational/non-integer ratios (e.g. sqrt(2):1) yield metallic, inharmonic spectra. Corroborates that non-integer ratios are the mechanism for bell/metal timbres but does not single out 1.4 or prescribe separate index/amp envelopes.

research.implication: Audibly minor and NOT a likely cause of the 'presets sound the same/boring' symptom. The 1:1.4 ratio is correct, so the FM exciter's basic bell character is right. The faster index-envelope decay (30 ms vs 80 ms amp) makes attacks brighter and tails purer — that ADDS timbral variation, it does not flatten it. The two real deviations from a classic Chowning bell are (a) low modulation index (max 3.0 vs Chowning's ~10), which makes the spectrum noticeably less rich/inharmonic — bells will sound thinner and more sine-like than a true FM bell, and (b) the index range being velocity-mapped 0.5-3.0 narrows dynamic timbral change. But neither homogenizes presets: per-preset 'sameness' is far more likely downstream (1/sqrt(N) normalize + gain comp, per-voice softClip on shaped*env*level, and the master energy limiter), not in this exciter. If anything the FM exciter is too sine-like/quiet to assert itself in the mix, which could contribute to a generic body-dominated timbre, but that is a secondary effect.

research.claim_as_coded:
The waveguide loop loss filter is implemented exactly as claimed. In WaveguideString::process (waveguide_string.h:196-198): `float lossOutput = rho * ((1.0f - S) * x + S * lossState_); lossState_ = x;` which realizes H(z) = rho*[(1-S) + S*z^-1]. S is derived from brightness in [0,1] mapped to [0,0.5] via `float S = smoothedBrightness * 0.5f;` (line 168, also line 303). The pick/strike position is implemented as a feed-forward comb H(z) = 1 - z^-M applied circularly to the excitation noise burst (waveguide_string.h:415-432), with M = round(pickPosition * bridgeDelaySamples) (line 421-422). StringMapper (string_mapper.h:40-61) maps Size->f0 = 800*0.1^size, forwards strikePos->pickPosition clamped to [0.02,0.98], and inverts material into brightness (brightness = 1 - material, line 53) precisely because higher S = darker tail, so that high Material = bright/even tail.

VERIFICATION OF S->darker: |H(e^jw)|/rho = sqrt((1-S)^2 + 2S(1-S)cos w + S^2). At Nyquist (w=pi): |H| = rho*|1-2S|. With S in [0,0.5]: S=0 gives flat gain rho (no extra HF loss); S=0.5 gives a zero at Nyquist (max HF damping). So increasing S monotonically increases high-frequency per-round-trip loss => darker tail. CORRECT.

VERIFICATION OF PICK COMB: H(z)=1-z^-M has zeros at f = m*fs/M. The string fundamental is f0 = fs/N where N is the loop period (bridgeDelaySamples ~ N). Harmonic k (at k*fs/N) is nulled when k = m*N/M = m/beta (beta = M/N = pickPosition). So harmonics 1/beta, 2/beta, ... are suppressed -- exactly the harmonics that have a vibrational node at the normalized pluck point beta. CORRECT and matches Jaffe & Smith.
research.correct_form: No correction needed. The coded forms match the canonical literature: (1) Jaffe-Smith generalized one-zero loop filter H_loss(z) = g*[(1-S) + S*z^-1] (their Eq. for the "loss/damping" filter; the Karplus-Strong original two-point averager (1+z^-1)/2 is the S=0.5 special case). The [0,0.5] range for S keeps the zero inside/on the unit circle (minimum-phase, stable, monotonically increasing HF loss). (2) Jaffe-Smith pick-position filter H_pick(z) = 1 - z^-(beta*N), nulling harmonics with a node at the normalized pick position beta. Both are reproduced faithfully.
