Minor stylistic notes (not errors): the standalone comment at string_mapper.h:53 says material=1 -> brightness_=0 -> "all harmonics survive equally" -- with S=0 the loop filter is pure real gain rho, so it is frequency-flat (correct); rho itself (computeRho = 10^(-3/(T60*f0))) sets overall decay, consistent with Jaffe-Smith's separation of overall loss (g/rho) from frequency-dependent loss (S).

sources.title: Jaffe & Smith, 'Extensions of the Karplus-Strong Plucked-String Algorithm', Computer Music Journal 7(2), 1983
sources.says: Defines the generalized one-zero loop filter for decay shortening/stretching and the pick-position comb filter 1 - z^-(beta*N) that attenuates harmonics with a node at the pluck point. The KS two-point average (1+z^-1)/2 is the symmetric special case.

sources.title: Karplus & Strong, 'Digital Synthesis of Plucked-String and Drum Timbres', Computer Music Journal 7(2), 1983
sources.says: Original algorithm uses the two-point averaging lowpass y[n]=0.5*(y[n-N]+y[n-N-1]) as the loop filter; averaging causes faster high-frequency decay (darker tail), the limiting behavior the (1-S)+S*z^-1 generalization controls.

sources.title: Julius O. Smith III, 'Physical Audio Signal Processing' (CCRMA, online book) -- Digital Waveguide / Karplus-Strong chapters
sources.says: Standard digital-waveguide string uses a one-zero (lowpass) loop filter whose increasing HF attenuation darkens the decay, plus a comb/pluck-position model; loop filter must be minimum-phase with |H(e^jw)|<=1 for stability, satisfied by S in [0,0.5] with real gain rho<=1.

research.implication: Not a bug -- the string body's loop filter and pick comb are scientifically correct and DO respond audibly to Material (brightness/HF decay) and Strike Position (comb nulls). So this stage is NOT a contributor to the 'presets sound the same/boring' symptom; if anything it is one of the few stages with genuine per-parameter timbral variation. CAVEAT for the broader sameness investigation: this correctness only matters when the WaveguideString body is actually selected and when downstream stages (per-voice softClip on shaped*env*level, 1/sqrt(N) normalize + gain compensation, master energy limiter) do not re-flatten the dynamics this stage produces. The string body itself preserves the timbral spread; look elsewhere (output normalize/limiter/clip) for the homogenization.

research.claim_as_coded: Per-mode inharmonic "scatter" multiplies each modal frequency by (1 + C*sin(k * kScatterD)) where kScatterD = pi*(phi-1) (≈1.9416 rad) and C = scatter*0.10. So mode k's frequency is warped by f_w *= 1 + C*sin(k * pi*(phi-1)). It is a SINE of (k * pi*(phi-1)), not the fractional part frac(k*phi) used by canonical golden-ratio low-discrepancy sequences. The constant phi-1 = 1/phi ≈ 0.618 (continued fraction [0;1,1,1,...]).
research.correct_form: The literature premise is correct: the golden ratio (phi-1 = 1/phi, continued fraction all-1s, the "most irrational" number) gives the lowest-discrepancy Kronecker/Weyl additive-recurrence sequence frac(k*alpha). BUT the canonical low-discrepancy construction uses the FRACTIONAL PART frac(k*(phi-1)), not sin(k*pi*(phi-1)). Two deviations weaken the claim: (1) The sine's 2*pi period means the effective per-mode fractional turn is (phi-1)/2 ≈ 0.309, whose continued fraction is [0;3,4,4,4,...] — NOT all-1s, so it is no longer the "most irrational." Measured three-gap ratio for k=0..31 degrades from the golden-optimal 2.618 (=phi^2) to 3.236. The sequence is still irrational and Weyl-equidistributed (so "aperiodic / non-repeating" holds), but it is NOT "maximally" uniform / minimal-discrepancy. (2) Passing an equidistributed phase through sin() makes the actual frequency DISPLACEMENTS follow an arcsine distribution: values cluster at the ±C extremes rather than spreading uniformly (histogram of sin(k*kScatterD) for k=0..31 puts 7-8 modes in the two outer bins near ±1 and leaves middle bins near-empty). To realize the claimed "maximally uniform/irrational mode detuning spread," use the displacement directly from the low-discrepancy value, e.g. C*(2*frac(k*(phi-1)) - 1) (uniform in [-C,C]) or apply the golden increment to the fractional turn without the sine, rather than C*sin(k*pi*(phi-1)).

sources.title: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Quasirandom Sequences (Roberts)
sources.says: Defines the additive-recurrence (Weyl) R-sequence t_n = frac(s0 + n*alpha) with alpha irrational; the golden ratio gives empirically the lowest L-infinity star discrepancy known — establishing the canonical construction is the FRACTIONAL PART of n*alpha, not a sine of it.

sources.title: Golden Ratio Sequences for Low-Discrepancy Sampling (J. Graphics Tools, Wong/Niederreiter-style)
sources.says: Constructs and analyses golden-ratio Kronecker sequences for low-discrepancy sampling, comparing against random/Hammersley/blue-noise distributions; the golden ratio is the optimal irrational multiplier for the additive recurrence.

sources.title: Low-discrepancy sequence — Wikipedia (Kronecker/Weyl sequences, Weyl's criterion, three-gap theorem)
sources.says: Kronecker sequence is frac(n*alpha); equidistribution follows from Weyl's criterion for irrational alpha; the golden ratio's all-1s continued fraction makes it the slowest-to-approximate (most irrational), minimizing discrepancy. The three-gap theorem gives the gap structure (golden ratio -> gap ratio phi^2 = 2.618).

sources.title: Biophysical optimality of the golden angle in phyllotaxis — Scientific Reports
sources.says: Among constant-angle insertion rules, the golden angle (fractional turn 1/phi^2, continued fraction all-1s) yields the most even, non-overlapping distribution — confirming 'most irrational' = most uniform, but the property is tied to the specific irrational value, which the code's (phi-1)/2 effective increment does not preserve.

research.implication: Audibly minor and NOT a primary cause of the boring/same-sounding presets. Consequences of the two issues: (1) The scatter detunings are slightly less evenly spread than an ideal golden sequence (gap ratio 3.24 vs 2.62), so for small mode counts a couple of modes sit closer together than optimal — a subtle loss of inharmonic "shimmer" uniformity, not an audible defect. (2) More relevant: because sin() makes displacements cluster at ±10% extremes (arcsine), the scatter knob biases modes toward the maximum detune rather than a graded spread, so the perceptual change per knob step is non-linear and front-loaded. Still, scatter IS reaching the DSP and DOES warp frequencies (C up to 0.10 = ±10%, widened in Phase 8C), so it produces audible timbral change. This stage does not flatten dynamics or collapse parameter range — it is a correctness/quality nuance, not the sameness culprit. The dynamics-homogenizing suspects (per-voice softClip, 1/sqrt(N) normalize + gain comp, master energy limiter) are elsewhere in the path.
